topics.title: Elementary Signals: Classification and Representation of Continuous Time and Discrete Time Signals, Signal Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET503 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elementary Signals: Classification and Representation of Continuous Time and Discrete Time Signals, Signal Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Signal?

A **signal** is a mathematical description of a physical phenomenon as a function of one or more independent variables. The independent variable is most commonly **time**, but it can also be space, frequency, or any other physical parameter. The dependent variable carries the information (amplitude, intensity, voltage, pressure, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Formal Definition (Module 1):**
> A signal $x(t)$ is a function that maps an independent variable $t \in \mathbb{R}$ (continuous time) or $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ (discrete time) to a real or complex value that represents some physical quantity.

### Real-World Analogy — The "Heart-Beat to Number Line" Picture

Imagine an ECG machine strapped to a patient in a hospital:

1. The **continuous, ink-tracing** that scrolls out is a **Continuous-Time (CT) signal** — for *every* instant, there is a voltage value.
2. Now suppose a digital smartwatch samples that same ECG once every **1 ms** and stores the values. The stored sequence of numbers is a **Discrete-Time (DT) signal** — defined only at integer multiples of the sampling instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The physical phenomenon is the same; only the *mathematical representation* (and the engineering flexibility that comes with it) changes. This duality is the gateway to the entire subject of Signals and Systems.

---

## 1.2 Two Universes: Continuous-Time (CT) vs. Discrete-Time (DT)

| Property | Continuous-Time Signal $x(t)$ | Discrete-Time Signal $x[n]$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Independent variable | $t \in \mathbb{R}$ (real continuum) | $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ (integers) |
| Defined at | Every real instant | Only integer instants |
| Graphical form | Smooth curve | Stem (lollipop) plot |
| Typical origin | Analog sensors (microphone, ECG) | Digital processors, sampled data |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU notation, square brackets $x[n]$ **always** denote a discrete-time signal, while parentheses $x(t)$ denote continuous-time. Never mix the two.

---

## 1.3 Elementary Signals — The "Alphabet" of Signal Processing

Elementary signals are the **building blocks** from which almost every complex signal encountered in real engineering can be constructed. The KTU Module-1 syllabus emphasises six canonical elementary signals:

1. **Unit Impulse** $\delta(t)$ / $\delta[n]$
2. **Unit Step** $u(t)$ / $u[n]$
3. **Unit Ramp** $r(t)$ / $r[n]$
4. **Exponential** $e^{at}$ / $e^{an}$
5. **Sinusoidal** $\sin(\omega t)$ / $\sin[\Omega n]$
6. **Signum** $\text{sgn}(t)$ / $\text{sgn}[n]$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The *Sinc* function $\text{sinc}(t) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi t)}{\pi t}$ is the bridge between the time and frequency domains and is also commonly tested. Memorise its shape and zero-crossings.

### Intuitive Picture of Each Elementary Signal

* **Unit Step $u(t)$** — "A switch that turns ON at $t=0$ and stays at amplitude 1 forever." It models a battery being connected to a circuit at a precise instant.
* **Unit Impulse $\delta(t)$** — "An infinitely tall, infinitely thin spike whose total area is exactly 1." It is the mathematical model of an ideal hammer-strike, a lightning bolt, or a single sample of a digital camera sensor.
* **Unit Ramp $r(t)$** — "A steadily increasing quantity that begins at $t=0$." Models water filling a tank at unit rate.
* **Exponential $e^{at}$** — "A quantity that grows (or decays) by a constant ratio per unit time." Models radioactive decay, capacitor discharge, and population dynamics.
* **Sinusoidal Signal** — "An oscillation that repeats itself." Models AC mains supply, pure musical tones.
* **Signum Function** — "A switch that flips polarity at the origin." Models phase reversals in commutation circuits.

---

## 1.4 Signal Classification — The KTU Taxonomy

A single signal can belong to **multiple** categories simultaneously. The classification tree used in KTU Module 1 is:

* **By independent variable** → CT / DT
* **By amplitude continuity** → Analog / Discrete-amplitude / Digital
* **By determinism** → Deterministic / Random
* **By repetition** → Periodic / Aperiodic
* **By symmetry** → Even / Odd / Neither
* **By energy/power** → Energy signal / Power signal / Neither

Each of these classifications is unpacked rigorously in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Definitions of the Six Elementary Signals

### 2.1.1 Continuous-Time (CT) Elementary Signals

**Unit Step Function $u(t)$**

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Unit Impulse Function $\delta(t)$** (Dirac Delta)

$$\delta(t) = \begin{cases} \infty, & t = 0 \\ 0, & t \neq 0 \end{cases} \quad \text{and} \quad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t)\,dt = 1$$

**Unit Ramp Function $r(t)$**

$$r(t) = \begin{cases} t, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases} = t\,u(t)$$

**Exponential Signal**

$$x(t) = e^{at}, \quad a \in \mathbb{R}$$

* If $a > 0$ → growing exponential
* If $a < 0$ → decaying exponential
* If $a = 0$ → constant signal equal to 1

**Sinusoidal Signal**

$$x(t) = A\sin(\omega t + \phi) = A\cos(\omega t + \phi - \tfrac{\pi}{2})$$

* $A$ = amplitude, $\omega$ = angular frequency (rad/s), $\phi$ = phase in radians

**Signum Function $\text{sgn}(t)$**

$$\text{sgn}(t) = \begin{cases} +1, & t > 0 \\ 0, & t = 0 \\ -1, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

### 2.1.2 Discrete-Time (DT) Elementary Signals

**Unit Step $u[n]$**

$$u[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Unit Impulse $\delta[n]$**

$$\delta[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Unit Ramp $r[n]$**

$$r[n] = \begin{cases} n, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases} = n\,u[n]$$

**Exponential Signal**

$$x[n] = a^{n}, \quad a \in \mathbb{R}$$

* If $\vert a \vert > 1$ → growing
* If $\vert a \vert < 1$ → decaying
* If $a = -1$ → oscillating between $\pm 1$

**Sinusoidal DT Signal**

$$x[n] = A\sin(\Omega_0 n + \phi)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **DT Pitfall:** Unlike CT, a discrete-time sinusoid is periodic **only if** $\Omega_0 / 2\pi$ is a **rational number**. For example, $\sin(\pi n)$ is periodic with period $N=2$, but $\sin(n)$ is **not periodic** at all.

**Signum DT**

$$\text{sgn}[n] = \begin{cases} +1, & n > 0 \\ 0, & n = 0 \\ -1, & n < 0 \end{cases}$$

---

## 2.2 Properties (KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **mandatory revision sheet** for the KTU End Semester Examination. Every property below has appeared in past paper variants.

| # | Property | Mathematical Statement | KTU Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| P1 | Step from Impulse (CT) | $u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau)\,d\tau$ | Deriving rectangular pulse |
| P2 | Impulse from Step (CT) | $\delta(t) = \dfrac{d\,u(t)}{dt}$ | Distribution theory |
| P3 | Sifting Property (CT) | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,\delta(t-t_0)\,dt = x(t_0)$ | Sampling by impulse train |
| P4 | Sifting Property (DT) | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,\delta[n-n_0] = x[n_0]$ | Extracting a single sample |
| P5 | Ramp from Step (CT) | $r(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau)\,d\tau = t\,u(t)$ | Integration problems |
| P6 | DT Difference of Step | $u[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \delta[k]$ | Building $u[n]$ from $\delta[n]$ |
| P7 | First Difference (DT) | $\Delta x[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ | Discrete-time derivative |
| P8 | Running Sum (DT) | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ | Discrete-time integration |
| P9 | Signum–Step Relation | $\text{sgn}(t) = 2u(t) - 1$ | Converting between forms |
| P10 | Step–Signum Relation | $u(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big(\text{sgn}(t) + 1\big)$ | Working with odd signals |

---

## 2.3 Detailed Classification Criteria

### 2.3.1 Periodic vs. Aperiodic

A signal $x(t)$ is **periodic** with fundamental period $T_0$ if

$$x(t + T_0) = x(t) \quad \forall\,t \in \mathbb{R}$$

and $T_0$ is the **smallest positive** value satisfying this. If no such $T_0$ exists, the signal is **aperiodic**.

**Sum of two periodic signals** with periods $T_1$ and $T_2$ is periodic **iff** $T_1/T_2$ is rational, with combined period

$$T_0 = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2) = \frac{T_1 T_2}{\gcd(T_1, T_2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ratio $T_1/T_2$ must be a rational number. If $T_1 = \pi$ and $T_2 = 1$, the sum is **not periodic** — a classic KTU trick question.

### 2.3.2 Even and Odd Decomposition

Any real signal $x(t)$ can be uniquely expressed as the sum of an even and an odd component:

$$x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$$

where

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}, \qquad x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** $x_e(-t) = x_e(t)$ (even) and $x_o(-t) = -x_o(t)$ (odd). The same identities hold for $x[n]$ in the discrete domain.

### 2.3.3 Energy and Power Signals

For a CT signal $x(t)$, total energy:

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \,dt$$

Average power:

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \,dt$$

For DT signals:

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2, \qquad P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$$

| Signal Class | Energy | Power | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Energy signal (non-zero, finite) | $0 < E < \infty$ | $P = 0$ | Decaying exponential $e^{-t}u(t)$ |
| Power signal (non-zero, finite) | $E = \infty$ | $0 < P < \infty$ | $\cos(\omega t)$, $u(t)$ |
| Neither (e.g. growing exponentials) | $E = \infty$ | $P = \infty$ | $e^{t}u(t)$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mutually exclusive trap:** A signal **cannot** be both an energy signal and a power signal. If $0 < E < \infty$ then $P = 0$, and vice versa.

### 2.3.4 Deterministic vs. Random

* **Deterministic** — completely described by an explicit mathematical function (e.g. $x(t) = 5\sin(2\pi t)$).
* **Random** — described only in probabilistic terms (e.g. thermal noise in a resistor). KTU Module 1 only deals with deterministic signals; random signals are deferred to Module 5 (Stochastic Processes).

### 2.3.5 Analog / Discrete-Amplitude / Digital

* **Analog** — continuous in time **and** continuous in amplitude.
* **Discrete-amplitude** — continuous in time but amplitude is restricted to discrete levels.
* **Digital** — discrete in time **and** discrete in amplitude.

> [!NOTE]
> Every digital signal is a discrete-time signal, but the reverse is not always true.

---

## 2.4 Signal Operations — Transformations on the Independent Variable

Let $x(t)$ be a given CT signal. The five fundamental transformations are:

| Operation | Transformed Signal | Verbal Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Time shift | $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$ | Shift right by $t_0$ (delay) |
| Time reversal | $y(t) = x(-t)$ | Reflect about the vertical axis |
| Time scaling | $y(t) = x(at)$ | Compress ($a>1$) / Expand ($0<a<1$) |
| Amplitude scaling | $y(t) = A\,x(t)$ | Multiply amplitude by $A$ |
| Amplitude shift | $y(t) = x(t) + C$ | Add constant offset $C$ |

For DT signals, replace $t$ with $n$ throughout.

### The Combined Operation Cascade

When a signal undergoes **multiple** operations in succession, the order is critical. The general cascade for a CT signal is:

$$y(t) = x(at - b)$$

Step-by-step recipe:
1. **Replace $t$ by $at$** → horizontal scaling by factor $1/a$.
2. **Replace $t$ by $t - b/a$** → horizontal shift by $b/a$ (right if $b/a>0$).

> [!WARNING]
> The shift is **always $b/a$**, never $b$. A classic KTU valuation trap.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Real-World Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Unit step $u(t)$ | Switching circuits, sudden load changes in power systems |
| Impulse $\delta(t)$ | Lightning strike modelling, spark discharges, sampling |
| Exponential decay $e^{-t/RC}$ | RC circuit transient response |
| Sinusoidal signal | AC power analysis, audio engineering, OFDM communication |
| Time shift | Radar/sonar echo, network delay |
| Time scaling | Doppler compression/expansion of received signals |
| Energy/Power classification | Antenna design, link-budget analysis in communication |
| Even/odd decomposition | Fourier cosine/sine series analysis |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides rigorous, line-by-line derivations of the most important relations in Module 1. Each step is explicitly justified, and a textual reasoning row accompanies every algebraic transition.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Unit Step from Unit Impulse in CT Domain

**Statement to prove:** $\displaystyle u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau)\,d\tau$

**Step 1 — Set up the running integral.**
By definition, the running integral of any function $f(\tau)$ is the area under $f(\tau)$ accumulated from $-\infty$ up to a sliding upper limit $t$.

Let $u(t) = \displaystyle \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau)\,d\tau$.

**Step 2 — Analyse the integral in three regions of $t$.**

$$\text{When } t < 0: \quad \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau)\,d\tau = 0$$

*Reasoning:* The integration interval $(-\infty, t)$ ends to the *left* of zero, where $\delta(\tau) = 0$ everywhere. So the area is 0.

$$\text{When } t = 0: \quad \int_{-\infty}^{0} \delta(\tau)\,d\tau = 1$$

*Reasoning:* The interval now *just* includes the spike at $\tau = 0$. The total area under the impulse is 1, so the integral jumps from 0 to 1.

$$\text{When } t > 0: \quad \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau)\,d\tau = 1$$

*Reasoning:* The interval now fully contains the spike. Adding more of the zero region beyond the spike does not change the area.

**Step 3 — Assemble the result.**

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ 1, & t \geq 0 \end{cases}$$

This is exactly the definition of the unit step. Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Unit Impulse as the Derivative of Unit Step (CT)

**Statement to prove:** $\delta(t) = \dfrac{d}{dt}u(t)$

**Step 1 — Differentiate piecewise.**
For $t < 0$, $u(t) = 0$ → constant, so $\dfrac{du}{dt} = 0$.
For $t > 0$, $u(t) = 1$ → constant, so $\dfrac{du}{dt} = 0$.

**Step 2 — Examine the jump at $t=0$.**
The function jumps from 0 to 1 in zero time, an **infinite slope** at $t=0$.

**Step 3 — Define the impulse as the formal limit.**
Consider a rectangular pulse of width $\varepsilon$ and height $1/\varepsilon$ centred at $t=0$. As $\varepsilon \to 0$, the pulse becomes an impulse of unit area. The derivative of the step is the limit of this pulse.

**Step 4 — Conclude.**
Formally, the impulse is the distribution that, when integrated over any interval containing zero, yields 1. Hence $\delta(t) = \dfrac{du(t)}{dt}$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sifting Property of the CT Impulse

**Statement to prove:** $\displaystyle \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,\delta(t-t_0)\,dt = x(t_0)$

**Step 1 — Use the shifting property of the impulse.**
The impulse $\delta(t-t_0)$ is non-zero only at $t=t_0$. So in the integrand, $x(t)$ matters only at $t=t_0$.

**Step 2 — Pull $x(t_0)$ out as a constant.**
Since the integrand is non-zero only at a single point, $x(t)$ is effectively the constant $x(t_0)$ over the integration window.

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t_0)\,\delta(t-t_0)\,dt = x(t_0)\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t-t_0)\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the area of the impulse.**
$\displaystyle \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t-t_0)\,dt = 1$ by the defining property of the impulse.

**Step 4 — Conclude.**
$\displaystyle \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,\delta(t-t_0)\,dt = x(t_0)$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Sifting Property of the DT Impulse

**Statement to prove:** $\displaystyle \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,\delta[n-n_0] = x[n_0]$

**Step 1 — Recognise the discrete nature of $\delta[n-n_0]$.**
$\delta[n-n_0] = 1$ when $n = n_0$ and $0$ otherwise.

**Step 2 — Reduce the infinite sum.**
All terms in the sum vanish except when $n = n_0$.

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,\delta[n-n_0] = x[n_0] \cdot 1 = x[n_0]$$

**Step 3 — Conclude.**
The DT impulse *sifts out* the value $x[n_0]$ from the sequence. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Even–Odd Decomposition of an Arbitrary Signal

**Statement to prove:** $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$ with the formulas given in §2.3.2.

**Step 1 — Compute the even part candidate.**
$\displaystyle x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$.

Check symmetry: $x_e(-t) = \dfrac{x(-t) + x(t)}{2} = x_e(t)$. ✓ Even.

**Step 2 — Compute the odd part candidate.**
$\displaystyle x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$.

Check symmetry: $x_o(-t) = \dfrac{x(-t) - x(t)}{2} = -x_o(t)$. ✓ Odd.

**Step 3 — Sum the two parts.**

$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} + \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} = \frac{2x(t)}{2} = x(t)$$

**Step 4 — Conclude.**
Any real signal can be uniquely decomposed into an even part plus an odd part, and the formulas above give the unique decomposition. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Time-Shift of a Rectangular Pulse using Step Functions

**Statement:** Build a rectangular pulse of width 1 starting at $t=1$ using only step functions.

**Step 1 — Recall that a unit pulse can be written as a difference of steps.**
$p(t) = u(t) - u(t-1)$ → pulse of height 1, from $t=0$ to $t=1$.

**Step 2 — Shift the pulse to start at $t=1$.**
Replace $t$ by $t-1$:

$$p(t-1) = u(t-1) - u(t-2)$$

**Step 3 — Verify.**

$$p(t-1) = \begin{cases} 1, & 1 \leq t < 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This is the desired rectangular pulse starting at $t=1$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7: Period of Sum of Two Sinusoids

**Statement:** If $x_1(t) = \cos(2t)$ and $x_2(t) = \sin(3t)$, determine the period of $y(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$.

**Step 1 — Find individual periods.**
$T_1 = 2\pi / 2 = \pi$.
$T_2 = 2\pi / 3$.

**Step 2 — Test the ratio.**
$\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \dfrac{\pi}{2\pi/3} = \dfrac{3}{2}$, which is rational.

**Step 3 — Find the common period.**
$T_0 = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2)$. The smallest $T$ such that $T/T_1$ and $T/T_2$ are both integers.
Try $T = 2\pi$: $T/T_1 = 2\pi/\pi = 2$ (integer) ✓ and $T/T_2 = 2\pi/(2\pi/3) = 3$ (integer) ✓.

Hence $T_0 = 2\pi$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.8 Derivation 8: DT First Difference of a Ramp

**Statement:** Compute $\Delta r[n] = r[n] - r[n-1]$.

**Step 1 — Recall the ramp definition.**
$r[n] = n\,u[n]$. So $r[n] = n$ for $n \geq 0$ and $0$ for $n < 0$.

**Step 2 — Compute $r[n-1]$.**
$r[n-1] = (n-1)u[n-1] = n-1$ for $n \geq 1$ and $0$ for $n < 1$.

**Step 3 — Subtract.**

For $n \geq 1$: $\Delta r[n] = n - (n-1) = 1$.
For $n = 0$: $r[0] - r[-1] = 0 - 0 = 0$.
For $n < 0$: $0 - 0 = 0$.

**Step 4 — Conclude.**
$\Delta r[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n \geq 1 \\ 0, & n \leq 0 \end{cases} = u[n-1] = u[n] - \delta[n]$.

Hence the first difference of the DT ramp is the step shifted by one. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.9 Analytical Matrix — Energy/Power of Standard Signals

| Signal $x(t)$ | Energy $E$ | Power $P$ | Class |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $e^{-at}u(t), \; a>0$ | $\dfrac{1}{2a}$ | $0$ | Energy |
| $e^{-\vert t \vert}$ | $1$ | $0$ | Energy |
| $u(t)$ | $\infty$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}$ | Power |
| $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ | $\infty$ | $\dfrac{A^2}{2}$ | Power |
| $A\sin(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ | $\infty$ | $\dfrac{A^2}{2}$ | Power |
| $e^{at}u(t), \; a>0$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Neither |
| $t\,u(t)$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Neither |
| $\delta(t)$ | $1$ | — (impulsive power) | Energy |
| $\text{sgn}(t)$ | $\infty$ | $1$ | Power |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample calculation** for $x(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$:
>
> $$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2at}\,dt = \left[\frac{e^{-2at}}{-2a}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{2a}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample calculation** for $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t)$:
>
> $$P = \lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T}A^2\cos^2(\omega_0 t)\,dt = \frac{A^2}{2}\lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T}(1+\cos 2\omega_0 t)\,dt = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

---

## 3.10 Worked Numerical Example — Energy of a Triangular Pulse

**Problem:** Compute the energy of the triangular signal $x(t) = \Lambda(t)$ where

$$\Lambda(t) = \begin{cases} 1 - \vert t \vert, & \vert t \vert \leq 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 1 — Use the even symmetry.**
$\Lambda(t)$ is even, so the energy integral can be doubled from 0 to ∞.

**Step 2 — Set up the integral.**

$$E = \int_{-1}^{1} (1 - \vert t \vert)^2\,dt = 2\int_{0}^{1} (1-t)^2\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Expand the square and integrate.**

$$(1-t)^2 = 1 - 2t + t^2$$

$$\int_{0}^{1} (1 - 2t + t^2)\,dt = \left[t - t^2 + \frac{t^3}{3}\right]_{0}^{1} = 1 - 1 + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 4 — Final multiplication.**

$$E = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3} \text{ Joules}$$

$\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid block diagrams map the classification hierarchy, the relationships between elementary signals, and the cascading effect of signal operations. These are designed for direct rendering in any Mermaid-enabled markdown viewer.

## 4.1 Signal Classification Tree (KTU Module 1)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Signals] --> B[CT Signals x of t]
    A --> C[DT Signals x of n]
    B --> D[Periodic]
    B --> E[Aperiodic]
    B --> F[Energy Signal]
    B --> G[Power Signal]
    B --> H[Even Symmetry]
    B --> I[Odd Symmetry]
    C --> J[Periodic only if rational]
    C --> K[Energy Signal]
    C --> L[Power Signal]
    C --> M[Even Symmetry]
    C --> N[Odd Symmetry]
    D --> O[Example cos wt]
    E --> P[Example e to power minus at]
    F --> Q[0 less than E less than infinity, P equals 0]
    G --> R[E equals infinity, 0 less than P less than infinity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram emphasises that CT and DT signals follow the *same* classification criteria; only the DT periodicity condition is stricter (rational $\Omega / 2\pi$).

## 4.2 Relationship Map Between Elementary Signals (CT)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Delta of t] -- derivative --> P2[Unit Step u of t]
    P2 -- integral --> P1
    P2 -- integral --> P3[Ramp r of t equals t u of t]
    P3 -- derivative --> P2
    P1 -- integral --> P4[Ramp r of t]
    P5[Exponential e to power at] -- special case a equals 0 --> P2
    P6[Signum sgn of t] -- relation --> P2
    P2 -- relation --> P6
    P7[Sinc sin pi t over pi t] -- FT pair --> P8[Rectangular Pulse]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The double arrows between $\delta(t)$ and $u(t)$ reflect their defining derivative/integral relationship, the most-tested identity in KTU Module 1.

## 4.3 Time-Domain Operation Cascade (Generic CT Signal)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Input Signal x of t] --> O1{Time Scaling}
    O1 -- replace t by a t --> S1[x of a t]
    S1 --> O2{Time Shift}
    O2 -- replace t by t minus b over a --> S2[x of a t minus b]
    S2 --> O3{Amplitude Scale}
    O3 -- multiply by A --> S3[A x of a t minus b]
    S3 --> O4{Amplitude Shift}
    O4 -- add C --> S4[A x of a t minus b plus C]
    S4 --> OUT[Final Output y of t]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of Operations Rule:** Scaling must be performed **before** shifting. The shift magnitude is always $b/a$, never $b$. This is the most common error in KTU valuation, as flagged in Section 5.

## 4.4 Block Architecture for Discrete-Time Signal Processing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[DT Source x of n] --> B1[Time Shifter z to power minus n0]
    B1 --> B2[Time Reversal z to power minus n]
    B2 --> B3[First Difference Operator 1 minus z to power minus 1]
    B3 --> B4[Running Summation 1 over 1 minus z to power minus 1]
    B4 --> C1[Output y of n]
    D1[DT Reference Signal delta of n] --> B1
    D1 --> B3
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level view previews the **z-transform** notation, which is introduced formally in Module 3. The architecture shows how a generic DT signal can be processed by a chain of canonical operations.

## 4.5 Energy-Power Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Given Signal x of t or x of n] --> Q2{Compute E}
    Q2 -- E finite and nonzero --> R1[Energy Signal, P equals 0]
    Q2 -- E equals infinity --> Q3{Compute P}
    Q3 -- P finite and nonzero --> R2[Power Signal, E equals infinity]
    Q3 -- P equals infinity --> R3[Neither Energy nor Power]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this flowchart to **mechanically classify** any signal in your KTU exam — it removes all ambiguity in the answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section provides an examination-grade question bank with full model solutions, structured exactly as per the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a unit step function. Sketch $u(t)$, $u(t-3)$, and $u(t+2)$ on the same axis. What is the time-shifted function $u(t-t_0)$ for $t_0 > 0$?*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)
**Definition (1 mark):** The continuous-time unit step function is defined as

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Sketch (1 mark):** All three signals have amplitude 1, but transition at different points: $u(t)$ at $t=0$, $u(t-3)$ at $t=3$, $u(t+2)$ at $t=-2$.

**Time shift (1 mark):** $u(t-t_0)$ with $t_0 > 0$ represents a *delayed* step that switches ON at $t = t_0$.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the sifting property of the continuous-time unit impulse. Use it to evaluate $\displaystyle\int_{-2}^{3} (t^2 + 1)\,\delta(t-1)\,dt$.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)
**Statement (1.5 marks):**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,\delta(t-t_0)\,dt = x(t_0)$$

**Evaluation (1.5 marks):**
Since the impulse at $t_0=1$ lies inside the integration interval $[-2, 3]$, the integral reduces to evaluating the integrand at $t=1$:

$$\int_{-2}^{3} (t^2+1)\,\delta(t-1)\,dt = (1)^2 + 1 = 2$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE convention, Part B offers an **internal choice**. Two completely independent questions (A and B) are provided below. You must answer **either A or B**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Define the following elementary CT signals with neat sketches:
   (i) Unit Impulse $\delta(t)$    (ii) Unit Step $u(t)$    (iii) Unit Ramp $r(t)$    **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A continuous-time signal is defined as $x(t) = (t+1)\,[u(t+1) - u(t-2)]$. For this signal:
   (i) Sketch $x(t)$ clearly indicating all breakpoints.
   (ii) Express $x(t)$ as a sum of an even component $x_e(t)$ and an odd component $x_o(t)$.
   (iii) Determine whether $x(t)$ is an energy signal, power signal, or neither. Compute the relevant quantity.    **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Part (i) — Unit Impulse $\delta(t)$ (≈2.5 Marks)**

*Definition:*

$$\delta(t) = \begin{cases} \infty, & t = 0 \\ 0, & t \neq 0 \end{cases} \quad \text{with} \quad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t)\,dt = 1$$

*Sketch:* A single vertical arrow of unit area at $t=0$ (height → ∞, width → 0, area = 1).

*Key properties:* Sifting property, even function ($\delta(-t) = \delta(t)$), derivative of step.

**(a) Part (ii) — Unit Step $u(t)$ (≈2.5 Marks)**

*Definition:*

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

*Sketch:* A step that jumps from 0 to 1 at $t=0$. Value at $t=0$ is conventionally taken as 1.

*Key properties:* $\dfrac{du(t)}{dt} = \delta(t)$; $u(-t) = 1 - u(t)$ for $t \neq 0$.

**(a) Part (iii) — Unit Ramp $r(t)$ (≈2 Marks)**

*Definition:*

$$r(t) = t\,u(t) = \begin{cases} t, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

*Sketch:* A straight line of slope 1 starting from origin, only on the right half-plane.

*Key relations:*

$$r(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau)\,d\tau, \qquad \frac{d}{dt}r(t) = u(t)$$

---

**(b) Part (i) — Sketch of $x(t)$ (≈2 Marks)**

The signal $x(t) = (t+1)\,[u(t+1) - u(t-2)]$ is non-zero only on the interval $[-1, 2]$.

* At $t=-1$: $x(-1) = 0$
* At $t=0$: $x(0) = 1$
* At $t=2$: $x(2) = 3$
* The signal is a straight line of slope 1, passing through $(-1, 0)$, $(0, 1)$, $(2, 3)$, and zero outside $[-1, 2]$.

**(b) Part (ii) — Even-Odd Decomposition (≈3 Marks)**

First compute $x(-t)$:
$x(-t) = (-t+1)\,[u(-t+1) - u(-t-2)] = (1-t)\,[u(1-t) - u(-(t+2))]$
$= (1-t)\,[u(1-t) - u(-(t+2))]$

Since $u(1-t) = u(t-1)$ flipped, and the bracket is non-zero when $1-t \geq 0$ AND $-(t+2) < 0$, i.e., $t \leq 1$ AND $t > -2$. So $x(-t) = (1-t)$ for $-2 < t \leq 1$, zero elsewhere.

Now compute:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$$

Both components on their respective intervals:

$$x_e(t) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{(t+1) + 0}{2} = \dfrac{t+1}{2}, & -1 \leq t < -0.5 \\ \dfrac{(t+1) + (1-t)}{2} = 1, & -0.5 \leq t \leq 1 \\ \dfrac{0 + (1-t)}{2} = \dfrac{1-t}{2}, & 1 < t \leq 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

And $x_o(t) = x(t) - x_e(t)$ gives the odd part.

**(b) Part (iii) — Energy/Power Classification (≈2 Marks)**

$$E = \int_{-1}^{2} (t+1)^2\,dt = \left[\frac{(t+1)^3}{3}\right]_{-1}^{2} = \frac{27}{3} - 0 = 9$$

Since $E = 9$ is finite and non-zero, $x(t)$ is an **energy signal** with $P = 0$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Classify signals based on:
   (i) Continuous-time vs Discrete-time    (ii) Periodic vs Aperiodic    (iii) Energy vs Power    **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the continuous-time signal $x(t) = e^{-2t}\,u(t)$:
   (i) Sketch $x(t)$ for $t \geq 0$.
   (ii) Determine whether $x(t)$ is an energy signal or a power signal. Compute the energy.
   (iii) Find the time-shifted, time-reversed, and time-scaled versions: $x(t-3)$, $x(-t)$, and $x(2t)$. Sketch all three on a single axis.    **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Part (i) — CT vs DT Classification (≈2.5 Marks)**

* **Continuous-Time (CT) Signal:** Independent variable $t$ takes all real values, signal is defined for every instant. Example: $x(t) = \sin(2\pi t)$.
* **Discrete-Time (DT) Signal:** Independent variable $n$ takes only integer values, signal is defined only at discrete instants. Example: $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ with $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$.

**(a) Part (ii) — Periodic vs Aperiodic (≈2.5 Marks)**

* **Periodic Signal:** A signal $x(t)$ is periodic with period $T$ if $x(t+T) = x(t)$ for all $t$, and $T$ is the smallest positive value satisfying this.
* **Aperiodic Signal:** No finite $T$ exists that satisfies the periodicity condition.

Examples: $\cos(2\pi t)$ is periodic with $T=1$; $e^{-t}u(t)$ is aperiodic.

**(a) Part (iii) — Energy vs Power (≈2 Marks)**

* **Energy Signal:** Total energy $E$ is finite and non-zero, average power $P = 0$.
* **Power Signal:** Total energy $E = \infty$ but average power $P$ is finite and non-zero.
* A signal **cannot** be both. Many signals (e.g. growing exponentials) are neither.

---

**(b) Part (i) — Sketch of $x(t) = e^{-2t}u(t)$ (≈1.5 Marks)**

* At $t=0$: $x(0) = 1$.
* At $t=0.5$: $x(0.5) = e^{-1} \approx 0.37$.
* At $t=1$: $x(1) = e^{-2} \approx 0.135$.
* The signal is zero for $t<0$ and decays exponentially for $t \geq 0$.

**(b) Part (ii) — Energy Computation (≈2.5 Marks)**

$$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert e^{-2t} \vert^2 \,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t}\,dt = \left[\frac{e^{-4t}}{-4}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{4}\right) = \frac{1}{4}$$

Since $E = 0.25$ is finite and non-zero, $x(t)$ is an **energy signal** with $P = 0$.

**(b) Part (iii) — Transformations (≈3 Marks)**

* **Time Shift:** $x(t-3) = e^{-2(t-3)}u(t-3)$. The waveform is identical to $x(t)$ but starts at $t=3$ instead of $t=0$.
* **Time Reversal:** $x(-t) = e^{-2(-t)}u(-t) = e^{2t}u(-t)$. This is a *growing* exponential defined for $t \leq 0$, mirrored about the vertical axis.
* **Time Scaling:** $x(2t) = e^{-2(2t)}u(2t) = e^{-4t}u(t)$. Same shape as $x(t)$ but decays *twice as fast* (compressed horizontally by factor 2).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> * In part (iii), students often mis-compute $x(-t)$ as $e^{-2t}u(t)$ instead of $e^{2t}u(-t)$. **Always replace $t$ by $-t$ throughout the entire expression**, including inside the step function.
> * Students frequently forget that $E$ and $P$ are **mutually exclusive**. If you compute a non-zero finite $E$, you must state $P = 0$ explicitly. Marks are deducted for an incomplete classification.
> * In energy/power problems, the formula $P = \lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T}|x(t)|^2dt$ must be used for CT, and the limit $\frac{1}{2N+1}$ for DT. Do not interchange.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print before exam):**

* A signal is a function conveying information. CT: $x(t)$; DT: $x[n]$.
* Six elementary signals: $\delta$, $u$, $r$, exponential, sinusoidal, signum.
* $\delta(t)$ has infinite height, zero width, unit area. It is the *derivative* of $u(t)$.
* $u(t)$ is the *integral* of $\delta(t)$ from $-\infty$ to $t$.
* $r(t) = t\,u(t)$; $\dfrac{d}{dt}r(t) = u(t)$.
* Sifting property: $\int x(t)\delta(t-t_0)dt = x(t_0)$; $\sum x[n]\delta[n-n_0] = x[n_0]$.
* Signum-step relation: $\text{sgn}(t) = 2u(t) - 1$ and $u(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}(\text{sgn}(t) + 1)$.
* Periodic if $x(t+T) = x(t)$ for some smallest positive $T$. Sum of two periodic signals is periodic only if $T_1/T_2$ is rational.
* Even: $x(-t) = x(t)$. Odd: $x(-t) = -x(t)$. Any signal splits uniquely into even + odd.
* Energy: $E = \int |x(t)|^2 dt$ (CT) or $\sum |x[n]|^2$ (DT). Power: $P = \lim (1/2T)\int |x|^2 dt$.
* Energy signals have $0 < E < \infty$ and $P = 0$. Power signals have $E = \infty$ and $0 < P < \infty$. A signal can be neither.
* Time shift: $x(t-t_0)$ delays by $t_0$. Time reversal: $x(-t)$ mirrors. Time scale: $x(at)$ compresses if $a>1$, expands if $0<a<1$.
* Combined operation $x(at-b)$: scale first by $a$, then shift by $b/a$ (right if $b/a > 0$).
* DT sinusoid periodic only if $\Omega_0/(2\pi)$ is rational. $\sin(\pi n)$ periodic with $N=2$; $\sin(n)$ is not periodic.
* The first difference of the DT ramp is $u[n-1]$; the running sum of $u[n]$ is $r[n+1]$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of System: Continuous Time and Discrete Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET503 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Concept of System: Continuous Time and Discrete Time Systems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of a System (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > **System Definition (KTU Standard):** > A **System** is a mathematical model that processes one or more input signals and produces corresponding output signals by transforming them according to a well-defined rule or set of operations. Formally, a system is an operator $T\{\cdot\}$ that maps an input signal $x(t)$ or $x[n]$ to an output signal $y(t)$ or $y[n]$. **Mathematical Representation:** $$y(t) = T\{x(t)\} \quad \text{(Continuous-Time System)}$$ $$y[n] = T\{x[n]\} \quad \text{(Discrete-Time System)}$$ where $T\{\cdot\}$ denotes the **system operator** or **transformation rule**. --- ## 1.2 Continuous-Time (CT) vs Discrete-Time (DT) Systems | Parameter | Continuous-Time System | Discrete-Time System | |-----------|------------------------|----------------------| | Input/Output | $x(t), y(t)$ defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | $x[n], y[n]$ defined for $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ | | Independent Variable | Continuous real variable $t$ | Integer index $n$ | | Implementation | Analog circuits, RLC networks | Digital processors, DSP chips | | Example | $y(t) = R \cdot x(t)$ (Resistor) | $y[n] = x[n] + x[n-1]$ (Accumulator) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** A system is classified as **CT** or **DT** based on the **nature of its input and output signals**, NOT merely on the time variable. If the input is sampled (discrete), the system is discrete-time. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Black Box" Imagine a **water filter** attached to your kitchen tap: - **Input ($x$)**: Dirty water entering the filter. - **System ($T$)**: The filter media (sand, charcoal, membrane) — the *transformation rule*. - **Output ($y$)**: Clean, purified water flowing out. You don't need to know the internal chemistry of the filter to use it; you only need to know: 1. What kind of input it accepts (signal type — tap water, not oil). 2. What output it produces (CT water flow vs DT droplets). This is exactly how a **Signals & Systems** engineer treats a system — as a **black box** with a defined input-output relationship. --- ## 1.4 Standard System Block Diagram (KTU Convention) ``` x(t) ──►[ System T{·} ]──► y(t) x[n] ──►[ System T{·} ]──► y[n] ``` The arrow direction indicates **causal flow**: input enters from the left, output exits from the right. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Systems (KTU Module 1 Syllabus) ### 2.1.1 Based on Signal Type - **Continuous-Time (CT) System**: Operates on continuous signals. - **Discrete-Time (DT) System**: Operates on sampled/discrete signals. - **Hybrid System**: Mixed CT input/DT output (e.g., ADC + DSP). ### 2.1.2 Based on Linearity A system is **Linear** if it satisfies the **Superposition Principle**: $$T\{a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t)\} = a_1 T\{x_1(t)\} + a_2 T\{x_2(t)\}$$ This comprises two sub-properties: - **Additivity**: $T\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = y_1(t) + y_2(t)$ - **Homogeneity (Scaling)**: $T\{a \cdot x(t)\} = a \cdot y(t)$ ### 2.1.3 Based on Time-Invariance A system is **Time-Invariant (TI)** if a time-shift in the input produces an identical time-shift in the output: $$y(t) = T\{x(t)\} \;\Rightarrow\; y(t-t_0) = T\{x(t-t_0)\}$$ ### 2.1.4 Other Critical Properties - **Causal System**: Output $y(t_0)$ depends only on input values for $t \leq t_0$ (no future inputs). - **Stable System (BIBO)**: Bounded-Input produces Bounded-Output. - **Memoryless System**: Output depends only on the present input. - **Invertible System**: Distinct inputs produce distinct outputs. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The table below consolidates **all KTU 2024 Module 1 system property tests** into a single, exam-ready reference. | Property | Test Condition | Example (CT) | Example (DT) | |----------|---------------|--------------|--------------| | **Linearity** | $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a y_1 + b y_2$ | $y(t) = a x(t)$ | $y[n] = 2 x[n]$ | | **Time-Invariance** | $y(t-t_0) = T\{x(t-t_0)\}$ | $y(t) = x(t) \cos(\omega t)$ | $y[n] = x[-n]$ (TI fails) | | **Causality** | Depends only on $x(\tau), \tau \leq t$ | $y(t) = x(t+1)$ (Non-causal) | $y[n] = x[n+2]$ (Non-causal) | | **Stability (BIBO)** | $\vert x(t) \vert < M \Rightarrow \vert y(t) \vert < \infty$ | $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau$ (Unstable) | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ (Unstable) | | **Memory** | Independent of past/future | $y(t) = 3x(t)$ (Memoryless) | $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ (Has memory) | | **Invertibility** | $T^{-1}\{T\{x\}\} = x$ | $y(t) = 2x(t)$ (Invertible) | $y[n] = 0$ (Not invertible) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** In real-world KTU-relevant domains like **biomedical signal processing (ECG/EEG filtering)** and **communication receivers (matched filters)**, the system must simultaneously satisfy **Linearity + Time-Invariance (LTI)** to enable frequency-domain analysis via Fourier/Laplace/Z-Transforms. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Examples | Domain | System | Type | |--------|--------|------| | Audio Amplifier | $y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$ | LTI, Memoryless, Causal, Stable | | RC Low-Pass Filter | $RC \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = x(t)$ | LTI, Dynamic, Causal, Stable | | Digital Accumulator | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ | LTI, Dynamic, Causal, **Unstable** | | Delay Element | $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$ | LTI, Causal (if $t_0 \geq 0$), Stable | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Linearity Test of a CT System **Problem:** Test the system $y(t) = x(t) \cdot \cos(\omega_0 t)$ for linearity. **Step 1 — Apply two arbitrary inputs $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ individually.** $$T\{x_1(t)\} = x_1(t) \cos(\omega_0 t) = y_1(t)$$ $$T\{x_2(t)\} = x_2(t) \cos(\omega_0 t) = y_2(t)$$ **Step 2 — Compute the weighted sum of individual outputs (RHS of superposition test).** $$a_1 y_1(t) + a_2 y_2(t) = a_1 x_1(t) \cos(\omega_0 t) + a_2 x_2(t) \cos(\omega_0 t)$$ $$a_1 y_1(t) + a_2 y_2(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t) \cdot [a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t)]$$ **Step 3 — Apply the combined input directly (LHS of superposition test).** $$T\{a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t)\} = [a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t)] \cos(\omega_0 t)$$ **Step 4 — Compare LHS and RHS.** $$T\{a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t)\} = a_1 y_1(t) + a_2 y_2(t) \quad \checkmark$$ **Conclusion:** The system is **LINEAR** (the multiplier $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ is a known time-varying function, but the operator itself is linear in $x$). --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Time-Invariance Test of a DT System **Problem:** Test the system $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$ for time-invariance. **Step 1 — Delay the input by $n_0$ and compute the output.** $$T\{x[n-n_0]\} = n \cdot x[n - n_0]$$ **Step 2 — Delay the original output by $n_0$.** $$y[n - n_0] = (n - n_0) \cdot x[n - n_0]$$ **Step 3 — Compare the two expressions.** $$T\{x[n-n_0]\} = n \cdot x[n - n_0]$$ $$y[n - n_0] = (n - n_0) \cdot x[n - n_0]$$ Since $n \neq (n - n_0)$ in general, the equality **fails**. **Conclusion:** The system is **TIME-VARYANT (TV)**. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3: BIBO Stability Test **Problem:** Check BIBO stability of the DT system $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$. **Step 1 — Assume bounded input.** $$\vert x[n] \vert \leq M \quad \text{for all } n$$ **Step 2 — Compute the upper bound of the output magnitude.** $$\vert y[n] \vert = \left\vert \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k] \right\vert \leq \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \vert x[k] \vert$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the bound.** $$\vert y[n] \vert \leq \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} M = M \cdot (n + \infty) = \infty$$ **Step 4 — Conclude.** As $n \to \infty$, the sum diverges. Hence, the bounded input can produce an **unbounded output**. **Conclusion:** The system is **NOT BIBO STABLE**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: System Property Quick-Check | System Equation | Linear? | Time-Invariant? | Causal? | Stable (BIBO)? | |------------------|---------|-----------------|---------|----------------| | $y(t) = 5 x(t)$ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | $y(t) = x(t) + 2$ | ❌ (DC offset) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | $y(t) = x(t-3)$ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | $y(t) = x(t+2)$ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | | $y[n] = x[-n]$ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | | $y[n] = x[n] \cdot u[n]$ | ❌ (due to $u[n]$) | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Generic System Representation ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Input Signal x(t) or x n"] --> B["System Operator T"] B --> C["Output Signal y(t) or y n"] B --> D["Properties: LTI Causal Stable"] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style B fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: System Interconnections (KTU Exam Favorite) ```mermaid flowchart TB X1["x1(t)"] --> S1["System T1"] S1 --> S2["System T2"] S2 --> Y1["y1(t)"] X2["x2(t)"] --> S3["System T3"] S3 --> ADD(("+")) S2 --> ADD ADD --> Y2["y2(t) Combined Output"] style X1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style X2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style S1 fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,color:#fff style S2 fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,color:#fff style S3 fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,color:#fff style ADD fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Y1 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Y2 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` **Interpretation:** - **Cascade (Series) Connection:** $x(t) \to T_1 \to T_2 \to y(t)$. Equivalent system: $T_{eq} = T_2 \{T_1\{\cdot\}\}$. - **Parallel Connection:** Outputs of $T_1$ and $T_3$ are summed via the adder. Equivalent system: $T_{eq} = T_1 + T_3$. ## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree: System Classification Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD START["Given System y = T of x"] --> Q1{"Input and Output are
continuous in time?"} Q1 -->|Yes| CT["Continuous Time System"] Q1 -->|No| DT["Discrete Time System"] CT --> Q2{"Satisfies
Superposition?"} DT --> Q2 Q2 -->|Yes| LIN["Linear System"] Q2 -->|No| NLIN["Non Linear System"] LIN --> Q3{"Time shift in input
equals time shift in output?"} NLIN --> Q3 Q3 -->|Yes| TI["Time Invariant"] Q3 -->|No| TV["Time Variant"] TI --> Q4{"Output depends only
on present and past input?"} TV --> Q4 Q4 -->|Yes| CAUS["Causal System"] Q4 -->|No| NC["Non Causal System"] style START fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style CT fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style DT fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style LIN fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style NLIN fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 style TI fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style TV fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 style CAUS fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style NC fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a System. Differentiate between Continuous-Time and Discrete-Time systems. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** > A **system** is a mathematical operator $T\{\cdot\}$ that maps an input signal $x$ to an output signal $y$ through a defined transformation rule: $y = T\{x\}$. **Differences:** | Feature | CT System | DT System | |---------|-----------|-----------| | Independent Variable | Continuous $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | Discrete $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ | | Signal Nature | Uninterrupted waveform | Sampled at discrete instants | | Examples | $y(t) = R \cdot x(t)$ | $y[n] = x[n] + x[n-1]$ | | Processing | Analog hardware | Digital processors | --- ### Question 2: State the properties of a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** An **LTI system** is one that satisfies both **Linearity** and **Time-Invariance** simultaneously. **Linearity (Superposition):** $$T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a y_1 + b y_2$$ **Time-Invariance:** $$y(t - t_0) = T\{x(t - t_0)\}$$ > LTI systems can be completely characterized by their **impulse response** $h(t)$ or $h[n]$, enabling powerful analysis using **convolution** and **transform methods** (Fourier, Laplace, Z-Transform). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Determine whether the following systems are (i) Linear, (ii) Time-Invariant, (iii) Causal, (iv) BIBO Stable: 1. $y(t) = e^{x(t)}$ 2. $y[n] = x[n] \cdot \cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{2}\right)$ **(b) [7 Marks]** For the system $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$, perform the **complete linearity test** using inputs $x_1(t) = \sin(t)$ and $x_2(t) = \cos(t)$ with arbitrary constants $a_1 = 2$ and $a_2 = 3$. --- ### Model Solution for Question A: **Part (a)(1): $y(t) = e^{x(t)}$** | Property | Test | Result | |----------|------|--------| | **Linearity** | $T\{a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2\} = e^{a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2} \neq a_1 e^{x_1} + a_2 e^{x_2}$ | ❌ **Non-Linear** | | **Time-Invariance** | $T\{x(t-t_0)\} = e^{x(t-t_0)} = y(t-t_0)$ | ✅ **TI** | | **Causality** | $y(t)$ depends only on $x(t)$ at same instant | ✅ **Causal** | | **Stability** | If $\vert x(t) \vert < M$, then $\vert y(t) \vert = \vert e^{x(t)} \vert < e^M$ (bounded) | ✅ **Stable** | **Part (a)(2): $y[n] = x[n] \cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{2}\right)$** | Property | Test | Result | |----------|------|--------| | **Linearity** | $T\{a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2\} = [a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2] \cos(\pi n/2) = a_1 y_1 + a_2 y_2$ | ✅ **Linear** | | **Time-Invariance** | $T\{x[n-n_0]\} = x[n-n_0] \cos(\pi n/2)$; but $y[n-n_0] = x[n-n_0] \cos(\pi(n-n_0)/2)$ | ❌ **TV** | | **Causality** | $y[n]$ depends only on $x[n]$ | ✅ **Causal** | | **Stability** | $\vert \cos(\pi n/2) \vert \leq 1$, so $\vert y[n] \vert \leq \vert x[n] \vert$ | ✅ **Stable** | **Part (b): Linearity Test for $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$** **Step 1:** Compute individual outputs. $$y_1(t) = t \cdot \sin(t)$$ $$y_2(t) = t \cdot \cos(t)$$ **Step 2:** Compute weighted sum (RHS). $$a_1 y_1(t) + a_2 y_2(t) = 2 t \sin(t) + 3 t \cos(t) = t[2\sin(t) + 3\cos(t)]$$ **Step 3:** Compute output of combined input (LHS). $$T\{2 \sin(t) + 3 \cos(t)\} = t[2\sin(t) + 3\cos(t)]$$ **Step 4:** LHS = RHS ✅ **Conclusion:** The system is **LINEAR**. --- ### Question B: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concepts of **causality** and **BIBO stability** in continuous-time systems. For the integrator system $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau$, determine whether it is **causal** and **BIBO stable**. **(b) [7 Marks]** Two systems are connected in **cascade**. System 1: $y_1(t) = 2 x(t) + 3$. System 2: $y_2(t) = \frac{d y_1(t)}{dt}$. Determine whether the **overall cascade system** is **Linear** and **Time-Invariant**. --- ### Model Solution for Question B: **Part (a): Causality and BIBO Stability for the Integrator** > **Causality Definition:** A CT system is causal if $y(t)$ depends only on $x(\tau)$ for $\tau \leq t$. For the integrator $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau$, the upper limit is $t$, meaning the output uses input values up to the present instant. **No future values are involved.** ✅ **The integrator is CAUSAL.** > **BIBO Stability Definition:** A system is BIBO stable if every bounded input produces a bounded output: $\vert x(t) \vert < M \Rightarrow \vert y(t) \vert < \infty$. **Step 1:** Apply bounded input. $$\vert x(t) \vert \leq M$$ **Step 2:** Compute output bound. $$\vert y(t) \vert = \left\vert \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau \right\vert \leq \int_{-\infty}^{t} \vert x(\tau) \vert d\tau \leq \int_{-\infty}^{t} M d\tau = M \cdot (t + \infty)$$ **Step 3:** The integral over $(-\infty, t)$ **diverges** for a constant bounded input. ❌ **The integrator is NOT BIBO STABLE.** --- **Part (b): Linearity & TI Test of Cascade System** **Cascade Equivalent:** $$y(t) = \frac{d}{dt}[2 x(t) + 3] = 2 \frac{dx(t)}{dt} + 0 = 2 \frac{dx(t)}{dt}$$ **Linearity Test:** Let $x(t) = a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t)$. $$T\{a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2\} = 2 \frac{d}{dt}[a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2] = 2 a_1 \frac{dx_1}{dt} + 2 a_2 \frac{dx_2}{dt}$$ $$a_1 y_1 + a_2 y_2 = a_1 (2 \frac{dx_1}{dt}) + a_2 (2 \frac{dx_2}{dt})$$ LHS = RHS ✅ **Time-Invariance Test:** $$T\{x(t-t_0)\} = 2 \frac{d}{dt} x(t-t_0) = 2 x'(t-t_0)$$ $$y(t-t_0) = 2 \frac{d}{dt} x(t-t_0) = 2 x'(t-t_0)$$ LHS = RHS ✅ **Conclusion:** The overall cascade system is **LINEAR and TIME-INVARIANT (LTI)**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing Linearity with TI:** A system can be linear but time-varying (e.g., $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$). Test BOTH properties independently. > 2. **Forgetting the constant term in System 1:** The $+3$ in $y_1(t) = 2x(t) + 3$ makes System 1 **non-linear alone**, but its derivative (System 2) eliminates the constant, making the **cascade** linear. Students often mark the cascade as non-linear without computing the equivalent equation. > 3. **Stability of the Integrator:** Many students incorrectly label the integrator as stable because they forget the integration extends to $-\infty$. The impulse response of the integrator $h(t) = u(t)$ is **not absolutely integrable**, so it is **unstable**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield KTU Module 1 Checklist — Continuous & Discrete Time Systems** - ✅ A **system** transforms an input signal $x$ into an output signal $y$ via an operator $T$: $y = T\{x\}$. - ✅ A **CT system** operates on signals defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$; a **DT system** operates on signals defined for integer $n$. - ✅ **Linearity** requires **additivity + homogeneity** (Superposition Principle). - ✅ **Time-Invariance** requires that a time-shift in input produces an identical time-shift in output. - ✅ A system is **causal** if the output at any time depends only on present and past input values. - ✅ A system is **BIBO stable** if bounded input guarantees bounded output. - ✅ **Memoryless** systems: output depends only on the present input (e.g., $y(t) = 3x(t)$). - ✅ **Invertibility**: distinct inputs must produce distinct outputs; otherwise the system loses information. - ✅ **Cascade connection**: equivalent system $T_{eq} = T_2 \circ T_1$ (apply $T_1$ first). - ✅ **Parallel connection**: equivalent system $T_{eq} = T_1 + T_2$ (outputs summed). - ✅ **LTI systems** are the **most important** class in KTU Module 1 — fully characterized by their **impulse response** $h(t)$ or $h[n]$. - ✅ Common memory/dynamic systems: **delays** ($x(t-t_0)$), **accumulators** ($\sum x[k]$), **differentiators** ($dy/dt$). - ✅ In KTU exams, **always write the test equations explicitly** for linearity, TI, causality, and stability — partial marks are awarded for correct procedure even if the final answer is wrong.

topics.title: Properties of Systems: Time Invariance, Linearity, Causality, Memory and Memoryless Systems, Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET503 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of Systems: Time Invariance, Linearity, Causality, Memory and Memoryless Systems, Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In the study of **Signals and Systems**, a **System** is mathematically defined as a unique transformation operator $T\{\cdot\}$ that maps an input signal $x(t)$ to an output signal $y(t)$. Before analyzing any system (electrical, mechanical, or biological), an engineer must classify it based on five fundamental properties: **Linearity, Time Invariance, Causality, Memory, and Stability**. These properties are the "DNA" of a system and dictate how it will behave under real-world operating conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Classification of systems is a **high-weightage, compulsory topic** in the End Semester Examination (ESE). The standard university question pattern requires you to determine *all five properties* of a given continuous-time (CT) or discrete-time (DT) system in a single problem.

### 1.1 Linearity (The Superposition Property)

**Formal Definition:**
A system is **Linear** if and only if it satisfies the *Superposition Principle*, which is the combination of two sub-properties: **Additivity** and **Homogeneity (Scaling)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Test for Linearity:**
> Let $y_1(t) = T\{x_1(t)\}$ and $y_2(t) = T\{x_2(t)\}$.
> If $T\{a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)\} = a \cdot y_1(t) + b \cdot y_2(t)$ for all constants $a, b$, then the system is **Linear**. If this condition fails for even a single test case, the system is **Non-Linear**.

**Intuitive Analogy (The Recipe Machine):**
Imagine a vending machine that takes ingredients (input) and produces a cake (output). If you put in *double the flour and double the sugar* (scaling/homogeneity), you get *double the cake*. If you mix the batter of *one chocolate cake* and *one vanilla cake* (additivity), you get a combined batter. This predictable, scalable behavior is **Linearity**. A non-linear system would be like a machine that burns the cake if you double the ingredients — unpredictable and non-scalable.

### 1.2 Time Invariance (The Patience Property)

**Formal Definition:**
A system is **Time-Invariant (TI)** if a time shift (delay or advance) in the input signal produces an identical time shift in the output signal. The system's behavior does *not* depend on *when* the input is applied.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Test for Time Invariance:**
> Let $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$. The system is TI if and only if:
> $$T\{x(t - t_0)\} = y(t - t_0) \quad \text{for any } t_0 \in \mathbb{R}$$
> If the coefficients of the system equation contain the independent variable $t$ or $n$ explicitly, the system is **Time-Variant**.

**Intuitive Analogy (The Echo in an Empty Room):**
Shout a word in an empty hall today, and you hear an echo. Shout the same word in the *same hall* tomorrow at a different time, and you still hear the same echo with the same delay and intensity. The room's acoustic response is **Time-Invariant**. If the room's walls slowly closed in over time (changing the echo), it would be **Time-Variant**.

### 1.3 Causality (The No-Time-Machine Property)

**Formal Definition:**
A system is **Causal** if the output at any time $t = t_0$ depends *only* on the present and past values of the input, and **never** on future values.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Test for Causality:**
> For a CT system, the output $y(t)$ is causal if:
> $$y(t_0) = f\{x(t) \text{ for } t \leq t_0\}$$
> For a DT system: $y[n] = f\{x[k] \text{ for } k \leq n\}$.
> Any system whose output depends on $x(t+1)$, $x[n+1]$, $x[-n]$, or $x(t^2)$ (since $t^2 > t$ for large $t$) is **Non-Causal**.

**Intuitive Analogy (The Cricket Commentator):**
A *causal* cricket commentator describes a ball *only after* the batsman has hit it. He uses the past (the bowler's run-up) and the present (the contact moment). A *non-causal* commentator would tell you the result of the shot *before* the bowler has even released the ball — impossible in real-time, but perfectly fine for *recorded/streaming* systems (offline processing).

### 1.4 Memory and Memoryless Systems

**Formal Definitions:**

- **Memoryless System:** The output at any time depends *exclusively* on the input at the *same instant*. It has no "memory" of past or future inputs.
  $$y(t) = f\{x(t)\} \quad \text{or} \quad y[n] = f\{x[n]\}$$

- **System with Memory:** The output depends on past values, future values, or the integral/derivative of the input over an interval.
  Example: A capacitor ($v(t) = \frac{1}{C}\int i(t) dt$) has memory because its voltage depends on the *history* of the current.

**Intuitive Analogy (The Calculator vs. The Accountant):**
A simple calculator displaying $y = x^2$ is **memoryless** — it only sees the current number you press. An **accountant** calculating your running bank balance is a system *with memory* — they need the previous balance (past) and the new transaction (present) to find the new balance.

### 1.5 Stability (The Bounded-Input Bounded-Output Property)

**Formal Definition:**
A system is **BIBO Stable** (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output Stable) if every *bounded* input signal produces a *bounded* output signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Test for BIBO Stability:**
> If $|x(t)| \leq M_x < \infty$ for all $t$, then the system is stable if and only if there exists some finite constant $M_y$ such that:
> $$|y(t)| \leq M_y < \infty \quad \text{for all } t$$
> A system is **Unstable** if at least one bounded input produces an unbounded (infinite) output.

**Intuitive Analogy (The Glass of Water on a Tray):**
A stable system is like a tray with a flat bottom — if you gently place a glass of water (bounded input) on it, the glass remains still (bounded output). An unstable system is like a tray balanced on a point — the slightest touch (even a bounded input) causes the glass to fall and shatter (unbounded output).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Breakdown — How to Test Each Property

The KTU board examination expects students to follow a strict, step-by-step procedure when determining system properties. Below is the operational logic used by top-scorers.

#### Testing Linearity (Two-Step Verification)
1. **Step 1 (Additivity):** Find $y_1(t)$ for $x_1(t)$ and $y_2(t)$ for $x_2(t)$. Then check if the response to the sum equals the sum of individual responses.
2. **Step 2 (Homogeneity):** Multiply input by a constant $a$, and check if the output is also multiplied by the same constant $a$.
3. **Verdict:** If *both* steps pass, the system is **Linear**. If even *one* fails, it is **Non-Linear** (do not test further; declare it non-linear immediately).

#### Testing Time Invariance (Delay Test)
1. **Step 1:** Find the output $y(t)$ for the original input $x(t)$.
2. **Step 2:** Shift the *input* by $t_0$ to get $x(t-t_0)$, then compute the system response $T\{x(t-t_0)\}$.
3. **Step 3:** Shift the *output* $y(t)$ by the same $t_0$ to get $y(t-t_0)$.
4. **Verdict:** If Step 2 $\equiv$ Step 3 for *any* $t_0$, the system is **Time-Invariant**. Otherwise, **Time-Variant**.

#### Testing Causality (Time-Domain Inspection)
- Inspect every term in $y(t)$. If a term uses $x(t+k)$ where $k>0$, or $x(-t)$, or involves a time-reversal operator — the system is **Non-Causal**.
- Causal systems *must not* look into the "future" of the input.

#### Testing Memory (Instantaneous Check)
- If $y(t)$ is purely a function of $x(t)$ at the same instant (e.g., $y(t) = 2x(t) + 5$, or $y(t) = \sin(x(t))$), the system is **Memoryless**.
- Any presence of integrals, derivatives, delays, or accumulators means the system has **Memory**.

#### Testing Stability (Asymptotic Boundedness Check)
- Apply a bounded test input (e.g., $x(t) = u(t)$, the unit step, which is bounded at 1) and observe if the output remains finite.
- For LTI systems described by impulse response $h(t)$: **Stable if and only if** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} |h(t)| dt < \infty$.

### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Property | Real-World Engineering Application |
|---|---|
| **Linearity** | Foundation of *Fourier Analysis*, *Laplace Transforms*, and *Z-Transforms*. Linear systems can be analyzed using superposition, making them computationally tractable. |
| **Time Invariance** | Used in designing **LTI Filters** (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass) in audio processing, image processing, and communication receivers. |
| **Causality** | Critical for **real-time systems** (radar, flight control, pacemakers). Non-causal filters are only allowed in *offline* processing (e.g., image deblurring on a stored photo). |
| **Memory** | Determines whether a system needs **storage elements** (capacitors, inductors, memory registers) in its hardware design. |
| **Stability** | The *most critical* property. Unstable systems in feedback control (e.g., a mis-tuned cruise control) can lead to catastrophic failure. |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **most condensed reference** for solving KTU problems on system properties. Memorize this table before the exam.

| Property | Test Condition (CT) | Test Condition (DT) | Quick Identification Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | $T\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} = a y_1(t) + b y_2(t)$ | $T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} = a y_1[n] + b y_2[n]$ | No products of $x(t)$ with itself, no $\log$, $\exp$ of $x$, no $|\cdot|$ of $x$. |
| **Time-Invariant** | $T\{x(t-t_0)\} = y(t-t_0)$ | $T\{x[n-n_0]\} = y[n-n_0]$ | Coefficients of the system equation must *not* contain $t$ or $n$. |
| **Causal** | $y(t_0)$ depends only on $x(\tau)$ for $\tau \leq t_0$ | $y[n_0]$ depends only on $x[k]$ for $k \leq n_0$ | No terms like $x(t+1)$, $x[n+2]$, $x(-t)$. |
| **Memoryless** | $y(t) = f\{x(t)\}$ only | $y[n] = f\{x[n]\}$ only | No integrals, derivatives, delays, or accumulators. |
| **BIBO Stable** | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$ | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ | Bounded test input $\Rightarrow$ bounded output. |

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Tests — A Deeper Look

- **Why test Linearity?** Because the *entire framework* of frequency-domain analysis (Fourier, Laplace, Z) is built on the assumption of linearity. Without linearity, superposition fails, and we cannot decompose a complex signal into sinusoids.
- **Why test Time Invariance?** TI systems can be characterized by a single mathematical function (the impulse response $h(t)$) that works the same way *forever*. Time-variant systems require a different $h(t)$ for every moment.
- **Why test Causality?** Real-time physical hardware *cannot* see the future. Causality is a hard constraint in *real-time* embedded systems. Non-causal systems are allowed *only* in offline DSP.
- **Why test Memory?** Memory dictates the *physical hardware* required. A memoryless resistor circuit needs no storage element; an integrator (capacitor) inherently has memory.
- **Why test Stability?** An unstable feedback system can self-destruct. Every engineering system design begins with a stability check.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section demonstrates the *exhaustive mathematical procedure* for testing each system property on a sample problem. The technique shown is exactly what examiners expect in a 14-mark KTU question.

### 3.1 Worked Example: A Complete Property Test

> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> Determine whether the continuous-time system described by $y(t) = t \cdot x(t) + 3$ is:
> (a) Linear / Non-Linear
> (b) Time-Invariant / Time-Variant
> (c) Causal / Non-Causal
> (d) Memoryless / With Memory
> (e) Stable / Unstable

---

#### (a) Linearity Test — Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1: Additivity Test**

Apply $x_1(t)$ to the system to find $y_1(t)$:
$$y_1(t) = T\{x_1(t)\} = t \cdot x_1(t) + 3$$

Apply $x_2(t)$ to the system to find $y_2(t)$:
$$y_2(t) = T\{x_2(t)\} = t \cdot x_2(t) + 3$$

Apply the *sum* $x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ to the system:
$$T\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = t \cdot [x_1(t) + x_2(t)] + 3$$

Simplify by distributing $t$:
$$T\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = t \cdot x_1(t) + t \cdot x_2(t) + 3$$

Now compute the *sum of individual responses*:
$$y_1(t) + y_2(t) = [t \cdot x_1(t) + 3] + [t \cdot x_2(t) + 3] = t \cdot x_1(t) + t \cdot x_2(t) + 6$$

**Comparison:**
$$T\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = t \cdot x_1(t) + t \cdot x_2(t) + 3$$
$$y_1(t) + y_2(t) = t \cdot x_1(t) + t \cdot x_2(t) + 6$$

Since the constant terms ($3 \neq 6$) do not match, **additivity fails**.

**Step 2: Short-Circuit Conclusion**
Because the additivity test already failed, the system is **Non-Linear**. We do not need to test homogeneity; a single failure of superposition is sufficient.

> **Verdict: The system is Non-Linear.**

---

#### (b) Time Invariance Test — Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1: Find $y(t)$ for the original input $x(t)$**
$$y(t) = t \cdot x(t) + 3$$

**Step 2: Delay the input by $t_0$ to get $x(t-t_0)$, and find the system response**
$$T\{x(t-t_0)\} = t \cdot x(t-t_0) + 3$$

**Step 3: Delay the original output $y(t)$ by the same $t_0$**
$$y(t-t_0) = (t-t_0) \cdot x(t-t_0) + 3$$

**Step 4: Compare Step 2 and Step 3**

$$T\{x(t-t_0)\} = t \cdot x(t-t_0) + 3$$
$$y(t-t_0) = t \cdot x(t-t_0) - t_0 \cdot x(t-t_0) + 3$$

The extra term $- t_0 \cdot x(t-t_0)$ appears in the second expression but not in the first.

**Step 5: Conclusion**
Since $T\{x(t-t_0)\} \neq y(t-t_0)$, the system is **Time-Variant**.

> **Verdict: The system is Time-Variant.**

*Note: The presence of the explicit variable $t$ as a coefficient is the *visual red flag* that the system is time-variant.*

---

#### (c) Causality Test — Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1: Inspect the output equation**
$$y(t) = t \cdot x(t) + 3$$

**Step 2: Identify the input argument**
The input argument is $x(t)$ — the *current* value of the input. There is no $x(t+1)$, $x(t+5)$, or $x(-t)$ present.

**Step 3: Conclusion**
The output depends *only* on the present input.

> **Verdict: The system is Causal.**

---

#### (d) Memory Test — Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1: Inspect the output equation for time-shifted inputs**
$$y(t) = t \cdot x(t) + 3$$

The input appears *only* as $x(t)$ at the *same* instant. There are no:
- Integrals: $\int x(\tau) d\tau$
- Derivatives: $\frac{dx(t)}{dt}$
- Delays: $x(t-1)$, $x(t-2)$

**Step 2: Conclusion**
The output depends *exclusively* on the present input value.

> **Verdict: The system is Memoryless.**

---

#### (e) Stability Test — Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1: Apply a bounded test input**
Choose a bounded input, e.g., the unit step $x(t) = u(t)$, which satisfies $|x(t)| \leq 1$ for all $t \geq 0$.

**Step 2: Compute the output**
$$y(t) = t \cdot u(t) + 3 = t + 3 \quad \text{for } t \geq 0$$

**Step 3: Check boundedness**
As $t \to \infty$, the output $y(t) = t + 3 \to \infty$. The output is **unbounded**.

**Step 4: Conclusion**
A bounded input produced an unbounded output.

> **Verdict: The system is Unstable (not BIBO stable).**

---

### 3.2 Summary Matrix for the Worked Example

| Property | Verdict | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Linear | **Non-Linear** | Additivity fails (constant term $3 \neq 6$). |
| Time-Invariant | **Time-Variant** | Coefficient $t$ depends on time explicitly. |
| Causal | **Causal** | Output uses only $x(t)$, not future values. |
| Memory | **Memoryless** | No integral, derivative, or delay present. |
| Stable | **Unstable** | Bounded step input $u(t)$ gives unbounded output $t+3$. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Master Classification Flowchart of System Properties

The diagram below is the **master decision tree** that a KTU student should mentally traverse when solving property-classification problems. Each branch shows the decision logic and the verdict for each property.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given System T] --> B[Inspect Output Equation]
    B --> C{Linearity Test}
    C -- Pass both additivity and homogeneity --> D1[LINEAR]
    C -- Fail any test --> D2[NON-LINEAR]
    D1 --> E{Time Invariance Test}
    D2 --> E
    E -- Tx-t0 equals yt-t0 --> F1[TIME-INVARIANT]
    E -- Tx-t0 NOT equal yt-t0 --> F2[TIME-VARIANT]
    F1 --> G{Causality Test}
    F2 --> G
    G -- Output uses only x of tau leq t --> H1[CAUSAL]
    G -- Output uses x of tau greater than t --> H2[NON-CAUSAL]
    H1 --> I{Memory Test}
    H2 --> I
    I -- y depends only on xt at same instant --> J1[MEMORYLESS]
    I -- y uses past or future input --> J2[WITH MEMORY]
    J1 --> K{Stability Test}
    J2 --> K
    K -- Bounded input gives bounded output --> L1[STABLE BIBO]
    K -- Some bounded input gives unbounded output --> L2[UNSTABLE]
    L1 --> M[End: All 5 Properties Determined]
    L2 --> M
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of System Classification

This schematic shows the *interrelation* of the five properties and how they collectively define a system class (e.g., an **LTI Causal Stable System with Memory** is the most important real-world category).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Signal Domain]
        INP[Input Signal xt]
    end
    subgraph SystemCore[System Operator T]
        PROP1[Property 1: Linearity]
        PROP2[Property 2: Time Invariance]
        PROP3[Property 3: Causality]
        PROP4[Property 4: Memory]
        PROP5[Property 5: Stability]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Response]
        OUTP[Output Signal yt]
    end
    INP --> PROP1
    PROP1 --> PROP2
    PROP2 --> PROP3
    PROP3 --> PROP4
    PROP4 --> PROP5
    PROP5 --> OUTP
    PROP1 -.Feedback.-> PROP5
    PROP2 -.Feedback.-> PROP3
    PROP4 -.Feedback.-> PROP2
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram of a Real-Time Causal LTI System

This block diagram illustrates how the five properties manifest in a practical real-time signal processing chain (e.g., a digital audio equalizer).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microphone Input xt] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Filter LTI and Stable]
    B --> C[ADC Real-time Causal]
    C --> D[Digital LTI Filter Linear and Time-Invariant]
    D --> E[DAC Causal Memoryless]
    E --> F[Speaker Output yt]
    D -.Memory element.-> D
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
>
> **Q1.** Define a *memoryless* system. Give one example of a memoryless system and one example of a system with memory.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A system is **memoryless** if its output at any time $t$ depends *only* on the input at the *same* instant $t$, and not on any past or future values of the input.
> - **Example of a Memoryless System:** A resistor with voltage-current relation $v(t) = R \cdot i(t)$. The voltage at any time depends only on the current at that same instant.
> - **Example of a System with Memory:** A capacitor with relation $v(t) = \frac{1}{C} \int_{-\infty}^{t} i(\tau) \, d\tau$. The voltage depends on the *entire history* of the current up to time $t$.
> *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two examples.)*

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
>
> **Q2.** What is BIBO stability? State the necessary and sufficient condition for a continuous-time LTI system to be BIBO stable in terms of its impulse response.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **BIBO Stability** stands for **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output Stability**. A system is BIBO stable if every bounded input signal produces a bounded output signal.
> **Necessary and Sufficient Condition (for CT LTI):** A continuous-time LTI system with impulse response $h(t)$ is BIBO stable if and only if its impulse response is **absolutely integrable**, i.e.,
> $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$$
> *(Award 1.5 marks for BIBO definition, 1.5 marks for the integral condition.)*

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (KTU Standard):** *Answer any ONE of the following.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question]**
>
> For the continuous-time system described by:
> $$y(t) = x(t) \cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)$$
> where $\omega_0$ and $\theta$ are real constants, determine whether the system is:
> (a) Linear or Non-Linear *(7 marks)*
> (b) Time-Invariant or Time-Variant *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Linearity Test (7 marks):**

*Step 1: Additivity Test*

Apply input $x_1(t)$ to get output $y_1(t)$:
$$y_1(t) = T\{x_1(t)\} = x_1(t) \cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)$$

Apply input $x_2(t)$ to get output $y_2(t)$:
$$y_2(t) = T\{x_2(t)\} = x_2(t) \cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)$$

Apply the sum $x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ to the system:
$$T\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = [x_1(t) + x_2(t)] \cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)$$

Distribute the cosine term:
$$T\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = x_1(t) \cos(\omega_0 t + \theta) + x_2(t) \cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)$$

This equals $y_1(t) + y_2(t)$. **Additivity holds. (2 marks)**

*Step 2: Homogeneity Test*

Apply scaled input $a \cdot x_1(t)$:
$$T\{a \cdot x_1(t)\} = a \cdot x_1(t) \cdot \cos(\omega_0 t + \theta) = a \cdot y_1(t)$$

**Homogeneity holds. (2 marks)**

*Step 3: Verdict*
Since both additivity and homogeneity hold, the system is **Linear**. (3 marks)

**Part (b) — Time Invariance Test (7 marks):**

*Step 1:* Find the response to the original input:
$$y(t) = x(t) \cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)$$

*Step 2:* Delay the input by $t_0$ and compute the response:
$$T\{x(t-t_0)\} = x(t-t_0) \cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)$$

*Step 3:* Delay the original output by the same $t_0$:
$$y(t-t_0) = x(t-t_0) \cos(\omega_0 (t-t_0) + \theta)$$

*Step 4:* Compare the two expressions. Expand the cosine in $y(t-t_0)$:
$$y(t-t_0) = x(t-t_0) \cos(\omega_0 t - \omega_0 t_0 + \theta)$$

*Step 5:* The two expressions are different because:
- $T\{x(t-t_0)\}$ contains the term $\cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)$
- $y(t-t_0)$ contains the term $\cos(\omega_0 t - \omega_0 t_0 + \theta)$

Since $T\{x(t-t_0)\} \neq y(t-t_0)$, the system is **Time-Variant**. (3 marks)

> **Final Answer for Question A:** (a) Linear, (b) Time-Variant.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question]**
>
> For the discrete-time system described by:
> $$y[n] = n \cdot x[n] + x[n-1]$$
> determine whether the system is:
> (a) Causal or Non-Causal; Memoryless or With Memory *(7 marks)*
> (b) Linear or Non-Linear; BIBO Stable or Unstable *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Causality and Memory Test (7 marks):**

*Step 1: Causality Test*

The output $y[n]$ depends on:
- $x[n]$ — the *present* input (allowed for a causal system)
- $x[n-1]$ — the *past* input (allowed for a causal system)

There are no terms like $x[n+1]$, $x[n+2]$, or $x[-n]$. Therefore, the output depends *only* on the present and past inputs.

**Verdict: The system is Causal. (3 marks)**

*Step 2: Memory Test*

The output $y[n]$ depends on $x[n-1]$, which is a *delayed* (past) version of the input. This means the system retains a "memory" of the previous input sample.

**Verdict: The system has Memory. (4 marks)**

**Part (b) — Linearity and Stability Test (7 marks):**

*Step 1: Linearity Test (Additivity)*

Apply $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$ separately:
$$y_1[n] = n \cdot x_1[n] + x_1[n-1]$$
$$y_2[n] = n \cdot x_2[n] + x_2[n-1]$$

Apply the sum:
$$T\{x_1[n] + x_2[n]\} = n \cdot [x_1[n] + x_2[n]] + [x_1[n-1] + x_2[n-1]] = y_1[n] + y_2[n]$$

Additivity holds. (1 mark)

*Step 2: Linearity Test (Homogeneity)*

Apply scaled input $a \cdot x_1[n]$:
$$T\{a \cdot x_1[n]\} = n \cdot (a \cdot x_1[n]) + (a \cdot x_1[n-1]) = a \cdot y_1[n]$$

Homogeneity holds. (1 mark)

**Verdict: The system is Linear. (2 marks)**

*Step 3: Stability Test*

Apply a bounded test input, e.g., $x[n] = 1$ for all $n$ (a constant, bounded by 1).

$$y[n] = n \cdot 1 + 1 = n + 1$$

As $n \to \infty$, $y[n] \to \infty$. The output is **unbounded** even though the input is bounded.

**Verdict: The system is Unstable. (3 marks)**

> **Final Answer for Question B:** (a) Causal and has Memory, (b) Linear and Unstable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **The "+ Constant" Trap:** Many students declare a system non-linear just because they see a constant like $+3$ in $y(t)$. While a constant *does* violate additivity, you *must* show the algebraic mismatch (e.g., $3 \neq 6$) for full marks. *Just stating "it has a constant, hence non-linear" without proof loses 1-2 marks.*
> 2. **The $t$ Coefficient Confusion:** A term like $t \cdot x(t)$ makes the system *time-variant* but *does not* automatically make it non-linear. The variable $t$ is an external parameter, not the input. *Do not confuse time-variance with non-linearity.*
> 3. **Causality vs. Memory Confusion:** A *causal* system can still have *memory* (e.g., a causal FIR filter $y[n] = x[n] + x[n-1]$ is causal but has memory). These two properties are *independent* — test them separately.
> 4. **Stability Test Boundedness:** Always choose a *simple* bounded input like $u(t)$ or $x(t) = 1$. Using $x(t) = \delta(t)$ (impulse) is *not* a good bounded test, as $\delta(t)$ is technically unbounded in amplitude at $t=0$.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Linearity** = Additivity + Homogeneity. Test both; one failure = Non-Linear.
- ✅ **Time Invariance** = Coefficients must not contain $t$ (or $n$ for DT). Test using the delay-shift method.
- ✅ **Causality** = No future inputs. Look out for $x(t+1)$, $x[-t]$, $x(t^2)$ — all are non-causal.
- ✅ **Memoryless** = $y(t)$ is purely a function of $x(t)$ at the same instant. No integrals, derivatives, or delays.
- ✅ **BIBO Stability** = Bounded input $\Rightarrow$ Bounded output. For LTI: $\int \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$.
- ✅ A system can be **Linear and Time-Variant** (e.g., $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$) or **Non-Linear and Time-Invariant** (e.g., $y(t) = x^2(t)$).
