- ✅ A **Causal** system can still have **Memory**; these are independent properties.
- ✅ **Unstable** systems must be redesigned before deployment — stability is non-negotiable in feedback control.
- ✅ Always **show full derivation** in the exam; a bare verdict without proof gets only partial credit.
- ✅ Master the **five-step decision tree** (mermaid diagram in Section 4.1) — it works for *any* KTU problem on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convolution Integral and Sum, Impulse and Step Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET503 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Convolution Integral and Sum, Impulse and Step Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Convolution Integral and Sum, Impulse and Step Response

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Convolution: The Formal Definition

**Convolution** is a mathematical operator that produces a third signal (the output) from two input signals. It expresses how the shape of one signal is modified by the other. For **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) systems**, convolution is the fundamental tool that determines the output of the system for any arbitrary input, given that we know the system's response to a unit impulse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Convolution is the mathematical process that represents the response of an LTI system to any input signal, expressed as a weighted superposition of shifted and scaled impulse responses. It bridges the time-domain description with the system's memory and causal behavior.

The **Continuous-Time Convolution Integral** is defined as:

$$y(t) = x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, h(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

The **Discrete-Time Convolution Sum** is defined as:

$$y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, h[n - k]$$

where:
- $x(t)$ or $x[n]$ is the **input signal** to the LTI system
- $h(t)$ or $h[n]$ is the **unit impulse response** of the LTI system
- $y(t)$ or $y[n]$ is the **output signal**
- $\tau$ (continuous) or $k$ (discrete) is the **dummy integration/summation variable**
- The symbol $*$ denotes the **convolution operator**

### 1.2 Impulse Response: The System's Fingerprint

The **Impulse Response** of an LTI system is the output of the system when the input is a unit impulse. It is denoted by $h(t)$ in continuous time and $h[n]$ in discrete time.

$$\delta(t) \xrightarrow{\text{LTI System}} h(t)$$

$$\delta[n] \xrightarrow{\text{LTI System}} h[n]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the impulse response so important?**
> Because for any LTI system, knowledge of $h(t)$ (or $h[n]$) is **sufficient to determine the output for ANY input signal** through convolution. This is why $h(t)$ is often called the "complete characterization" of an LTI system.

### 1.3 Step Response: The Cumulative Behavior

The **Step Response** is the output of the system when the input is a unit step function.

$$u(t) \xrightarrow{\text{LTI System}} s(t)$$

The step response is mathematically related to the impulse response through integration (continuous) or summation (discrete):

$$s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau) \, d\tau$$

$$s[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} h[k]$$

Conversely, the impulse response can be obtained by differentiating (continuous) or differencing (discrete) the step response:

$$h(t) = \frac{d s(t)}{dt}$$

$$h[n] = s[n] - s[n-1]$$

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy: The Echo in a Cave

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: Convolution as "Smearing"**
> Imagine you are in a large, empty hall and you clap your hands once sharply (an impulse). The sound you hear afterward is the **impulse response** of the hall — it describes how the hall "remembers" and reverberates the original sharp sound.
>
> Now, if you speak a sentence in that hall (a continuous input signal $x(t)$), the sound you actually hear in the recording is your voice **convolved** with the hall's response. The hall "smears" each tiny sound you make by superimposing many delayed, scaled echoes of it. That superposition is precisely what convolution computes.
>
> If you instead walk through the hall with a steady musical tone (a step input), the way the tone "builds up and settles" in the room is the **step response**.

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics

- **Sampling period $T_s$** in discrete-time systems, typically measured in **seconds (s)**
- **Unit impulse $\delta(t)$** has units of $1/\text{time}$ such that its integral equals **1** (dimensionless)
- **Unit impulse $\delta[n]$** is **dimensionless** and equals 1 at $n=0$ and 0 elsewhere
- **Step response rise time $t_r$** in **seconds (s)**
- **Settling time $t_s$** in **seconds (s)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Convolution Procedure (Graphical Method)

The graphical evaluation of convolution follows four critical steps. Mastering this procedure is **mandatory** for KTU board examinations.

### 2.1.1 Continuous-Time Convolution Steps

**Step 1: Folding (Time Reversal)**
Replace $\tau$ with $-\tau$ in $h(\tau)$ to get $h(-\tau)$. This effectively "mirrors" the impulse response about the vertical axis.

**Step 2: Shifting**
Shift the folded signal by $t$ to obtain $h(t-\tau)$. The parameter $t$ acts as the "sliding knob."

**Step 3: Multiplication**
Multiply $x(\tau)$ and $h(t-\tau)$ point-by-point to obtain the integrand $x(\tau) \cdot h(t-\tau)$.

**Step 4: Integration (Area Under the Product)**
The integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, h(t-\tau) \, d\tau$ gives the value $y(t)$ for the chosen $t$. Slide $t$ from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ to obtain the complete output.

### 2.1.2 Discrete-Time Convolution Steps

The discrete case is similar but uses summation and a discrete shift index $n$:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, h[n-k]$$

For finite-duration signals of lengths $N_1$ and $N_2$, the output has length $N_1 + N_2 - 1$.

## 2.2 Properties of Convolution

The convolution operator satisfies several algebraic properties that simplify system analysis:

| Property | Continuous-Time | Discrete-Time |
|----------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Commutative** | $x(t) * h(t) = h(t) * x(t)$ | $x[n] * h[n] = h[n] * x[n]$ |
| **Associative** | $[x(t) * h_1(t)] * h_2(t) = x(t) * [h_1(t) * h_2(t)]$ | $[x[n] * h_1[n]] * h_2[n] = x[n] * [h_1[n] * h_2[n]]$ |
| **Distributive** | $x(t) * [h_1(t) + h_2(t)] = x(t) * h_1(t) + x(t) * h_2(t)$ | $x[n] * [h_1[n] + h_2[n]] = x[n] * h_1[n] + x[n] * h_2[n]$ |
| **Identity** | $x(t) * \delta(t) = x(t)$ | $x[n] * \delta[n] = x[n]$ |
| **Cascade** | $h_{eq}(t) = h_1(t) * h_2(t)$ | $h_{eq}[n] = h_1[n] * h_2[n]$ |
| **Parallel** | $h_{eq}(t) = h_1(t) + h_2(t)$ | $h_{eq}[n] = h_1[n] + h_2[n]$ |
| **Time Shift** | $x(t-t_0) * h(t) = y(t-t_0)$ | $x[n-n_0] * h[n] = y[n-n_0]$ |

## 2.3 Memory, Causality, and Stability from Impulse Response

The impulse response $h(t)$ (or $h[n]$) reveals critical system properties:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Causality Condition:** A system is causal if and only if $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ (continuous) or $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ (discrete).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Condition (BIBO):** A system is Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable if and only if the impulse response is absolutely integrable (continuous) or absolutely summable (discrete):
> $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$$
> $$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Filters**: FIR (Finite Impulse Response) filters are designed by specifying $h[n]$ directly. IIR filters are designed via recursive structures.
- **Audio Processing**: Convolution with a room's impulse response produces realistic reverberation effects.
- **Image Processing**: 2D convolution is used for blurring, sharpening, and edge detection.
- **Communication Systems**: Channel equalization uses deconvolution to undo the smearing caused by the channel.
- **Control Systems**: Step response is used to characterize rise time, overshoot, and settling time of feedback systems.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table — Memorize for KTU 2024**

| Concept | Continuous-Time Formula | Discrete-Time Formula |
|---------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| **Convolution** | $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) h(t-\tau) d\tau$ | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] h[n-k]$ |
| **Commutative** | $x * h = h * x$ | $x * h = h * x$ |
| **Identity Element** | $\delta(t)$ | $\delta[n]$ |
| **Impulse Response Definition** | $h(t) = T\{\delta(t)\}$ | $h[n] = T\{\delta[n]\}$ |
| **Step Response Definition** | $s(t) = T\{u(t)\}$ | $s[n] = T\{u[n]\}$ |
| **Step to Impulse Relation** | $h(t) = \dfrac{d s(t)}{dt}$ | $h[n] = s[n] - s[n-1]$ |
| **Impulse to Step Relation** | $s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau) d\tau$ | $s[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} h[k]$ |
| **Causality** | $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ | $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ |
| **Stability (BIBO)** | $\int \vert h(t) \vert dt < \infty$ | $\sum \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ |
| **Output Length (finite signals)** | Continuous (infinite resolution) | $N_1 + N_2 - 1$ |
| **LTI Output Formula** | $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ | $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ |
| **Convolution with Impulse** | $x(t) * \delta(t - t_0) = x(t - t_0)$ | $x[n] * \delta[n - n_0] = x[n - n_0]$ |
| **Time-Invariance Property** | $x(t - t_0) * h(t) = y(t - t_0)$ | $x[n - n_0] * h[n] = y[n - n_0]$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Convolution Describes LTI Systems

We will derive the convolution integral from first principles using the **sifting property** of the impulse and the **linearity** and **time-invariance** of the system.

### 3.1.1 Continuous-Time Derivation

**Step 1: Represent $x(t)$ as a Superposition of Impulses**

Any continuous-time signal $x(t)$ can be expressed as an integral of weighted, shifted impulses using the sifting property:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, \delta(t - \tau) \, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **sifting property** of the Dirac delta: the integral "picks out" the value of $x(\tau)$ at $t$.

**Step 2: Apply the LTI System Operator**

Let $T\{\cdot\}$ denote the LTI system operator. Applying it to both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) = T\{x(t)\} &= T\left\{ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, \delta(t - \tau) \, d\tau \right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Invoke Linearity**

By the **linearity** property, the system response to a weighted sum equals the weighted sum of individual responses. Since integration is a continuous sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, T\{\delta(t - \tau)\} \, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Invoke Time-Invariance**

By the **time-invariance** property, the response of the system to a delayed impulse $\delta(t - \tau)$ is simply a delayed version of the impulse response $h(t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T\{\delta(t - \tau)\} &= h(t - \tau)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute to Obtain the Convolution Integral**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, h(t - \tau) \, d\tau \\
y(t) &= x(t) * h(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The convolution integral is **derived**, not assumed. It emerges directly from the combination of linearity, time-invariance, and the sifting property of the Dirac delta.

### 3.1.2 Discrete-Time Derivation

**Step 1: Express $x[n]$ as a Sum of Weighted Impulses**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x[n] &= \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, \delta[n - k]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the LTI Operator**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] = T\{x[n]\} &= T\left\{ \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, \delta[n - k] \right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Linearity and Time-Invariance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, T\{\delta[n - k]\} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, h[n - k]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Discrete-Time Convolution Sum**.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: Continuous-Time Convolution

**Problem:** Compute $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ where

$$
x(t) = e^{-t} u(t) \quad \text{and} \quad h(t) = u(t)
$$

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Write Down the Convolution Integral**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, h(t - \tau) \, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Given Signals**

We have $x(\tau) = e^{-\tau} u(\tau)$ and $h(t - \tau) = u(t - \tau)$. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-\tau} u(\tau) \, u(t - \tau) \, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Determine the Limits of Integration**

The product $u(\tau) \cdot u(t - \tau)$ equals 1 only when $\tau \geq 0$ AND $\tau \leq t$. This requires $t \geq 0$. The valid integration range is $0 \leq \tau \leq t$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \int_{0}^{t} e^{-\tau} \, d\tau \quad \text{(for } t \geq 0\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the Integral**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \left[ -e^{-\tau} \right]_{0}^{t} = -e^{-t} - (-e^{0}) = -e^{-t} + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Express the Final Result with the Unit Step**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= (1 - e^{-t}) \, u(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** At $t = 0$, $y(0) = 0$ (system memory is empty). As $t \to \infty$, $y(t) \to 1$ (steady-state reached). The system is a first-order low-pass filter.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example: Discrete-Time Convolution

**Problem:** Compute $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ where

$$
x[n] = \{1, 2, 3\} \quad \text{(for } n = 0, 1, 2\text{)}
$$

$$
h[n] = \{1, 1, 1\} \quad \text{(for } n = 0, 1, 2\text{)}
$$

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Use the Convolution Sum Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= \sum_{k=0}^{2} x[k] \, h[n - k]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute for Each Value of $n$**

The output length is $3 + 3 - 1 = 5$, so $n$ ranges from $0$ to $4$.

**For $n = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[0] &= x[0] h[0] = (1)(1) = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $n = 1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[1] &= x[0] h[1] + x[1] h[0] = (1)(1) + (2)(1) = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $n = 2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[2] &= x[0] h[2] + x[1] h[1] + x[2] h[0] \\
y[2] &= (1)(1) + (2)(1) + (3)(1) = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $n = 3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[3] &= x[1] h[2] + x[2] h[1] = (2)(1) + (3)(1) = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $n = 4$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[4] &= x[2] h[2] = (3)(1) = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= \{1, 3, 6, 5, 3\} \quad \text{for } n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example: Step Response from Impulse Response

**Problem:** Given the impulse response $h(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$, compute the step response $s(t)$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Use the Integration Relation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau) \, d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{t} e^{-2\tau} u(\tau) \, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Determine the Lower Limit**

Since $u(\tau) = 0$ for $\tau < 0$, the effective lower limit is 0.

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(t) &= \int_{0}^{t} e^{-2\tau} \, d\tau \quad \text{(for } t \geq 0\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the Integral**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(t) &= \left[ \frac{e^{-2\tau}}{-2} \right]_{0}^{t} = -\frac{1}{2} e^{-2t} + \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Final Result**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(t) &= \frac{1}{2} \left(1 - e^{-2t}\right) u(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification by Differentiation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d s(t)}{dt} &= \frac{d}{dt}\left[\frac{1}{2}(1 - e^{-2t})u(t)\right] = \frac{1}{2}(2 e^{-2t}) u(t) + \frac{1}{2}(1 - e^{-2t})\delta(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $1 - e^{-2t} = 0$ at $t = 0$, the impulse term vanishes. Thus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h(t) &= e^{-2t} u(t) \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: LTI System Characterized by Impulse Response

The following block diagram illustrates how an LTI system is completely characterized by its impulse response, and how the convolution operation arises from its input-output relationship.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["INPUT x(t) or x[n]"]:::signalNode
    impGen["IMPULSE GENERATOR delta(t) or delta[n]"]:::impulseNode
    sysBlock["LTI SYSTEM h(t) or h[n]"]:::systemNode
    outputA["OUTPUT y(t) or y[n]"]:::signalNode
    convOp["CONVOLUTION OPERATOR y = x * h"]:::convNode
    stepIn["STEP INPUT u(t) or u[n]"]:::signalNode
    stepOut["STEP RESPONSE s(t) or s[n]"]:::signalNode

    impGen -->|"delta(t)"| sysBlock
    sysBlock -->|"h(t)"| convOp
    inputA -->|"x(t)"| convOp
    convOp -->|"y(t)"| outputA
    stepIn -->|"u(t)"| sysBlock
    sysBlock -->|"s(t)"| stepOut

    classDef signalNode fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0284C7,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef impulseNode fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#D97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef systemNode fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#16A34A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef convNode fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#BE185D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Four-Step Graphical Convolution Procedure

This diagram maps the operational flow of the graphical convolution method (used in KTU board exam problems).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["STEP 1: FOLDING - Reverse h about vertical axis to get h of minus tau"]:::procNode
    step2["STEP 2: SHIFTING - Slide folded signal by t to get h of t minus tau"]:::procNode
    step3["STEP 3: MULTIPLICATION - Multiply x of tau and h of t minus tau point by point"]:::procNode
    step4["STEP 4: INTEGRATION OR SUMMATION - Compute area under product to get y at fixed t"]:::procNode
    step5["SLIDE t FROM minus infinity TO plus infinity - Generate complete output y"]:::procNode

    step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5

    classDef procNode fill:#EDE9FE,stroke:#7C3AED,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Interconnection of LTI Systems

This diagram shows how the impulse responses combine when LTI systems are connected in cascade (series) or in parallel.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputSig["INPUT x(t)"]:::signalNode
    sys1["SYSTEM 1 h1 of t"]:::systemNode
    sys2["SYSTEM 2 h2 of t"]:::systemNode
    cascadeOut["OUTPUT y(t)"]:::signalNode
    parInput["INPUT x(t)"]:::signalNode
    parSys1["SYSTEM A hA of t"]:::systemNode
    parSys2["SYSTEM B hB of t"]:::systemNode
    summer["SUMMER +"]:::sumNode
    parOut["OUTPUT y(t)"]:::signalNode

    inputSig --> sys1 --> sys2 --> cascadeOut

    parInput --> parSys1
    parInput --> parSys2
    parSys1 --> summer
    parSys2 --> summer
    summer --> parOut

    classDef signalNode fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0284C7,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef systemNode fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#16A34A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef sumNode fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#D97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interconnection Rules:**
> - **Cascade Connection:** Equivalent impulse response is the convolution: $h_{eq}(t) = h_1(t) * h_2(t)$
> - **Parallel Connection:** Equivalent impulse response is the sum: $h_{eq}(t) = h_A(t) + h_B(t)$

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Convolution as Memory Operation

This schematic conceptualizes convolution as a sliding weighted-average operation, which is the core intuition behind LTI system behavior.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    past["PAST INPUT VALUES x of tau"]:::pastNode
    weights["WEIGHTS h of t minus tau"]:::weightNode
    memory["SYSTEM MEMORY - stores past values"]:::memoryNode
    sumblock["CONTINUOUS SUMMATION"]:::sumNode
    outputVal["OUTPUT y of t at current time"]:::outputNode

    past --> memory
    weights --> memory
    memory --> sumblock
    sumblock --> outputVal

    classDef pastNode fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#DC2626,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef weightNode fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#2563EB,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef memoryNode fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#059669,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef sumNode fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#D97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef outputNode fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#BE185D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```
