# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]**
**Define the impulse response of an LTI system. Explain how it characterizes the system completely.**

**Model Answer:**

The **impulse response** of a continuous-time LTI system is defined as the output of the system when the input is a unit impulse $\delta(t)$. It is denoted as $h(t)$:

$$h(t) = T\{\delta(t)\}$$

In discrete time, $h[n] = T\{\delta[n]\}$.

The impulse response **completely characterizes** an LTI system because of two fundamental properties:

1. **Linearity:** If the input is a sum of scaled signals, the output is the same sum of scaled responses.
2. **Time-Invariance:** If the input is shifted in time, the output is identically shifted.

Using the **sifting property**, any input $x(t)$ can be written as a continuous superposition of shifted impulses:

$$x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, \delta(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

By linearity and time-invariance, the system response is:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, h(t - \tau) \, d\tau = x(t) * h(t)$$

Thus, knowing $h(t)$ is **sufficient** to determine the output for **any arbitrary input** through convolution. This makes the impulse response the complete "fingerprint" of the LTI system.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]**
**State and explain the BIBO stability condition for an LTI system in terms of its impulse response.**

**Model Answer:**

An LTI system is **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable** if every bounded input produces a bounded output.

**For Continuous-Time LTI Systems:**

The system is BIBO stable if and only if the impulse response is **absolutely integrable**:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$$

**For Discrete-Time LTI Systems:**

The system is BIBO stable if and only if the impulse response is **absolutely summable**:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$$

**Proof Sketch:** If $|x(t)| \leq M$ for all $t$, then:

$$|y(t)| = \left| \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) h(t-\tau) d\tau \right| \leq M \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t-\tau) \vert d\tau = M \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert d\tau$$

The output is bounded if and only if the integral of $|h(t)|$ is finite. A similar argument applies to the discrete case.

**Examples:**
- $h(t) = e^{-at} u(t)$ with $a > 0$: BIBO stable since $\int_0^{\infty} e^{-at} dt = 1/a < \infty$
- $h(t) = u(t)$: NOT BIBO stable since $\int_0^{\infty} 1 \, dt = \infty$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the convolution $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ for the following signals using the graphical method:

$$x(t) = u(t) - u(t-3) \quad \text{(rectangular pulse of width 3 starting at } t=0\text{)}$$

$$h(t) = e^{-t} u(t)$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** For an LTI system with impulse response $h[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$, determine whether the system is causal and BIBO stable. Compute the step response $s[n]$ of the system.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Convolution of Rectangular Pulse with Exponential

**Step 1: Substitute into the Convolution Integral**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, h(t - \tau) \, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

The signal $x(\tau) = u(\tau) - u(\tau - 3)$ is non-zero only for $0 \leq \tau \leq 3$. The signal $h(t - \tau) = e^{-(t-\tau)} u(t - \tau)$ is non-zero only when $\tau \leq t$.

**Step 2: Determine the Integration Range**

The product $x(\tau) h(t-\tau)$ is non-zero when **both** conditions are satisfied: $0 \leq \tau \leq 3$ AND $\tau \leq t$. So the limits are $\tau$ from $0$ to $\min(3, t)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \int_{0}^{\min(3, t)} e^{-(t - \tau)} \, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Case 1 — For $t < 0$**

The integrand is zero everywhere, so $y(t) = 0$.

**Step 4: Case 2 — For $0 \leq t < 3$**

Here $\min(3, t) = t$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \int_{0}^{t} e^{-(t - \tau)} \, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $u = t - \tau$, then $du = -d\tau$. When $\tau = 0$, $u = t$; when $\tau = t$, $u = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \int_{t}^{0} e^{-u} (-du) = \int_{0}^{t} e^{-u} \, du = \left[-e^{-u}\right]_{0}^{t} = 1 - e^{-t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Case 3 — For $t \geq 3$**

Here $\min(3, t) = 3$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \int_{0}^{3} e^{-(t - \tau)} \, d\tau = e^{-t} \int_{0}^{3} e^{\tau} \, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= e^{-t} \left[ e^{\tau} \right]_{0}^{3} = e^{-t} (e^{3} - 1) = (e^{3} - 1) e^{-t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compile the Final Answer**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \begin{cases}
0, & t < 0 \\
1 - e^{-t}, & 0 \leq t < 3 \\
(e^{3} - 1) e^{-t}, & t \geq 3
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** At $t = 0$: $y(0) = 1 - 1 = 0$ ✓ (system starts from rest). At $t = 3$: $y(3^-) = 1 - e^{-3}$ and $y(3^+) = (e^3 - 1)e^{-3} = 1 - e^{-3}$ ✓ (continuous).

---

##### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Causality, Stability, and Step Response

**Step 1: Check Causality**

A discrete-time LTI system is causal if and only if $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$.

Given $h[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$:

- For $n < 0$: $u[n] = 0$, so $h[n] = 0$ ✓
- For $n \geq 0$: $h[n] = (0.5)^n$

**Conclusion:** The system is **causal** because $h[n] = 0$ for all $n < 0$.

**Step 2: Check BIBO Stability**

Compute the absolute sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert &= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5)^n = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5} = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

The geometric series converges because $|0.5| < 1$. The sum equals $2 < \infty$.

**Conclusion:** The system is **BIBO stable**.

**Step 3: Compute the Step Response $s[n]$**

The step response is the system's response to $u[n]$. Using the convolution sum with $x[n] = u[n]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s[n] &= h[n] * u[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \, u[n - k] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} (0.5)^k \quad \text{(for } n \geq 0\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The summation requires $k \geq 0$ AND $k \leq n$, giving $0 \leq k \leq n$.

**Step 4: Evaluate the Geometric Sum**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s[n] &= \sum_{k=0}^{n} (0.5)^k = \frac{1 - (0.5)^{n+1}}{1 - 0.5} = 2 \left[1 - (0.5)^{n+1}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Final Result**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s[n] &= 2 \left[1 - (0.5)^{n+1}\right] u[n]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** As $n \to \infty$, $s[n] \to 2 \cdot 1 = 2$ (steady-state value). At $n = 0$: $s[0] = 2(1 - 0.5) = 1$. Cross-check via $h[0] = (0.5)^0 = 1$ ✓.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the convolution sum $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ where:

$$x[n] = \{2, 1, 2\} \quad \text{(for } n = 0, 1, 2\text{)}$$

$$h[n] = \{1, -1, 1, -1\} \quad \text{(for } n = 0, 1, 2, 3\text{)}$$

Show all steps using the tabular method.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An LTI system has the impulse response $h(t) = e^{-t} u(t) + 2 e^{-2t} u(t)$. Find the step response $s(t)$ of the system and verify the causality and stability.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Discrete Convolution by Tabular Method

**Step 1: Determine Output Length**

The output length is $3 + 4 - 1 = 6$ samples (for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$).

**Step 2: Set Up the Tabular Multiplication (Flip $h[n]$ and Shift)**

We compute $y[n] = \sum_{k} x[k] h[n-k]$. Equivalently, we multiply the **unflipped** $x$ sequence with the **flipped** $h$ sequence and sum diagonally.

| $n \backslash k$ | $x[0]=2$ | $x[1]=1$ | $x[2]=2$ | $h_{\text{flip}}[0]=h[0]=1$ | $h_{\text{flip}}[1]=h[3]=-1$ | $h_{\text{flip}}[2]=h[2]=1$ | $h_{\text{flip}}[3]=h[1]=-1$ | $y[n]$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $2 \cdot 1$ | - | - | - | - | - | - | $2$ |
| $1$ | $2 \cdot (-1)$ | $1 \cdot 1$ | - | - | - | - | - | $-1$ |
| $2$ | $2 \cdot 1$ | $1 \cdot (-1)$ | $2 \cdot 1$ | - | - | - | - | $3$ |
| $3$ | $2 \cdot (-1)$ | $1 \cdot 1$ | $2 \cdot (-1)$ | - | - | - | - | $-3$ |
| $4$ | - | $1 \cdot (-1)$ | $2 \cdot 1$ | - | - | - | - | $1$ |
| $5$ | - | - | $2 \cdot (-1)$ | - | - | - | - | $-2$ |

**Step 3: Compute Each Output Sample**

**For $n = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[0] &= x[0] \cdot h[0] = 2 \cdot 1 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $n = 1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[1] &= x[0] \cdot h[1] + x[1] \cdot h[0] = 2 \cdot (-1) + 1 \cdot 1 = -1
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $n = 2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[2] &= x[0] \cdot h[2] + x[1] \cdot h[1] + x[2] \cdot h[0] \\
y[2] &= 2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot (-1) + 2 \cdot 1 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $n = 3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[3] &= x[1] \cdot h[2] + x[2] \cdot h[1] = 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot (-1) = -1
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $n = 4$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[4] &= x[2] \cdot h[2] + (0) = 2 \cdot 1 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The full table-based computation with $x[0..2]$ and $h[0..3]$ flipped gives indices $n - k$ ranging through $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$. The result is shown below.

**For $n = 5$:** All contributing terms fall outside the defined range, so $y[5] = 0$.

**Step 4: Final Convolution Result**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= \{2, -1, 3, -1, 2, 0\} \quad \text{for } n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
\end{aligned}
$$

---

##### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Step Response and System Properties

**Step 1: Check Causality**

Given $h(t) = e^{-t} u(t) + 2 e^{-2t} u(t)$. Since $u(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, both terms are zero for $t < 0$.

**Conclusion:** The system is **causal**.

**Step 2: Check BIBO Stability**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt &= \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-t} \, dt + 2 \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2t} \, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-t} \, dt &= 1 \\
\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2t} \, dt &= \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt &= 1 + 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 1 + 1 = 2 < \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The system is **BIBO stable**.

**Step 3: Compute the Step Response Using Convolution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(t) &= h(t) * u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau) \, d\tau = \int_{0}^{t} \left[ e^{-\tau} + 2 e^{-2\tau} \right] d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the Integral**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(t) &= \int_{0}^{t} e^{-\tau} \, d\tau + 2 \int_{0}^{t} e^{-2\tau} \, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(t) &= \left[ -e^{-\tau} \right]_{0}^{t} + 2 \left[ \frac{e^{-2\tau}}{-2} \right]_{0}^{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(t) &= (1 - e^{-t}) + (1 - e^{-2t}) = 2 - e^{-t} - e^{-2t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Final Result**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(t) &= \left( 2 - e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \right) u(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification by Differentiation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d s(t)}{dt} &= e^{-t} u(t) + 2 e^{-2t} u(t) + (2 - e^{-t} - e^{-2t}) \delta(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

At $t = 0$, $2 - e^0 - e^0 = 2 - 1 - 1 = 0$, so the impulse term vanishes. Thus $h(t) = e^{-t} u(t) + 2 e^{-2t} u(t)$ ✓.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Integration Limits (3-mark penalty common):** Students often forget to adjust the integration limits based on the support of $u(\tau)$ and $u(t-\tau)$. Always draw the two signals and identify the overlap region for each $t$.
> - **Sign Error in Folding:** When applying the graphical method, the folded signal $h(-\tau)$ is often drawn incorrectly. Always remember that $h(t-\tau)$ shifts $h(-\tau)$ to the right by $t$.
> - **Causality Misconception:** A system is causal if $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, NOT if the output is "well-defined." A non-causal system can still produce well-defined outputs.
> - **Stability Check:** Many students confuse absolute integrability with the integrability of $h(t)$ itself. BIBO stability requires $\int |h(t)| dt < \infty$, not $\int h(t) dt < \infty$.
> - **Step Response Setup:** A common error is computing $s(t) = h(t) \cdot u(t)$ (multiplication) instead of $s(t) = h(t) * u(t)$ (convolution).
> - **Discrete Convolution Indexing:** Off-by-one errors in the convolution index $n - k$ are the #1 source of lost marks. Always verify the range of $k$ and $n$ before summing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1**

- **Convolution Integral (CT):** $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) h(t-\tau) d\tau$
- **Convolution Sum (DT):** $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] h[n-k]$
- **Convolution Origin:** Derives from linearity + time-invariance + sifting property of impulse
- **Graphical Method (4 steps):** Folding $\rightarrow$ Shifting $\rightarrow$ Multiplication $\rightarrow$ Integration
- **Commutative Property:** $x * h = h * x$ — can flip either signal
- **Identity Element:** Convolution with $\delta(t)$ or $\delta[n]$ returns the original signal
- **Cascade Systems:** $h_{eq}(t) = h_1(t) * h_2(t)$ (convolution)
- **Parallel Systems:** $h_{eq}(t) = h_1(t) + h_2(t)$ (summation)
- **Output Length (DT finite):** $N_1 + N_2 - 1$
- **Causality Test:** $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ (CT) or $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ (DT)
- **BIBO Stability:** $\int |h(t)| dt < \infty$ (CT) or $\sum |h[n]| < \infty$ (DT)
- **Step Response Formula:** $s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau) d\tau$ and $s[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} h[k]$
- **Impulse from Step:** $h(t) = ds(t)/dt$ and $h[n] = s[n] - s[n-1]$
- **LTI Output:** $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ — this is the single most important equation in the module
- **Time-Invariance Property:** A delayed input produces a delayed output: $x(t-t_0) * h(t) = y(t-t_0)$
- **Common Integrals:** $\int_0^{\infty} e^{-at} dt = 1/a$ for $a > 0$ — useful for stability tests
- **Geometric Series:** $\sum_{k=0}^{N} r^k = (1 - r^{N+1})/(1 - r)$ for $r \neq 1$ — essential for discrete convolution sums
- **Memory Effect:** Convolution inherently captures system memory by weighting past input values

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fourier Transform: Existence and Properties of Continuous Time Fourier Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET503 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fourier Transform: Existence and Properties of Continuous Time Fourier Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: Continuous Time Fourier Transform (CTFT)

The **Continuous Time Fourier Transform (CTFT)** of an aperiodic, continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is defined as a frequency-domain representation that decomposes $x(t)$ into a continuum of complex exponentials $e^{j\omega t}$ spanning all frequencies $\omega \in \mathbb{R}$.

### Forward Transform (Analysis Equation)

$$
X(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt
$$

### Inverse Transform (Synthesis Equation)

$$
x(t) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{X(\omega)\} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(\omega)\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Convention**: Always express the inverse transform with the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor in front of the integral and the $e^{+j\omega t}$ kernel. The pair is denoted as $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega)$.

The variable $\omega$ has units of **radians per second (rad/s)**, and $X(\omega)$ is generally a **complex-valued** function whose magnitude $\vert X(\omega) \vert$ is the *magnitude spectrum* and whose angle $\angle X(\omega)$ is the *phase spectrum*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Prism of Light"

Imagine shining white light (your time-domain signal $x(t)$) through a glass prism. The prism does not *create* colors — it *separates* the white light into its constituent colors (red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet), each of which corresponds to a single pure frequency.

- The **white light** is analogous to $x(t)$ in the time domain — a complex mixture of many simpler components.
- The **glass prism** is the Fourier Transform operator $\mathcal{F}$.
- The **rainbow spread of colors** is the spectrum $X(\omega)$ — telling you *how much* of each frequency is present.
- **Reassembling the rainbow back into white light** is the Inverse Fourier Transform $\mathcal{F}^{-1}$.

This is precisely why the Fourier Transform is the cornerstone of **spectrum analysis** in communication receivers, audio equalizers, and vibration analysis in mechanical systems.

## 1.3 Existence — The Big Question

Not every signal possesses a Fourier Transform. The integral $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$ may diverge (become infinite) for certain pathological signals. The classical conditions ensuring existence are the **Dirichlet Conditions**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Dirichlet Conditions (Sufficient Conditions for CTFT Existence)**
> 1. **Absolute Integrability**: $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert\, dt < \infty$
> 2. **Finite Extrema**: $x(t)$ has only a *finite number of maxima and minima* in any finite interval.
> 3. **Finite Discontinuities**: $x(t)$ has only a *finite number of discontinuities* in any finite interval.
>
> If all three are satisfied, the CTFT is **guaranteed to exist** and is **uniformly convergent**.

These are **sufficient but not necessary**. Several important signals (e.g., sinusoids, the unit step, periodic signals) fail absolute integrability but still possess a Fourier Transform in the **generalized sense** (using impulses / distributions in the frequency domain).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Students often state "if the signal is absolutely integrable, it has a Fourier Transform." This is correct as a *sufficient* condition but is **not necessary**. Many non-absolutely-integrable signals (like $u(t)$ or $\cos(\omega_0 t)$) have a valid CTFT expressed using Dirac delta functions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Existence of CTFT — Detailed Logic

### Why Dirichlet Conditions Matter
- **Condition 1** (Absolute Integrability): Bounding $\vert e^{-j\omega t} \vert = 1$, we have $\vert \int x(t)e^{-j\omega t}dt \vert \le \int \vert x(t) \vert dt$. So if the latter is finite, the CTFT integral converges absolutely for *every* $\omega$.
- **Conditions 2 & 3** ensure the function is "well-behaved" — no infinite oscillations or pathological behaviors that would cause the integral to fail to converge to a well-defined function. Together with Condition 1, they guarantee a **continuous, bounded** spectrum $X(\omega)$.

### Signals That Violate These Conditions
| Signal | Violation | Resolution via Generalized CTFT |
|---|---|---|
| $u(t)$ (unit step) | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u(t) dt = \infty$ | Uses $\pi\delta(\omega) + \frac{1}{j\omega}$ |
| $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ | Periodic, not absolutely integrable | Uses $\pi[\delta(\omega - \omega_0) + \delta(\omega + \omega_0)]$ |
| Constant $x(t) = 1$ | Not absolutely integrable | Uses $2\pi\delta(\omega)$ |
| $t \cdot u(t)$ (ramp) | Not absolutely integrable | Uses second-order derivatives and distributions |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Properties of CTFT)

Let $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega)$ and $y(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} Y(\omega)$. The properties below are the **most frequently tested** items in KTU university examinations.

| # | Property | Time Domain | Frequency Domain | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linearity** | $a\,x(t) + b\,y(t)$ | $a\,X(\omega) + b\,Y(\omega)$ | Very High |
| 2 | **Time Shifting** | $x(t - t_0)$ | $X(\omega)\,e^{-j\omega t_0}$ | Very High |
| 3 | **Frequency Shifting** | $x(t)\,e^{j\omega_0 t}$ | $X(\omega - \omega_0)$ | Very High |
| 4 | **Time Scaling** | $x(at)$, $a \neq 0$ | $\frac{1}{\vert a \vert}\,X\!\left(\frac{\omega}{a}\right)$ | High |
| 5 | **Time Reversal** | $x(-t)$ | $X(-\omega)$ | High |
| 6 | **Duality** | $X(t)$ | $2\pi\,x(-\omega)$ | Very High |
| 7 | **Time Differentiation** | $\frac{d^{n}x(t)}{dt^{n}}$ | $(j\omega)^{n}\,X(\omega)$ | Very High |
| 8 | **Frequency Differentiation** | $t\,x(t)$ | $j\,\frac{dX(\omega)}{d\omega}$ | High |
| 9 | **Time Integration** | $\int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)d\tau$ | $\frac{X(\omega)}{j\omega} + \pi X(0)\delta(\omega)$ | High |
| 10 | **Convolution in Time** | $x(t) * y(t)$ | $X(\omega)\,Y(\omega)$ | Very High |
| 11 | **Multiplication in Time** | $x(t)\,y(t)$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi}\,X(\omega) * Y(\omega)$ | Very High |
| 12 | **Conjugate Symmetry** (Real $x(t)$) | $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}$ | $X(-\omega) = X^{*}(\omega)$ | Medium |
| 13 | **Parseval's Theorem** | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^{2} dt$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(\omega) \vert^{2} d\omega$ | High |
| 14 | **Modulation** | $x(t)\cos(\omega_0 t)$ | $\frac{1}{2}[X(\omega-\omega_0)+X(\omega+\omega_0)]$ | High |
| 15 | **Real & Even Signal** | $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}, x(t)=x(-t)$ | $X(\omega) \in \mathbb{R}, X(\omega)=X(-\omega)$ | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip**: The Time-Shifting property carries a phase factor of magnitude **1** (i.e., $e^{-j\omega t_0}$), which is why time shifts only modify the *phase spectrum*, not the *magnitude spectrum*. This is a one-mark favorite in KTU exams.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Property | Engineering Application |
|---|---|
| Convolution ↔ Multiplication | Computing LTI system output in frequency domain: $Y(\omega) = H(\omega)X(\omega)$ |
| Modulation | AM radio transmission, OFDM in 4G/5G |
| Time Shifting | Time-delay beamforming in radar and sonar |
| Parseval's Theorem | Energy calculations, signal power estimation |
| Duality | Designing filters in time domain by analogy with frequency domain |
| Differentiation | Solving differential equations using transfer functions $H(\omega)$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time Shifting Property

**Statement**: If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega)$, then $x(t - t_0) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} e^{-j\omega t_0} X(\omega)$.

**Proof**:

By definition of CTFT, for the shifted signal $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$:

$$
Y(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t - t_0)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt
$$

Introduce a change of variable: let $\tau = t - t_0$, so that $t = \tau + t_0$ and $dt = d\tau$. The limits of integration remain $(-\infty, +\infty)$.

$$
Y(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega(\tau + t_0)}\, d\tau
$$

Separate the exponent using $e^{a+b} = e^a \cdot e^b$:

$$
Y(\omega) = e^{-j\omega t_0} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau
$$

The integral in the second factor is, by definition, the CTFT of $x(\tau)$, which is $X(\omega)$. Hence:

$$
Y(\omega) = X(\omega)\, e^{-j\omega t_0}
$$

Therefore, $x(t - t_0) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} e^{-j\omega t_0}\, X(\omega)$. $\blacksquare$

**Physical Insight**: A time shift by $t_0$ introduces a **linear phase term** $-\omega t_0$ in the spectrum. The magnitude spectrum is unchanged: $\vert e^{-j\omega t_0} \vert = 1$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Frequency Shifting (Modulation) Property

**Statement**: If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega)$, then $x(t)\,e^{j\omega_0 t} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega - \omega_0)$.

**Proof**:

Let $y(t) = x(t)\,e^{j\omega_0 t}$. Its CTFT is:

$$
Y(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\,e^{j\omega_0 t}\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt
$$

Combine the exponentials in the exponent:

$$
Y(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j(\omega - \omega_0)t}\, dt
$$

Recognize that the integral $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j(\omega - \omega_0)t}\, dt$ is exactly $X(\omega - \omega_0)$, the CTFT evaluated at frequency $(\omega - \omega_0)$.

$$
Y(\omega) = X(\omega - \omega_0)
$$

Hence, $x(t)\,e^{j\omega_0 t} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega - \omega_0)$. $\blacksquare$

**Modulation Corollary**: Multiplying $x(t)$ by $\cos(\omega_0 t) = \frac{1}{2}(e^{j\omega_0 t} + e^{-j\omega_0 t})$ gives:

$$
\mathcal{F}\{x(t)\cos(\omega_0 t)\} = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[X(\omega - \omega_0) + X(\omega + \omega_0)\bigr]
$$

This is the **foundation of amplitude modulation (AM)** in communication systems.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time Differentiation Property

**Statement**: If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega)$, then $\frac{dx(t)}{dt} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} j\omega X(\omega)$.

**Proof**:

Differentiating the inverse CTFT of $x(t)$ with respect to $t$:

$$
\frac{dx(t)}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left[\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(\omega)\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega\right]
$$

Assuming uniform convergence, we can swap differentiation and integration:

$$
\frac{dx(t)}{dt} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(\omega)\, \frac{d}{dt}\left[e^{j\omega t}\right]\, d\omega
$$

Differentiate the exponential: $\frac{d}{dt}e^{j\omega t} = j\omega\, e^{j\omega t}$.

$$
\frac{dx(t)}{dt} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \bigl[j\omega\, X(\omega)\bigr]\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega
$$

The right-hand side is, by definition, the inverse CTFT of $j\omega X(\omega)$. Hence:

$$
\mathcal{F}\!\left\{\frac{dx(t)}{dt}\right\} = j\omega\, X(\omega)
$$

By induction, this generalizes to the $n$-th derivative:

$$
\mathcal{F}\!\left\{\frac{d^{n} x(t)}{dt^{n}}\right\} = (j\omega)^{n}\, X(\omega)
$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Convolution Property

**Statement**: If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega)$ and $y(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} Y(\omega)$, then $z(t) = x(t) * y(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega)\,Y(\omega)$.

**Proof**:

Start with the CTFT of the convolution $z(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, y(t - \tau)\, d\tau$:

$$
Z(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \left[\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, y(t - \tau)\, d\tau\right] e^{-j\omega t}\, dt
$$

Swap the order of integration (Fubini's theorem, valid for absolutely integrable signals):

$$
Z(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\left[\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} y(t - \tau)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt\right] d\tau
$$

The inner integral, by the Time-Shifting Property, equals $Y(\omega)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}$:

$$
Z(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, Y(\omega)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau
$$

Factor $Y(\omega)$ out (it does not depend on $\tau$):

$$
Z(\omega) = Y(\omega)\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau
$$

The remaining integral is, by definition, $X(\omega)$:

$$
Z(\omega) = X(\omega)\, Y(\omega)
$$

Hence, $x(t) * y(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega)\,Y(\omega)$. $\blacksquare$

**Engineering Significance**: This is the **most important property** in signals and systems. It converts the computationally expensive convolution operation in the time domain into a simple multiplication in the frequency domain — the heart of all digital filtering, modulation, and LTI system analysis.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Duality Property

**Statement**: If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega)$, then $X(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi\, x(-\omega)$.

**Proof**:

Start with the inverse CTFT:

$$
x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(\omega)\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega
$$

Rename the dummy variable of integration from $\omega$ to $-\omega$. When $\omega \to -\omega$, $d\omega \to -d\omega$, and the integration limits reverse but the negative sign cancels, leaving the limits $(-\infty, +\infty)$:

$$
x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(-\omega)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, (-d\omega)
$$

Simplifying the sign:

$$
2\pi\, x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(-\omega)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, d\omega
$$

Now replace the dummy variable $\omega$ with $-\omega$ again (or equivalently, replace $t$ with $-\omega$ as a *dummy* argument) — we interpret $t$ in the integral as a generic variable. Setting the integration variable as $\omega$ and the function argument as $-\omega$:

$$
2\pi\, x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(-\omega)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, d\omega
$$

By the definition of the CTFT, $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(\omega) e^{-j\omega t} d\omega$ is the CTFT of $f(\omega)$ evaluated at $t$. Identifying $f(\omega) = X(-\omega)$:

$$
\mathcal{F}\{X(-\omega)\} \Big|_{\text{at } t} = 2\pi\, x(t)
$$

Switching the roles: the *time-domain function* is $X(t)$, and the *frequency-domain function* is $2\pi\,x(-\omega)$. Formally:

$$
X(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi\, x(-\omega)
$$

$\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Application**: The Duality property is used in KTU problems to derive the CTFT of signals like the sinc function from the rectangular pulse, and vice versa.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Parseval's Theorem (Energy Conservation)

**Statement**: For an energy signal $x(t)$ with CTFT $X(\omega)$:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^{2} dt = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert X(\omega) \vert^{2}\, d\omega
$$

**Proof Outline**:

The energy in the time domain is $E = \int x(t) x^{*}(t) dt$. Substitute the inverse CTFT for $x(t)$ and the complex conjugate of the inverse CTFT for $x^{*}(t)$, then apply Fubini's theorem to swap integrals. After simplification (standard textbook derivation), the cross-terms collapse via the orthogonality of exponentials, yielding the result above. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip**: When asked for the **Energy Spectral Density (ESD)** of a signal, write $S_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{\vert X(\omega) \vert^{2}}{2\pi}$ (in $\omega$-form). The total energy is $E = \int S_{xx}(\omega)\, d\omega$.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Derivation Summary

| Property | Core Algebraic Trick | Frequency Factor Introduced |
|---|---|---|
| Time Shifting | Change of variable $\tau = t - t_0$ | $e^{-j\omega t_0}$ |
| Frequency Shifting | Combine exponents | $X(\omega - \omega_0)$ |
| Differentiation | Differentiate $e^{j\omega t}$ under the integral | $j\omega$ |
| Convolution | Swap integrals, apply time shift | $X(\omega)Y(\omega)$ |
| Duality | Rename dummy variables in inverse CTFT | $2\pi x(-\omega)$ |
| Parseval | Express $x(t) x^{*}(t)$, swap integrals | $\frac{1}{2\pi}\vert X(\omega)\vert^{2}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — CTFT Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["xt time domain signal"] --> B["CTFT operator F"]
    B --> C["Xw frequency spectrum"]
    C --> D["Property applied: Shift Differentiate Convolve Modulate"]
    D --> E["Modified spectrum Yw"]
    E --> F["Inverse CTFT operator F inv"]
    F --> G["yt time domain signal"]
    A -.->|x-t0 time shift| A
    C -.->|j omega differentiation| C
```

## 4.2 Property Interaction Map — CTFT Property Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["CTFT Properties"]
    P --> P1["LINEARITY a x plus b y"]
    P --> P2["SHIFTING Time and Frequency"]
    P --> P3["SCALING Time and Frequency"]
    P --> P4["DERIVATIVES Time and Frequency"]
    P --> P5["CONVOLUTION Time domain becomes Multiplication"]
    P --> P6["MULTIPLICATION Time domain becomes Convolution by 2 pi"]
    P --> P7["DUALITY Xt becomes 2 pi x minus w"]
    P --> P8["PARSEVAL Energy conservation"]
    P2 --> P2a["Time shift e to the minus j w t0"]
    P2 --> P2b["Frequency shift X w minus w0"]
    P3 --> P3a["Time scaling 1 by a X w by a"]
    P3 --> P3b["Time reversal X minus w"]
    P4 --> P4a["Time derivative j w X w"]
    P4 --> P4b["Frequency derivative t x t"]
```

## 4.3 Existence Condition Logic Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Signal xt arrives"]
    S --> Q1{"Absolutely integrable? Integral of absolute xt finite"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> V["Standard CTFT exists Xw is continuous"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Finite number of maxima minima discontinuities?"}
    Q2 -- No --> N["CTFT may not exist"]
    Q2 -- Yes --> G["Generalized CTFT via Dirac deltas allowed"]
    G --> G1["Periodic signals use impulse trains"]
    G --> G2["Unit step uses pi delta w plus 1 over j w"]
    G --> G3["Constant signal uses 2 pi delta w"]
    V --> APP["Apply properties: shift, scale, convolve, modulate"]
    G --> APP
```

## 4.4 Property Pairing Schematic — Duality Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TD["Time Domain x t"] -- CTFT forward --> FD["Frequency Domain X w"]
    FD -- Reinterpret X as x --> TD2["Time Domain X t"]
    TD2 -- CTFT of Xt --> FD2["Frequency Domain 2 pi x minus w"]
    FD2 -.->|equals| TD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams**: Block A represents the input signal, Block B is the analytical engine (CTFT), Block C is the spectrum, Block D is the property application layer (where you multiply by $e^{-j\omega t_0}$, by $j\omega$, or by $Y(\omega)$), Block E is the modified spectrum, Block F is the synthesis engine, and Block G is the recovered time-domain signal. The dashed loop arrows illustrate that properties are reversible — knowing the spectrum of $x(t)$ lets you immediately write the spectrum of its shifted/differentiated/convolution variants.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**State and explain the Dirichlet conditions for the existence of the Fourier Transform of a continuous-time signal.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Dirichlet conditions are *sufficient* conditions guaranteeing the existence of the CTFT of a signal $x(t)$:

1. **Absolute Integrability**: The signal must be absolutely integrable over all time, i.e., $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert\, dt < \infty$. *(1 mark)*
2. **Finite Extrema**: In any finite interval, $x(t)$ must have only a *finite number of maxima and minima*. *(1 mark)*
3. **Finite Discontinuities**: In any finite interval, $x(t)$ must have only a *finite number of discontinuities*, and each discontinuity must be finite. *(1 mark)*

If all three conditions are satisfied, the CTFT integral converges uniformly and $X(\omega)$ is a continuous, bounded function of $\omega$.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**State any three properties of the Continuous Time Fourier Transform with mathematical expressions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Let $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega)$ and $y(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} Y(\omega)$. Three properties are:

1. **Time Shifting** *(1 mark)*:
$$
x(t - t_0) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega)\, e^{-j\omega t_0}
$$

2. **Frequency Shifting** *(1 mark)*:
$$
x(t)\, e^{j\omega_0 t} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega - \omega_0)
$$

3. **Time Differentiation** *(1 mark)*:
$$
\frac{dx(t)}{dt} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} j\omega\, X(\omega)
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Derive the **time-shifting** and **frequency-shifting** properties of the Continuous Time Fourier Transform. Use the convolution property to explain why a time shift introduces only a *phase* change in the spectrum. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Using the **duality** property of the CTFT, derive the Fourier Transform of the signal $x(t) = \frac{1}{\pi t}$ given that $\text{rect}(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2\pi}\right) = \frac{\sin(\omega/2)}{\omega/2}$. Hence, state the Fourier Transform pair for the ideal Hilbert transformer. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Step 1 — Time-Shifting Derivation** *(3 marks)*:

By definition, for $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$:

$$
Y(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t - t_0)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt
$$

Substitute $\tau = t - t_0$:

$$
Y(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega(\tau + t_0)}\, d\tau = e^{-j\omega t_0}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau
$$

$$
\therefore \quad x(t - t_0) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega)\, e^{-j\omega t_0}
$$

**Step 2 — Frequency-Shifting Derivation** *(2 marks)*:

For $y(t) = x(t) e^{j\omega_0 t}$:

$$
Y(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, e^{j\omega_0 t}\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j(\omega - \omega_0)t}\, dt = X(\omega - \omega_0)
$$

**Step 3 — Phase-Only Effect Explanation** *(2 marks)*:

The factor $e^{-j\omega t_0}$ has magnitude $1$ for all $\omega$. Therefore:

$$
\vert Y(\omega) \vert = \vert X(\omega) \vert, \qquad \angle Y(\omega) = \angle X(\omega) - \omega t_0
$$

A time shift leaves the **magnitude spectrum** untouched and adds a **linear phase** $-\omega t_0$ to the phase spectrum. The convolution property confirms this: $x(t - t_0) = x(t) * \delta(t - t_0)$ in the time domain, so $Y(\omega) = X(\omega) \cdot e^{-j\omega t_0}$ — multiplication by a unit-magnitude complex number rotates the spectrum's phase but preserves its magnitude.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Step 1 — Recall the Standard Pair** *(1 mark)*:

We are given:
$$
\text{rect}(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \frac{\sin(\omega/2)}{\omega/2} = \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2\pi}\right)
$$

That is, $\text{rect}(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \text{Sa}(\omega/2)$, where $\text{Sa}(x) = \frac{\sin x}{x}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Duality Property** *(3 marks)*:

The duality property states: if $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega)$, then $X(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi\, x(-\omega)$.

Identifying $x(t) = \text{rect}(t)$ and $X(\omega) = \text{Sa}(\omega/2)$, the duality property gives:

$$
\text{Sa}(t/2) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi\, \text{rect}(-\omega)
$$

Since $\text{rect}(-\omega) = \text{rect}(\omega)$ (the rectangular function is even):

$$
\text{Sa}(t/2) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi\, \text{rect}(\omega)
$$

**Step 3 — Use Time Reversal to Obtain the Required Pair** *(2 marks)*:

We need $\frac{1}{\pi t}$, which is related to $\text{Sa}(t/2)$ up to a constant and variable scaling. Note that $\text{Sa}(t/2) = \frac{\sin(t/2)}{t/2} = \frac{2\sin(t/2)}{t}$.

We can scale and combine properties. The signal $\frac{1}{\pi t}$ has CTFT:

$$
\mathcal{F}\!\left\{\frac{1}{\pi t}\right\} = -j\, \text{sgn}(\omega)
$$

This is a well-known pair (the Hilbert transformer kernel), derivable from the duality result above by invoking the time-reversal and scaling properties, and noting that the inverse-sinc is the rectangular spectrum. *(1 mark)*

**Final Result**:

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{\pi t} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} -j\, \text{sgn}(\omega)}
$$

This is the Fourier Transform of the **ideal Hilbert transformer** impulse response, used to generate 90° phase shifts in single-sideband (SSB) communication systems.

---

### Question B: **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style — Internal Choice]**

**(a)** State and prove the **convolution theorem** for Continuous Time Fourier Transforms. Using this theorem, determine the output $y(t)$ of an LTI system with impulse response $h(t) = e^{-t} u(t)$ when the input is $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** State and prove **Parseval's theorem** for the CTFT. An energy signal has the magnitude spectrum $\vert X(\omega) \vert = \frac{2}{1 + \omega^{2}}$. Calculate the total energy of the signal. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Step 1 — State the Convolution Theorem** *(1 mark)*:

If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega)$ and $h(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} H(\omega)$, then the output of an LTI system is:

$$
y(t) = x(t) * h(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} Y(\omega) = X(\omega)\, H(\omega)
$$

**Step 2 — Proof** *(3 marks)*:

By definition, $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau$. Its CTFT is:

$$
Y(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \left[\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau\right] e^{-j\omega t}\, dt
$$

Swap the order of integration (Fubini):

$$
Y(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\left[\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(t - \tau)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt\right] d\tau
$$

The inner integral equals $H(\omega) e^{-j\omega \tau}$ by the time-shifting property:

$$
Y(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, H(\omega)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau = H(\omega)\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau = X(\omega)\, H(\omega)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the LTI Problem** *(3 marks)*:

Given $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$ and $h(t) = e^{-t} u(t)$.

CTFT of $x(t)$: $\mathcal{F}\{e^{-at} u(t)\} = \frac{1}{a + j\omega}$ for $a > 0$.

$$
X(\omega) = \frac{1}{2 + j\omega}, \qquad H(\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Y(\omega) = X(\omega) H(\omega) = \frac{1}{(2 + j\omega)(1 + j\omega)}
$$

**Partial Fraction Expansion**:

$$
Y(\omega) = \frac{A}{2 + j\omega} + \frac{B}{1 + j\omega}
$$

Solving: $1 = A(1 + j\omega) + B(2 + j\omega)$. Setting $j\omega = -1$: $A = 1$. Setting $j\omega = -2$: $B = -1$.

$$
Y(\omega) = \frac{1}{2 + j\omega} - \frac{1}{1 + j\omega}
$$

Taking the inverse CTFT:

$$
\boxed{y(t) = \left[e^{-2t} - e^{-t}\right] u(t)}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Step 1 — State Parseval's Theorem** *(1 mark)*:

For an energy signal $x(t)$ with CTFT $X(\omega)$:

$$
E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert X(\omega) \vert^{2}\, d\omega
$$

**Step 2 — Proof Sketch** *(2 marks)*:

Express $E = \int x(t) x^{*}(t) dt$. Substitute the inverse CTFT for $x(t)$ and the conjugate form for $x^{*}(t)$:

$$
E = \int x(t) \left[\frac{1}{2\pi}\int X^{*}(\omega) e^{-j\omega t} d\omega\right] dt
$$

Swap the integrals (Fubini):

$$
E = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int X^{*}(\omega)\left[\int x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt\right] d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int X^{*}(\omega) X(\omega) d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int \vert X(\omega) \vert^{2} d\omega
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Energy** *(4 marks)*:

Given $\vert X(\omega) \vert = \frac{2}{1 + \omega^{2}}$, so $\vert X(\omega) \vert^{2} = \frac{4}{(1 + \omega^{2})^{2}}$.

$$
E = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{4}{(1 + \omega^{2})^{2}}\, d\omega
$$

Use the standard integral $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{d\omega}{(1 + \omega^{2})^{2}} = \frac{\pi}{2}$.

$$
E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 4 \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{4}{4} = 1
$$

$$
\boxed{E = 1 \text{ Joule}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks (Signals & Systems – CTFT Properties)**
> 1. **Forgetting the $2\pi$ factor in Duality**: Many students write $X(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} x(-\omega)$, omitting the $2\pi$ scaling. This costs **2 full marks** in Part B derivations.
> 2. **Incorrect sign in the phase of time-shifting**: The factor is $e^{-j\omega t_0}$, not $e^{+j\omega t_0}$. Mixing this up invalidates the entire modulation problem.
> 3. **Not distinguishing "sufficient" vs. "necessary" for Dirichlet conditions**: A 1-mark question in Part A. Saying "absolute integrability is necessary" is **wrong** — it is only *sufficient*.
> 4. **Failing to apply Partial Fractions in convolution problems**: For B(a) type questions, students compute $Y(\omega) = X(\omega)H(\omega)$ correctly but then forget to take the *inverse* CTFT via partial fraction expansion. Marks are split: 3 for forward, 3 for inverse, 1 for final boxed answer.
> 5. **Mixing $\omega$ and $f$ forms**: KTU uses the $\omega$-form. Writing $\frac{1}{f}$ instead of $2\pi \delta(\omega)$ in modulation problems loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Missing the $H(0)\pi\delta(\omega)$ term in integration property**: For $\int x(\tau) d\tau$, the spectrum is $\frac{X(\omega)}{j\omega} + \pi X(0)\delta(\omega)$. Forgetting the impulse term is a **classic 2-mark deduction**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — CTFT Existence & Properties**

### Core Definitions
- CTFT Forward: $X(\omega) = \int x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ ; Inverse: $x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int X(\omega) e^{j\omega t} d\omega$.
- CTFT exists when Dirichlet conditions are satisfied: absolute integrability + finite extrema + finite discontinuities (in any finite interval).
- Absolute integrability is **sufficient but not necessary** — periodic signals, $u(t)$, and constants have *generalized* CTFTs using Dirac impulses.

### Property Quick-Fire
- **Linearity**: $a x + b y \to a X + b Y$
- **Time shift**: $x(t - t_0) \to e^{-j\omega t_0} X(\omega)$ (phase only)
- **Frequency shift**: $x(t) e^{j\omega_0 t} \to X(\omega - \omega_0)$ (used in modulation)
- **Time scaling**: $x(at) \to \frac{1}{\vert a \vert} X(\omega/a)$ (compression in time ↔ expansion in frequency)
- **Time reversal**: $x(-t) \to X(-\omega)$
- **Duality**: $X(t) \to 2\pi x(-\omega)$ — don't forget the $2\pi$!
- **Time derivative**: $\frac{d^n x}{dt^n} \to (j\omega)^n X(\omega)$
- **Frequency derivative**: $t x(t) \to j \frac{dX}{d\omega}$
- **Convolution ↔ Multiplication**: $x * h \to X(\omega) H(\omega)$ (LTI systems in frequency domain)
- **Multiplication ↔ Convolution**: $x(t) y(t) \to \frac{1}{2\pi} X(\omega) * Y(\omega)$
- **Parseval**: $\int \vert x(t)\vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int \vert X(\omega)\vert^2 d\omega$
- **Modulation**: $x(t) \cos(\omega_0 t) \to \frac{1}{2}[X(\omega - \omega_0) + X(\omega + \omega_0)]$

### Standard CTFT Pairs to Memorize
- $\delta(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 1$
- $1 \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi \delta(\omega)$
- $e^{j\omega_0 t} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi \delta(\omega - \omega_0)$
- $\cos(\omega_0 t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \pi[\delta(\omega - \omega_0) + \delta(\omega + \omega_0)]$
- $u(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \pi \delta(\omega) + \frac{1}{j\omega}$
- $e^{-at} u(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \frac{1}{a + j\omega}$ for $a > 0$
- $\text{rect}(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \text{Sa}(\omega/2)$
- $\text{sgn}(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \frac{2}{j\omega}$
- $\frac{1}{\pi t} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} -j\, \text{sgn}(\omega)$

### Common Exam Patterns
1. **3-Mark Questions**: State 3 properties, list Dirichlet conditions, write CTFT of a standard signal.
2. **7-Mark Sub-parts**: Derive one property, apply Parseval's theorem, solve an LTI system using convolution property.
3. **Most-tested properties**: Time shift, frequency shift, convolution, duality, Parseval.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip**: In every derivation, **explicitly state the property you are about to prove**, then show the integral step-by-step, and **box the final result**. Examiners award partial marks generously when the structure is clear, but deduct heavily for missing steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Frequency Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET503 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Frequency Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequency Response** of a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system is the steady-state response of the system to a sinusoidal input of unit amplitude as a function of frequency $\omega$. Mathematically, it is defined as the **Fourier Transform of the impulse response $h(t)$**, equivalently obtained from the transfer function $H(s)$ by substituting $s = j\omega$ (i.e., evaluating the transfer function on the imaginary axis of the complex s-plane).

For a general LTI system, the frequency response is a **complex-valued function** of the real frequency variable $\omega$:

$$H(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(t)\,e^{-j\omega t}\,dt$$

This complex quantity can be decomposed into two physically meaningful parts:

$$H(j\omega) = \vert H(j\omega) \vert \, e^{j\angle H(j\omega)}$$

- **Magnitude Response** $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ : Ratio of output amplitude to input amplitude at frequency $\omega$.
- **Phase Response** $\angle H(j\omega)$ : Phase shift introduced by the system between output and input sinusoids at frequency $\omega$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The frequency response concept is the cornerstone of analyzing filters, control systems, and communication channels. Every Bode plot, polar plot, and filter design problem in your syllabus relies on the framework built around $H(j\omega)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **acoustic equalizer** in a music studio. The mixing console has multiple knobs—bass, mid, treble. Each knob controls *how loudly* the system reproduces a particular band of sound frequencies. The bass knob amplifies low frequencies, the treble knob amplifies high frequencies, and the mid knob controls middle frequencies. If you plot "amplification" vs "frequency," you get a curve—**that curve is precisely the magnitude response of the equalizer (a filter, which is an LTI system)**.

**Key Intuitive Insights:**

- A frequency response tells you *exactly* what the system does to **every possible frequency**—not just one.
- If you know the response to a single sinusoid $A\cos(\omega_0 t)$, you essentially know the response to *any* signal, because any signal can be written as a sum of sinusoids (Fourier's theorem).
- A system can **amplify** certain frequencies, **attenuate** others, and **delay** some more than others.
- The behavior of an LTI system in the **frequency domain** is fully described by one complex function $H(j\omega)$.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Reality:** The frequency response is *measured* directly in practice using a **Network Analyzer** or **Spectrum Analyzer**, which sweeps a sinusoidal input across frequencies and records the output's amplitude and phase.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **standard metrics** are universally used to characterize the frequency response:

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Magnitude | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ | dimensionless (ratio) | Amplification factor |
| Magnitude in dB | $20\log_{10}\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ | decibels (dB) | Logarithmic gain |
| Phase | $\angle H(j\omega)$ | radians or degrees | Time delay equivalent |
| Group Delay | $\tau_g = -\frac{d\angle H(j\omega)}{d\omega}$ | seconds (s) | Signal distortion indicator |
| Cut-off Frequency | $\omega_c$ | rad/s | −3 dB point |
| Bandwidth | $BW$ | rad/s or Hz | Frequency range of operation |
| Resonant Frequency | $\omega_r$ | rad/s | Peak in $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ |
| Quality Factor | $Q$ | dimensionless | Selectivity measure |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Framework — How Frequency Response Arises

The concept of frequency response is a direct consequence of three foundational pillars of LTI system theory:

**Step 1 — Eigenfunction Property of LTI Systems**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A complex exponential $e^{j\omega t}$ is an **eigenfunction** of every LTI system. When such a signal is applied, the output is the *same* exponential scaled by a complex number $H(j\omega)$.

For input $x(t) = e^{j\omega t}$ and impulse response $h(t)$:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau)\,x(t-\tau)\,d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau)\,e^{j\omega(t-\tau)}\,d\tau$$

Separating the time-dependent factor:

$$y(t) = e^{j\omega t} \underbrace{\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau)\,e^{-j\omega\tau}\,d\tau}_{H(j\omega)}$$

$$y(t) = H(j\omega)\cdot e^{j\omega t}$$

The complex scalar $H(j\omega)$ is **independent of $t$**—it depends only on $\omega$.

**Step 2 — Response to Real Sinusoids**
A real cosine input $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ produces a real sinusoidal output:

$$y(t) = A\,\vert H(j\omega_0)\vert\,\cos\bigl(\omega_0 t + \phi + \angle H(j\omega_0)\bigr)$$

**Interpretation:**
- Output amplitude = $A \cdot \vert H(j\omega_0)\vert$
- Output phase = $\phi + \angle H(j\omega_0)$

**Step 3 — Response to Arbitrary Signals (Fourier's Theorem)**
By the principle of superposition (valid only for LTI systems), if input is a sum of complex exponentials:

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(j\omega)\,e^{j\omega t}\,d\omega$$

Then the output is simply the same integral with each component scaled by $H(j\omega)$:

$$y(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} H(j\omega)\,X(j\omega)\,e^{j\omega t}\,d\omega$$

This is the celebrated convolution theorem: $Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega)\,X(j\omega)$.

### 2.2 Mathematical Pathways to Compute $H(j\omega)$

There are **three equivalent routes** to obtain the frequency response:

**Route 1 — From Transfer Function $H(s)$:**
For any system with transfer function $H(s)$, evaluate on the imaginary axis:

$$H(j\omega) = H(s)\big\vert_{s = j\omega}$$

This is valid **only if the system is stable** (all poles of $H(s)$ lie strictly in the left-half s-plane). The frequency response is then the **boundary value** of the transfer function.

**Route 2 — From Impulse Response $h(t)$:**
Directly via the Fourier transform:

$$H(j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{h(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(t)\,e^{-j\omega t}\,dt$$

**Route 3 — From Differential Equation:**
Given $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \frac{d^k y(t)}{dt^k} = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \frac{d^k x(t)}{dt^k}$, the transfer function is:

$$H(s) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k s^k}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k s^k} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad H(j\omega) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k (j\omega)^k}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k (j\omega)^k}$$

### 2.3 Graphical Representations of Frequency Response

KTU examiners frequently test knowledge of these four standard plots:

1. **Bode Plot** — Two semi-log plots: $\vert H(j\omega) \vert_{dB}$ vs $\log_{10}\omega$ and $\angle H(j\omega)$ vs $\log_{10}\omega$.
2. **Polar Plot (Nyquist Plot)** — A single plot of $\text{Im}\{H(j\omega)\}$ vs $\text{Re}\{H(j\omega)\}$ as $\omega$ sweeps from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$.
3. **Log-Magnitude vs Phase Plot (Nichols Chart)** — $\vert H(j\omega) \vert_{dB}$ vs $\angle H(j\omega)$.
4. **Magnitude & Phase vs Frequency (Linear Scale)** — Most direct visualization.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** required to solve Module 2 frequency response problems. The vertical bar `$\vert$` is escaped to `$\mid$` or `\vert` for safety.

| **#** | **Formula / Relation** | **Expression** | **Application / Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Frequency Response from Impulse Response | $H(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(t)\,e^{-j\omega t}\,dt$ | Definition via Fourier transform |
| 2 | Frequency Response from Transfer Function | $H(j\omega) = H(s)\vert_{s=j\omega}$ | For LTI systems with rational $H(s)$ |
| 3 | Magnitude in dB | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\bigl(\mid H(j\omega) \mid\bigr)$ | Bode magnitude plot |
| 4 | Phase | $\angle H(j\omega) = \tan^{-1}\!\Bigl(\dfrac{\text{Im}\{H(j\omega)\}}{\text{Re}\{H(j\omega)\}}\Bigr)$ | Phase plot computation |
| 5 | Output to Sinusoid | $y(t) = A\,\mid H(j\omega_0)\mid\,\cos\!\bigl(\omega_0 t + \phi + \angle H(j\omega_0)\bigr)$ | Steady-state sinusoidal response |
| 6 | Convolution Theorem | $Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega)\cdot X(j\omega)$ | Input–output relation in frequency domain |
| 7 | Group Delay | $\tau_g(\omega) = -\dfrac{d\,\angle H(j\omega)}{d\omega}$ | Signal envelope delay |
| 8 | Power Transfer | $\mid H(j\omega)\mid^2$ | Power gain of system |
| 9 | RC Low-Pass $H(j\omega)$ | $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$ | Standard 1st-order low-pass filter |
| 10 | RC High-Pass $H(j\omega)$ | $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$ | Standard 1st-order high-pass filter |
| 11 | RLC Band-Pass $\mid H(j\omega)\mid$ | $\mid H(j\omega)\mid = \dfrac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{(1-\omega^2 LC)^2 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ | Series RLC analysis |
| 12 | Resonant Frequency | $\omega_r = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | 2nd-order band-pass/band-stop systems |
| 13 | Quality Factor | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_r}{BW} = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Selectivity of resonant circuit |
| 14 | −3 dB Cutoff | $\mid H(j\omega_c)\mid = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \mid H(0)\mid$ or $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\mid H(j\infty)\mid$ | Defines filter bandwidth |
| 15 | Ideal Low-Pass Filter | $\mid H(j\omega)\mid = 1$ for $\mid\omega\mid \leq \omega_c$, else $0$ | Theoretical benchmark filter |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter to a Kerala B.Tech engineer?** Frequency response is the language spoken by:
> - **Audio engineers** — designing equalizers, crossovers, and noise-cancelling systems.
> - **Telecommunications engineers** — channel equalization and modulation/demodulation.
> - **Control systems engineers** — stability analysis via Bode and Nyquist criteria.
> - **Biomedical engineers** — ECG/EEG signal filtering and pacemakers.
> - **VLSI designers** — frequency compensation in operational amplifiers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Frequency Response from the Sinusoidal Steady-State

**Problem Setup:** Consider a stable LTI system with impulse response $h(t)$ and transfer function $H(s)$. Apply a complex exponential input $x(t) = e^{j\omega_0 t}$ and derive the output.

**Step 1 — Write the convolution integral** for the output:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau)\,x(t-\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** $x(t-\tau) = e^{j\omega_0(t-\tau)}$:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau)\,e^{j\omega_0(t-\tau)}\,d\tau$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the time-dependent term** $e^{j\omega_0 t}$, which does not depend on the integration variable $\tau$:

$$y(t) = e^{j\omega_0 t}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau)\,e^{-j\omega_0 \tau}\,d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the integral** as the Fourier transform of $h(t)$ evaluated at $\omega = \omega_0$:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau)\,e^{-j\omega_0 \tau}\,d\tau = H(j\omega_0)$$

**Step 5 — Conclude** the output form:

$$y(t) = H(j\omega_0)\cdot e^{j\omega_0 t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The complex exponential *passes through* the LTI system unchanged in shape; only its complex amplitude is scaled by $H(j\omega_0)$. This is the celebrated **eigenfunction property**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Response to a Real Cosine

**Problem Setup:** Apply $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ to an LTI system.

**Step 1 — Use Euler's formula** to express the cosine as a sum of complex exponentials:

$$x(t) = \frac{A}{2}\,e^{j(\omega_0 t + \phi)} + \frac{A}{2}\,e^{-j(\omega_0 t + \phi)}$$

$$x(t) = \frac{A}{2}\,e^{j\phi}\,e^{j\omega_0 t} + \frac{A}{2}\,e^{-j\phi}\,e^{-j\omega_0 t}$$

**Step 2 — Apply linearity.** The output is the superposition of responses to each exponential:

$$y(t) = \frac{A}{2}\,e^{j\phi}\,H(j\omega_0)\,e^{j\omega_0 t} + \frac{A}{2}\,e^{-j\phi}\,H(-j\omega_0)\,e^{-j\omega_0 t}$$

**Step 3 — Use the conjugate symmetry property** $H(-j\omega) = H^*(j\omega)$ (valid for real impulse responses):

$$y(t) = \frac{A}{2}\,e^{j\phi}\,H(j\omega_0)\,e^{j\omega_0 t} + \frac{A}{2}\,e^{-j\phi}\,H^*(j\omega_0)\,e^{-j\omega_0 t}$$

**Step 4 — Express $H(j\omega_0)$ in polar form** as $\mid H(j\omega_0)\mid e^{j\angle H(j\omega_0)}$:

$$y(t) = \frac{A}{2}\,\mid H(j\omega_0)\mid\left[e^{j(\omega_0 t + \phi + \angle H(j\omega_0))} + e^{-j(\omega_0 t + \phi + \angle H(j\omega_0))}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Apply Euler's identity** to combine into a real cosine:

$$y(t) = A\,\mid H(j\omega_0)\mid\,\cos\!\bigl(\omega_0 t + \phi + \angle H(j\omega_0)\bigr)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** The output is a sinusoid of the **same frequency $\omega_0$**, with **amplitude scaled by $\mid H(j\omega_0)\mid$** and **phase shifted by $\angle H(j\omega_0)$**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Magnitude and Phase of the RC Low-Pass Filter

**System:** Series RC circuit with input voltage $x(t)$ across the source and output voltage $y(t)$ across the capacitor.

**Transfer Function Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Apply the voltage divider rule** in the s-domain:

$$H(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{1/sC}{R + 1/sC} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** $s = j\omega$ to obtain the frequency response:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 3 — Rationalize** by multiplying numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}\cdot\frac{1 - j\omega RC}{1 - j\omega RC}$$

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1 - j\omega RC}{1 + (\omega RC)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Separate real and imaginary parts:**

$$\text{Re}\{H(j\omega)\} = \frac{1}{1 + (\omega RC)^2}$$

$$\text{Im}\{H(j\omega)\} = \frac{-\omega RC}{1 + (\omega RC)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute magnitude** using $\mid H \mid = \sqrt{\text{Re}^2 + \text{Im}^2}$:

$$\mid H(j\omega)\mid = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}{1 + (\omega RC)^2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute phase** using $\angle H = \tan^{-1}(\text{Im}/\text{Re})$:

$$\angle H(j\omega) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-\omega RC}{1}\right) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

**Step 7 — Identify the −3 dB cutoff frequency** by setting $\mid H(j\omega_c)\mid = 1/\sqrt{2}$:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}$$

**Final Bode Plot Behavior:**
- For $\omega \ll \omega_c$: $\mid H\mid_{dB} \to 0$ dB (passband), phase $\to 0°$.
- For $\omega = \omega_c$: $\mid H\mid_{dB} = -3$ dB, phase $= -45°$.
- For $\omega \gg \omega_c$: $\mid H\mid_{dB} \to -20\log_{10}(\omega RC)$ dB (rolls off at **−20 dB/decade**), phase $\to -90°$.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Series RLC Band-Pass Filter Frequency Response

**System:** Series RLC circuit with output taken across the resistor.

**Step 1 — Transfer function** in s-domain (output = voltage across R):

$$H(s) = \frac{R}{R + sL + 1/sC} = \frac{sRC}{s^2 LC + sRC + 1}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** $s = j\omega$:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 - \omega^2 LC + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 3 — Compute magnitude squared** $\mid H(j\omega)\mid^2 = H(j\omega)\cdot H^*(j\omega)$:

$$\mid H(j\omega)\mid^2 = \frac{(\omega RC)^2}{(1 - \omega^2 LC)^2 + (\omega RC)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Resonant frequency** occurs when denominator is minimum, i.e., when $(1 - \omega_r^2 LC) = 0$:

$$\omega_r = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

At resonance, $\mid H(j\omega_r)\mid = 1$ (maximum).

**Step 5 — Quality factor** is defined as $Q = \omega_r L / R = 1/(\omega_r RC)$:

$$Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

**Step 6 — Bandwidth** $BW = \omega_r / Q = R/L$:

$$BW = \frac{R}{L} \text{ rad/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** At frequencies far from $\omega_r$, the magnitude rolls off. Higher $Q$ gives a narrower, sharper peak — the circuit is *more selective* in the frequency domain.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** For a system with transfer function $H(s) = \dfrac{10}{s^2 + 3s + 2}$, find the magnitude and phase at $\omega = 1$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Substitute** $s = j\omega = j(1) = j$:

$$H(j1) = \frac{10}{(j)^2 + 3(j) + 2} = \frac{10}{-1 + 3j + 2} = \frac{10}{1 + 3j}$$

**Step 2 — Rationalize:**

$$H(j1) = \frac{10}{1 + 3j}\cdot\frac{1 - 3j}{1 - 3j} = \frac{10(1 - 3j)}{1 + 9} = \frac{10(1 - 3j)}{10} = 1 - 3j$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude:**

$$\mid H(j1)\mid = \sqrt{1^2 + (-3)^2} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$$

**Step 4 — Phase:**

$$\angle H(j1) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-3}{1}\right) = -71.57°$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude in dB:**

$$\mid H(j1)\mid_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(\sqrt{10}) = 10\log_{10}(10) = 10 \text{ dB}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Answer in sinusoidal form:** If input is $x(t) = 5\cos(t + 30°)$, then $y(t) = 5\sqrt{10}\cos(t + 30° - 71.57°) \approx 15.81\cos(t - 41.57°)$.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Time Domain to Frequency Domain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Signal x(t)"] --> B["LTI System with h(t)"]
    B --> C["Output Signal y(t)"]
    A2["Fourier Transform"] --> A
    C --> C2["Fourier Transform"]
    A2 -.->|"X(jω)"| D["Frequency Domain Multiplication"]
    C2 -.->|"Y(jω)"| D
    B -->|"H(jω) = F{h(t)}"| D
    D --> E["Y(jω) = H(jω) X(jω)"]
    E --> F["Magnitude & Phase Plots"]
    F --> F1["Bode Plot"]
    F --> F2["Polar / Nyquist Plot"]
    F --> F3["Log-Mag vs Phase"]
```

### 4.2 Frequency Response Computation Routes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["System Defined As"] --> R1["Impulse Response h(t)"]
    Start --> R2["Transfer Function H(s)"]
    Start --> R3["Differential Equation"]
    R1 -->|"Fourier Transform"| P1["H(jω) = ∫ h(t) e^(-jωt) dt"]
    R2 -->|"Substitute s = jω"| P2["H(jω) = H(jω) directly"]
    R3 -->|"Form H(s), then substitute"| P2
    P1 --> Out["Complex Function H(jω)"]
    P2 --> Out
    Out --> M["Magnitude |H(jω)|"]
    Out --> Ph["Phase ∠H(jω)"]
    M --> B1["Bode Magnitude Plot in dB"]
    Ph --> B2["Bode Phase Plot in Degrees"]
    M --> Pol["Polar Plot |H|e^(j∠H)"]
    Ph --> Pol
```

### 4.3 Standard Filter Magnitude Response Sketches (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LP["LOW-PASS FILTER"]
        LPshape["|H(jω)| = 1 for ω<ωc, decays for ω>ωc"]
    end
    subgraph HP["HIGH-PASS FILTER"]
        HPshape["|H(jω)| = 0 for ω<ωc, ≈ 1 for ω>ωc"]
    end
    subgraph BP["BAND-PASS FILTER"]
        BPshape["|H(jω)| peaks at ωr, falls on both sides"]
    end
    subgraph BS["BAND-STOP / NOTCH FILTER"]
        BSshape["|H(jω)| ≈ 1 except notch at ωr"]
    end
    LP --> S1["ωc = 1/RC"]
    HP --> S2["ωc = 1/RC"]
    BP --> S3["ωr = 1/√LC, Q = 1/R√(L/C)"]
    BS --> S4["Twin-T or RLC parallel network"]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Sinusoidal Steady-State Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input["x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ)"] --> Sys["LTI System H(jω)"]
    Sys --> Output["y(t) = A|H(jω)| cos(ωt + φ + ∠H(jω))"]
    Input -.->|"Amplitude A"| Note1["Amplitude scaled by |H(jω)|"]
    Input -.->|"Phase φ"| Note2["Phase shifted by ∠H(jω)"]
    Note1 --> Output
    Note2 --> Output
    Sys -.->|"Characterized by"| Mag["Magnitude |H(jω)|"]
    Sys -.->|"Characterized by"| Phs["Phase ∠H(jω)"]
```

### 4.5 Information Flow: Eigenfunction Property of LTI Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Complex Exponential e^(jω₀t)"] -->|"Input to LTI system"| B["Convolution with h(t)"]
    B -->|"y(t) = ∫ h(τ) e^(jω₀(t-τ)) dτ"| C["Factor out e^(jω₀t)"]
    C --> D["y(t) = e^(jω₀t) · ∫ h(τ) e^(-jω₀τ) dτ"]
    D --> E["Define H(jω₀) = Fourier Transform of h(t)"]
    E --> F["y(t) = H(jω₀) · e^(jω₀t)"]
    F --> G["Eigenvalue: H(jω₀) is a complex scalar"]
    F --> H["Eigenfunction: e^(jω₀t) is unchanged in form"]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the frequency response of an LTI system. Why is it significant in signal processing?

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The frequency response $H(j\omega)$ of an LTI system is the Fourier transform of its impulse response $h(t)$, representing the system's steady-state response (magnitude and phase) to a sinusoidal input of unit amplitude as a function of frequency $\omega$.
>
> **Significance:**
> 1. It characterizes how the system modifies **each frequency component** of an input signal.
> 2. It allows analysis using the **convolution theorem**: $Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega)X(j\omega)$.
> 3. It enables **filter design** (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, band-stop).
> 4. It is the basis for **Bode, Nyquist, and Nichols plots** used in stability analysis.
> 5. It directly links the system's time-domain behavior $h(t)$ to its frequency-domain behavior $H(j\omega)$ via the Fourier transform.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Distinguish between magnitude response and phase response of an LTI system. What is the physical meaning of the phase response?

**Model Answer:**

> | **Aspect** | **Magnitude Response** | **Phase Response** |
> |---|---|---|
> | Symbol | $\mid H(j\omega)\mid$ | $\angle H(j\omega)$ |
> | Definition | Ratio of output to input amplitude | Phase shift between output and input |
> | Unit | Dimensionless (or dB) | Radians or degrees |
> | Plot Type | Bode magnitude plot | Bode phase plot |
>
> **Physical meaning of phase response:** The phase response indicates the **time delay** each frequency component experiences while passing through the system. A linear phase response $\angle H(j\omega) = -\omega t_0$ corresponds to a **pure time delay** of $t_0$ seconds (no signal distortion). A **nonlinear** phase response causes different frequencies to be delayed by different amounts, resulting in **phase distortion** of the signal (e.g., a pulse becomes spread out and ringing appears).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students write $H(j\omega) = H(s)$ without substituting $s = j\omega$ explicitly — losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **radians/second** $\omega$ with **Hertz** $f$ ($f = \omega / 2\pi$).
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to take the **conjugate** when computing magnitude squared, leading to sign errors.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Reporting the phase in **radians** when the question demands **degrees** (or vice versa) — deduct 1 mark.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Skipping the step of **rationalizing** the complex fraction; magnitude computation becomes incorrect.
> 6. **Common Mistake #6:** For Bode plot sketches, students forget to mark the **−3 dB point** and the **asymptotic slopes** (±20 dB/decade per pole/zero).
> 7. **Common Mistake #7:** Confusing **group delay** with **phase delay** in filter design questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

> Consider an LTI system governed by the differential equation:
>
> $$\frac{d^2 y(t)}{dt^2} + 5\,\frac{dy(t)}{dt} + 6\,y(t) = x(t)$$
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Find the frequency response $H(j\omega)$ of the system. Compute the magnitude and phase at $\omega = 1$ rad/s and $\omega = 5$ rad/s.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Determine the steady-state response $y(t)$ of the system to the input $x(t) = 3\cos(2t + 45°)$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Find the transfer function** $H(s)$ by taking the Laplace transform of the differential equation (assuming zero initial conditions):

$$s^2 Y(s) + 5s Y(s) + 6 Y(s) = X(s)$$

$$H(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 5s + 6}$$

**Step 2 — Factor the denominator:**

$$H(s) = \frac{1}{(s+2)(s+3)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** $s = j\omega$:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{(j\omega + 2)(j\omega + 3)} = \frac{1}{(2 + j\omega)(3 + j\omega)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute magnitude:**

$$\mid H(j\omega)\mid = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4 + \omega^2}\cdot\sqrt{9 + \omega^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute phase:**

$$\angle H(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{3}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate at** $\omega = 1$ rad/s:

$$\mid H(j1)\mid = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4+1}\cdot\sqrt{9+1}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}\cdot\sqrt{10}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{50}} = \frac{1}{7.071} \approx 0.1414$$

$$\angle H(j1) = -\tan^{-1}(0.5) - \tan^{-1}(1/3) = -26.57° - 18.43° = -45.00°$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate at** $\omega = 5$ rad/s:

$$\mid H(j5)\mid = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4+25}\cdot\sqrt{9+25}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{29}\cdot\sqrt{34}} = \frac{1}{9.849 \cdot 5.831} = \frac{1}{57.43} \approx 0.01741$$

$$\angle H(j5) = -\tan^{-1}(2.5) - \tan^{-1}(5/3) = -68.20° - 59.04° = -127.24°$$

> **Answer (a):** $\mid H(j1)\mid \approx 0.1414$, $\angle H(j1) = -45°$; $\mid H(j5)\mid \approx 0.0174$, $\angle H(j5) = -127.24°$.

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Input frequency is** $\omega_0 = 2$ rad/s, amplitude $A = 3$, phase $\phi = 45°$.

**Step 2 — Compute** $H(j2)$:

$$H(j2) = \frac{1}{(2 + j2)(3 + j2)} = \frac{1}{(6 - 4) + j(4 + 6)} = \frac{1}{2 + j10}$$

**Step 3 — Rationalize:**

$$H(j2) = \frac{1}{2 + j10}\cdot\frac{2 - j10}{2 - j10} = \frac{2 - j10}{4 + 100} = \frac{2 - j10}{104} = 0.01923 - j0.0962$$

**Step 4 — Compute magnitude and phase:**

$$\mid H(j2)\mid = \sqrt{0.01923^2 + 0.0962^2} = \sqrt{0.000370 + 0.009254} = \sqrt{0.009624} \approx 0.0981$$

$$\angle H(j2) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-0.0962}{0.01923}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-5) \approx -78.69°$$

**Step 5 — Compute steady-state output** using the sinusoidal response formula:

$$y(t) = A\,\mid H(j\omega_0)\mid\,\cos\!\bigl(\omega_0 t + \phi + \angle H(j\omega_0)\bigr)$$

$$y(t) = 3 \times 0.0981 \times \cos(2t + 45° - 78.69°)$$

$$y(t) = 0.2943\,\cos(2t - 33.69°)$$

> **Answer (b):** $y(t) \approx 0.294\,\cos(2t - 33.69°)$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

> An RC low-pass filter consists of a resistor $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and a capacitor $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$.
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the frequency response $H(j\omega)$ of this filter. Sketch the asymptotic Bode magnitude plot and identify the cutoff frequency.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Find the output when the input is $x(t) = 2\cos(1000\,t) + \cos(5000\,t)$. Comment on the filtering action.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Transfer function** of the RC low-pass filter (output across capacitor):

$$H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** $s = j\omega$:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude:**

$$\mid H(j\omega)\mid = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude in dB:**

$$\mid H(j\omega)\mid_{dB} = -10\log_{10}\bigl(1 + (\omega RC)^2\bigr)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the time constant** $\tau = RC = 10{,}000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-3}$ s.

**Step 6 — Cutoff frequency:**

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} = \frac{1}{10^{-3}} = 1000 \text{ rad/s}$$

Equivalently, $f_c = \dfrac{1000}{2\pi} \approx 159.15$ Hz.

**Step 7 — Asymptotic Bode Magnitude Plot Behavior:**

| **Frequency Range** | **Asymptote** | **Slope** |
|---|---|---|
| $\omega \ll 1000$ rad/s | $\mid H\mid_{dB} \approx 0$ dB | 0 dB/decade (flat) |
| $\omega = 1000$ rad/s | $\mid H\mid_{dB} = -3$ dB | Corner point |
| $\omega \gg 1000$ rad/s | $\mid H\mid_{dB} \approx -20\log_{10}(\omega RC)$ | −20 dB/decade |

**Bode Magnitude Sketch (Asymptotic):**

```
|H(jω)|_dB
   0 ────────────●─────
                  \  ← -20 dB/decade slope
  -3               \
                    \___
  -20                     \____
  -40                           \________
        10²   10³   10⁴   10⁵    ω (rad/s)
              ↑
          ωc = 1000 rad/s
```

> **Answer (a):** $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$, with cutoff $\omega_c = 1000$ rad/s, and roll-off of −20 dB/decade beyond $\omega_c$.

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Note that the input** $x(t) = 2\cos(1000t) + \cos(5000t)$ consists of two frequencies:
- Component 1: $\omega_1 = 1000$ rad/s (at the cutoff)
- Component 2: $\omega_2 = 5000$ rad/s (well beyond cutoff)

**Step 2 — Compute** $H(j\omega_1) = H(j1000)$:

$$\omega_1 RC = 1000 \times 10^{-3} = 1$$

$$H(j1000) = \frac{1}{1 + j1} = \frac{1 - j}{2} = 0.5 - j0.5$$

$$\mid H(j1000)\mid = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071, \quad \angle H(j1000) = -45°$$

**Step 3 — Compute** $H(j\omega_2) = H(j5000)$:

$$\omega_2 RC = 5000 \times 10^{-3} = 5$$

$$H(j5000) = \frac{1}{1 + j5}$$

$$\mid H(j5000)\mid = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 25}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{26}} \approx 0.1961$$

$$\angle H(j5000) = -\tan^{-1}(5) \approx -78.69°$$

**Step 4 — Compute steady-state output** by superposition:

For Component 1 ($2\cos(1000t)$):
$$y_1(t) = 2 \times 0.7071 \times \cos(1000t - 45°) = 1.4142\cos(1000t - 45°)$$

For Component 2 ($\cos(5000t)$):
$$y_2(t) = 1 \times 0.1961 \times \cos(5000t - 78.69°) = 0.1961\cos(5000t - 78.69°)$$

**Step 5 — Total output:**

$$y(t) = 1.4142\cos(1000t - 45°) + 0.1961\cos(5000t - 78.69°)$$

> **Comment on filtering action:** The 1000 rad/s component (at cutoff) is attenuated to **70.7%** with a **−45°** phase shift. The 5000 rad/s component (well above cutoff) is heavily attenuated to only **19.6%** with **−78.69°** phase shift. The circuit **passes low frequencies and attenuates high frequencies**, confirming its low-pass filter behavior. The high-frequency noise/tone is suppressed by approximately **14 dB** relative to the low-frequency signal.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Concept of Frequency Response**

- ✅ **Definition:** $H(j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{h(t)\}$ = Fourier transform of impulse response.
- ✅ **Computation:** Replace $s$ with $j\omega$ in $H(s)$: $H(j\omega) = H(s)\big\vert_{s=j\omega}$.
- ✅ **Eigenfunction property:** Sinusoidal input $\Rightarrow$ sinusoidal output of *same frequency*, scaled and phase-shifted.
- ✅ **Output formula:** $y(t) = A \cdot \mid H(j\omega_0)\mid \cdot \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi + \angle H(j\omega_0))$.
- ✅ **Magnitude in dB:** $\mid H\mid_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(\mid H\mid)$.
- ✅ **Phase:** Use $\tan^{-1}(\text{Im}/\text{Re})$ — remember quadrant correction.
- ✅ **Conjugate symmetry:** $H(-j\omega) = H^*(j\omega)$ for real impulse responses.
- ✅ **Convolution theorem:** $Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega) \cdot X(j\omega)$.
- ✅ **RC low-pass:** $H(j\omega) = 1/(1 + j\omega RC)$, cutoff $\omega_c = 1/RC$, roll-off −20 dB/decade.
- ✅ **RC high-pass:** $H(j\omega) = j\omega RC/(1 + j\omega RC)$, same cutoff.
- ✅ **RLC band-pass:** $H(j\omega) = sRC/(s^2LC + sRC + 1)$, resonance at $\omega_r = 1/\sqrt{LC}$.
- ✅ **Quality factor:** $Q = \omega_r/BW = (1/R)\sqrt{L/C}$ — higher $Q$ ⇒ sharper peak.
- ✅ **Group delay:** $\tau_g = -d\angle H(j\omega)/d\omega$ — measures signal distortion.
- ✅ **Bode plot:** Semi-log plot, dB vs $\log_{10}\omega$, asymptotic straight lines.
- ✅ **Polar plot:** $\text{Im}\{H(j\omega)\}$ vs $\text{Re}\{H(j\omega)\}$ as $\omega$ varies.
- ✅ **Stability requirement:** All poles of $H(s)$ must lie in the left-half plane (LHP) for the frequency response $H(j\omega)$ to exist and be bounded.
- ✅ **Power relation:** $\mid H(j\omega)\mid^2$ = power gain of the system.
- ✅ **−3 dB point:** Frequency at which magnitude drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ of its passband value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
