\quad T = \frac{G_1 G_2}{1 + G_2 H_1 + G_1 G_2 H_2}$$ **Step 4 — Cross-check with manual block reduction.** Reduce inner loop first: $$G_{1,\text{eff}} = \frac{G_1}{1 + G_2 H_1}$$ Cascade with $G_2$ then apply outer feedback: $$T = \frac{G_{1,\text{eff}} G_2}{1 + G_{1,\text{eff}} G_2 H_2} = \frac{\frac{G_1 G_2}{1 + G_2 H_1}}{1 + \frac{G_1 G_2 H_2}{1 + G_2 H_1}}$$ $$\begin{aligned} T &= \frac{G_1 G_2}{1 + G_2 H_1 + G_1 G_2 H_2} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned}$$ The two methods agree perfectly, confirming Mason's formula correctness. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Universal Block Diagram — Single-Loop Negative Feedback ```mermaid flowchart LR R([R s]) -->|input| SP1(("+")) SP1 -->|error E s| G1["G s"] G1 -->|signal| SP2(("+")) SP2 --> G2["G s"] G2 -->|output| C([C s]) G2 -->|feedback path| H1["H s"] H1 -->|feedback signal| SP1 classDef node fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef junction fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px class SP1,SP2 junction class R,C node ``` **Interpretation:** The summing junction subtracts $B(s) = H(s) C(s)$ from $R(s)$, producing the actuating error $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$. This drives the forward path $G(s)$ to produce $C(s)$. --- ## 4.2 Canonical Multi-Loop Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB R([R s]) --> SP1(("+")) SP1 --> G1A["G1 s"] G1A --> nodeA((x)) nodeA --> G2A["G2 s"] nodeA --> G5["G5 s"] G5 --> SP1 G2A --> SP2(("+")) SP2 --> G3A["G3 s"] G3A --> C([C s]) G2A --> G4A["G4 s"] G4A --> nodeA G3A --> H2A["H2 s"] H2A --> SP2 classDef node fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef junction fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef tap fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px class R,C node class SP1,SP2 junction class nodeA tap ``` **Interpretation:** This is the topology analyzed in Section 3.2. It contains **three** feedback loops: $G_2 G_4$ (inner), $G_3 H_2$ (middle), $G_2 G_5$ (outer). Note that all loops touch each other at the central node, yielding a clean $\Delta$ with no $L_i L_j$ terms. --- ## 4.3 Signal Flow Graph Representation ```mermaid flowchart LR R([R s]) -- "1" --> E((E)) E -- "G1" --> X((X)) X -- "1" --> SPnode((Sum)) SPnode -- "G2" --> C([C s]) X -- "G4" --> SPnode SPnode -- "H2" --> E classDef input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef internal fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px class R,C input class E,X,SPnode internal ``` **Interpretation:** Every node is a labeled variable, every directed edge carries one transmittance. The two feedback edges $G_4$ (feedforward inner) and $H_2$ (outer) form the loops used in Mason's determinant. --- ## 4.4 System Classification Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB A[LTI System] --> B{Number of poles
at origin N} B -->|N = 0| C[Type 0 System
Constant steady-state error
to step input] B -->|N = 1| D[Type 1 System
Zero steady-state error
to step input] B -->|N = 2| E[Type 2 System
Zero steady-state error
to ramp input] B -->|N >= 3| F[Type N System
Rare in practice
Stability issues] A --> G{Degree of characteristic
polynomial n} G -->|n = 1| H[First-Order
Single energy storage] G -->|n = 2| I[Second-Order
Mass-spring-damper
classic dynamics] G -->|n >= 3| J[Higher-Order
Multiple storage elements] classDef typed fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef ordered fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px class C,D,E,F typed class H,I,J ordered ``` **Interpretation:** The classification is dual — **Type** governs steady-state tracking, **Order** governs transient dynamics. They are independent design parameters. --- ## 4.5 Block Diagram Reduction Algorithm — Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start: Complex Block Diagram]) --> Q1{Isolated
feedback loop?} Q1 -->|Yes| R1[Apply feedback rule
G over 1 plus GH] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Cascade of
two or more blocks?} Q2 -->|Yes| R2[Multiply transfer
functions together] Q2 -->|No| Q3{Parallel
branches?} Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Add or subtract
transfer functions] Q3 -->|No| Q4{Stuck or
getting messy?} Q4 -->|Yes| SFG[Convert to Signal
Flow Graph] Q4 -->|No| R4[Move take-off or
summing points] SFG --> Mason[Apply Mason Gain
Formula directly] R1 --> Done([Single Block
Transfer Function]) R2 --> Done R3 --> Done R4 --> Q1 Mason --> Done classDef decision fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef process fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef endpoint fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4 decision class R1,R2,R3,R4,Mason process class Start,Done endpoint ``` **Interpretation:** This is the canonical problem-solving strategy recommended by the KTU 2024 examiner pattern. Always prefer algebraic reduction; switch to Mason only when topology becomes tangled. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Define the terms (i) Forward Path, (ii) Loop, (iii) Non-touching Loop with reference to a Signal Flow Graph.** **Model Answer:** > **[i] Forward Path:** A path from the input node (source) to the output node (sink) that **does not cross any node more than once**. The product of branch transmittances along this path is the **forward path gain** $P_k$. > > **[ii] Loop:** A closed path that starts and ends at the **same node**, traversing any other node at most once. The product of branch transmittances around the loop is the **loop gain** $L_k$. > > **[iii] Non-touching Loops:** Two (or more) loops that **do not share any common node** between them. Their combined effect enters Mason's determinant through the product term $L_i L_j$ with a **positive sign**. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **What is meant by Type and Order of a control system? How are they determined from the open-loop transfer function?** **Model Answer:** > **Type** of a system is the number of **pure integrations** (poles at the origin) in the open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s)$. It is determined by the exponent $N$ of $s$ in the denominator when $G(s)H(s)$ is expressed in **time-constant form**. Type governs **steady-state accuracy** to standard inputs. > > **Order** of a system is the degree of the **characteristic polynomial** $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$, i.e., the total number of **closed-loop poles**. It is determined by the highest power of $s$ in the characteristic equation after clearing fractions. Order governs the **transient response dynamics** and stability margins. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 — 14 Marks]` **(a) [7 Marks]** For the system whose signal flow graph is given below, find the overall transfer function $C(s)/R(s)$ using **Mason's Gain Formula**. **SFG Description:** Input $R(s)$ connects through transmittance $1$ to node $E$. From $E$, branches of gain $G_1$ and $G_2$ go to node $X$. From $X$, branch of gain $G_3$ goes to output $C(s)$. A feedback branch of gain $H_1$ goes from $X$ back to $E$. Another feedback branch of gain $H_2$ goes from $C(s)$ back to $E$. **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify forward paths.** Only one path from $R$ to $C$: $$P_1 = G_1 G_3$$ (Through node $E \to X \to C$.) **Step 2 — Identify individual loops.** - Loop $L_1$: $E \to X \to E$ via $G_1$ forward and $H_1$ feedback → $L_1 = -G_1 H_1$ - Loop $L_2$: $E \to X \to C \to E$ via $G_1 G_3$ forward and $H_2$ feedback → $L_2 = -G_1 G_3 H_2$ - Loop $L_3$: $E \to X \to C \to E$ via $G_2 G_3$ forward and $H_2$ feedback → $L_3 = -G_2 G_3 H_2$ $$\sum L_i = -(G_1 H_1 + G_1 G_3 H_2 + G_2 G_3 H_2)$$ **Step 3 — Check for non-touching loop pairs.** All three loops share node $E$ and node $X$; therefore **every pair touches**. $$\sum L_i L_j = 0$$ **Step 4 — Compute the graph determinant.** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta &= 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \cdots \\ &= 1 + G_1 H_1 + G_1 G_3 H_2 + G_2 G_3 H_2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the path cofactor.** Path $P_1$ touches every loop, so removing all loops: $$\Delta_1 = 1$$ **Step 6 — Apply Mason's formula.** $$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_1 G_3}{1 + G_1 H_1 + G_1 G_3 H_2 + G_2 G_3 H_2}}$$ --- **(b) [7 Marks]** For the transfer function $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{20(s+1)}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$ in a unity feedback system, determine: **(i)** The type and order of the system. **(ii)** The static error constants $K_p$, $K_v$, $K_a$. **(iii)** The steady-state errors for unit step, unit ramp, and unit parabolic inputs. **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Express in standard form.** $$G(s)H(s) = \frac{20(s+1)}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$$ The denominator has **one** factor of $s$ at the origin. **Step 2 — Determine Type and Order.** $$\boxed{\text{Type } N = 1 \quad ; \quad \text{Order } n = 3}$$ (Order = total poles in closed-loop characteristic polynomial $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$, which expands to a cubic.) **Step 3 — Static error constants.** $$\begin{aligned} K_p &= \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{20(s+1)}{s(s+2)(s+5)} = \infty \\ K_v &= \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s)H(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{20(s+1)}{(s+2)(s+5)} = \frac{20 \cdot 1}{2 \cdot 5} = 2 \\ K_a &= \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot G(s)H(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{20 s(s+1)}{(s+2)(s+5)} = 0 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Steady-state errors.** For unit step input: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p} = \dfrac{1}{\infty} = 0$ For unit ramp input: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v} = \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5$ For unit parabolic input: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_a} = \dfrac{1}{0} = \infty$ --- ### Question B: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 — 14 Marks]` (Internal Choice Alternative) **(a) [7 Marks]** Reduce the following block diagram to obtain the closed-loop transfer function $C(s)/R(s)$: **Topology:** Input $R(s) \to$ Summing junction $\to$ Branch into two parallel paths: - Upper path: Block $G_1(s) \to$ another summing junction $\to$ Block $G_2(s) \to$ output $C(s)$. - Lower path: Block $G_3(s) \to$ feedback into the **second** summing junction (negative sign). - Feedback: From output $C(s)$ through $H(s)$ back to the **first** summing junction (negative sign). **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Resolve the inner summing junction first.** The second summer has inputs: $(+G_1 X)$ and $(-G_3 X)$, output $G_2 C_{\text{pre}}$. $$G_2 \text{ block input} = G_1(s) X(s) - G_3(s) X(s) = [G_1(s) - G_3(s)] X(s)$$ **Step 2 — Cascade with $G_2$.** $$C(s) = G_2(s) [G_1(s) - G_3(s)] X(s)$$ Define $G_{\text{eq}}(s) = G_2(s)[G_1(s) - G_3(s)]$. **Step 3 — Apply outer feedback reduction.** $$X(s) = R(s) - H(s) C(s)$$ $$\begin{aligned} C(s) &= G_{\text{eq}}(s) [R(s) - H(s) C(s)] \\ C(s) [1 + G_{\text{eq}}(s) H(s)] &= G_{\text{eq}}(s) R(s) \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_2(s)[G_1(s) - G_3(s)]}{1 + G_2(s) H(s)[G_1(s) - G_3(s)]}}$$ **Step 4 — Cross-verification with Mason's formula.** For the equivalent SFG, identify: - One forward path: $P_1 = G_1 G_2$ - One inner feedback (via second summer): equivalent branch $-G_3$ merged with $G_1$. - One outer feedback loop: $L_1 = -G_2 H (G_1 - G_3)$ $$\Delta = 1 - L_1 = 1 + G_2 H (G_1 - G_3) \quad \checkmark$$ Mason's yields the same result, confirming the reduction. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A unity feedback system has the open-loop transfer function: $$G(s) = \frac{K(s+3)}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$$ **(i)** Find the range of $K$ for which the system is stable using the **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** (state the characteristic equation only and the stability condition). **(ii)** Determine the **Type** and **Order**. **(iii)** Compute $K_p$, $K_v$, $K_a$ in terms of $K$. **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Characteristic equation.** $$1 + G(s) = 0 \implies s(s+1)(s+2) + K(s+3) = 0$$ Expand $s(s+1)(s+2) = s(s^2 + 3s + 2) = s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s$. $$s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s + Ks + 3K = 0$$ $$s^3 + 3s^2 + (2 + K)s + 3K = 0$$ **Step 2 — Apply Routh-Hurwitz.** $$\begin{array}{c|cc} s^3 & 1 & (2 + K) \\ s^2 & 3 & 3K \\ s^1 & \dfrac{3(2+K) - 3K}{3} = \dfrac{6}{3} = 2 & 0 \\ s^0 & 3K & \\ \end{array}$$ For stability, all first-column entries must be positive: $1 > 0$, $3 > 0$, $2 > 0$, $3K > 0$. $$\boxed{K > 0}$$ **Step 3 — Type and Order.** The denominator of $G(s)$ has $s^1$ at the origin and two other real poles → **Type = 1**, characteristic polynomial degree 3 → **Order = 3**. **Step 4 — Static error constants.** $$\begin{aligned} K_p &= \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K(s+3)}{s(s+1)(s+2)} = \infty \\ K_v &= \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s) = \frac{K \cdot 3}{1 \cdot 2} = \frac{3K}{2} \\ K_a &= \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot G(s) = 0 \end{aligned}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign Error in Mason's Formula:** When the loop gain is written as $-G_2 H_1$ and substituted into $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i$, the resulting denominator becomes $1 + G_2 H_1$ (positive sign). Students often write $-1 - G_2 H_1$, **losing 2 marks**. > 2. **Confusing Type with Order:** Writing "Type 1, Type 1" or "Order = 1, Order = 3" interchangeably. **Deduct 1 mark.** > 3. **Forgetting the $1+$ in the characteristic equation** when computing Order. The degree of $G(s)$ alone is NOT the order; the degree of $1 + G(s)H(s)$ is. > 4. **Missing non-touching loop product terms:** In Mason's formula, students often stop at $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i$ and forget $+ \sum L_i L_j$. **Deduct 3 marks.** > 5. **Sign of static error constant for Type-1 to ramp:** Writing $e_{ss} = 1 + K_v$ instead of $1/K_v$. **Deduct 1 mark.** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4** - **Block Diagram Reduction:** Always look for the **innermost feedback loop** first; reduce it; move outward. Use the **canonical form** rules (cascade, parallel, feedback) before attempting re-positioning of take-off or summing points. - **Negative Feedback Closed-Loop Gain:** Memorize $\dfrac{G}{1 + GH}$ — appears in **every** KTU paper. - **Mason's Gain Formula:** Memorize the **five-step protocol**: 1. Identify **all forward paths** $P_k$. 2. Identify **all individual loops** $L_i$ with proper **signs**. 3. Identify **all pairs** of non-touching loops and form $L_i L_j$. 4. Compute $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \cdots$ 5. Compute $\Delta_k$ by deleting all loops touching $P_k$, then apply $T = \dfrac{\sum P_k \Delta_k}{\Delta}$. - **Non-Touching Loop Detection:** Two loops are non-touching if and only if **no node and no branch** is shared between them. Draw the SFG clearly; mark loops in different colors. - **Type Definition:** Type is the **exponent of $s$** in the denominator of $G(s)$ when expressed in **Bode (time-constant) form**. It is **NOT** the number of integrators in the closed-loop transfer function. - **Order Definition:** Order is the **degree of the characteristic polynomial** $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$. Equals the number of closed-loop poles. - **Steady-State Error Constants:** - **Type 0** → finite $K_p$, zero $K_v$ and $K_a$. - **Type 1** → infinite $K_p$, finite $K_v$, zero $K_a$. - **Type 2** → infinite $K_p$ and $K_v$, finite $K_a$. - **Engineering Trade-off:** Increasing **Type** improves steady-state accuracy but **degrades stability margin** (Bode's theorem). Increasing **Order** complicates transient analysis but enables more sophisticated compensation. - **KTU Typical Marks Distribution:** Block Diagram Reduction = 7 marks, Mason's Gain Formula = 7 marks, Type/Order/Error Constants = 7 marks — usually one full 14-mark question OR two 7-mark sub-parts. - **Quick Conversion Aid:** Block diagram ↔ SFG: every block → branch transmittance; every summing junction → node with incoming branches; take-off point → node with multiple outgoing branches. - **Formula Card Priority:** $\dfrac{G}{1+GH}$, $\dfrac{G}{1-GH}$, Mason's $T = \dfrac{\sum P_k \Delta_k}{\Delta}$, $e_{ss}$ table — these four items alone cover 80% of KTU questions on this module.

topics.title: Pole-Zero Representation of Systems, Characteristic Equation, Routh Stability Criterion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET503 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pole-Zero Representation of Systems, Characteristic Equation, Routh Stability Criterion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pole-Zero Representation** is a graphical and algebraic technique used to characterize Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) systems described by a rational transfer function. For an LTI system, the transfer function $H(s)$ is expressed as the ratio of two polynomials:

$$H(s) = \frac{N(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{b_m s^m + b_{m-1} s^{m-1} + \cdots + b_1 s + b_0}{a_n s^n + a_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_1 s + a_0}$$

Where:
- **Zeros** are the roots of the numerator polynomial $N(s) = 0$. At zero frequencies, the system output is forced to zero.
- **Poles** are the roots of the denominator polynomial $D(s) = 0$. At pole frequencies, the system response becomes unbounded (infinite magnitude).
- The **Characteristic Equation** is defined as $D(s) = 0$. Its roots dictate the natural (intrinsic) behavior of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A system with $n$ poles and $m$ zeros is referred to as an *n-th order system with m finite zeros*. Poles and zeros are plotted on the complex s-plane, where the horizontal axis represents the real part ($\sigma$) and the vertical axis represents the imaginary part ($j\omega$).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Terrain Map" Concept

Imagine the s-plane as a vast **mountainous terrain**:
- **Poles** are like *bottomless pits* or *volcanic craters* — at these exact locations, the system's magnitude shoots to infinity. Just as a ball rolling on a map would fall into a pit, the signal energy gets "trapped" and resonates uncontrollably at pole locations.
- **Zeros** are like *smooth plateaus* — at these locations, the output vanishes completely (flat ground where motion stops).
- The **Left Half Plane (LHP)** is *stable solid bedrock* — poles sitting here cause decaying, well-behaved responses. The **Right Half Plane (RHP)** is *shifting sand* — poles here cause exponential growth, meaning the system blows up.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Insight:** For a causal, LTI system to be **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable**, ALL poles of $H(s)$ must lie strictly in the LEFT HALF PLANE (LHP). Any pole on the imaginary axis or in the RHP leads to instability.

### 1.3 Why Poles and Zeros Matter

| Aspect | Poles | Zeros |
|--------|-------|-------|
| **Mathematical Origin** | Roots of $D(s)$ | Roots of $N(s)$ |
| **Physical Effect** | Resonance, instability | Notch filtering, signal cancellation |
| **Stability Impact** | Determines stability | Does not affect stability directly |
| **Time Response** | Natural modes: $e^{p_i t}$ | Shape of transient response |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Pole-zero plots are the design cornerstone of **analog and digital filters**. Engineers place poles where they want amplification and zeros where they want rejection — this is the foundation of Butterworth, Chebyshev, and Bessel filter designs used in audio processing, communication receivers, and biomedical instrumentation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Obtaining the Transfer Function**
For an LTI system governed by a linear constant-coefficient differential equation:
$$\sum_{k=0}^{n} a_k \frac{d^k y(t)}{dt^k} = \sum_{k=0}^{m} b_k \frac{d^k x(t)}{dt^k}$$
Taking the Laplace transform (assuming zero initial conditions) yields the rational transfer function $H(s) = Y(s)/X(s)$.

**Step 2: Factoring the Polynomials**
By the **Fundamental Theorem of Algebra**, any polynomial of degree $n$ with real coefficients has $n$ roots (counting multiplicities) in the complex plane. We factor:
$$H(s) = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{m}(s - z_i)}{\prod_{j=1}^{n}(s - p_j)}$$
Where $K$ is the gain constant, $z_i$ are zeros, and $p_j$ are poles.

**Step 3: Locating the Poles**
Setting $D(s) = 0$ gives the **Characteristic Equation**. The roots $p_j$ determine:
- Natural unforced response: $y_n(t) = \sum c_j e^{p_j t}$
- Stability: $\text{Re}(p_j) < 0 \;\; \forall j \Rightarrow$ Stable
- Oscillation: If $p_j = \sigma_j \pm j\omega_j$, oscillation frequency is $\omega_j$.

**Step 4: Stability via Routh-Hurwitz Criterion**
Computing pole locations requires solving high-order polynomials (cubic, quartic, etc.) — algebraically tedious. **Edward John Routh (1877)** developed a tabular method to determine the NUMBER of RHP poles WITHOUT explicitly solving for them.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula / Expression** | **Condition for Stability** |
|--------------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| Transfer Function | $H(s) = K \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{m}(s - z_i) \;/\; \prod_{j=1}^{n}(s - p_j)$ | All $p_j$ in LHP |
| Characteristic Equation | $D(s) = a_n s^n + a_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_1 s + a_0 = 0$ | All roots have negative real parts |
| Routh Row 1 (top) | $a_n, \; a_{n-2}, \; a_{n-4}, \ldots$ | — |
| Routh Row 2 | $a_{n-1}, \; a_{n-3}, \; a_{n-5}, \ldots$ | — |
| Generic Routh Element | $b_{ij} = \dfrac{b_{i-1,0} \cdot b_{i-2,j+1} - b_{i-2,0} \cdot b_{i-1,j+1}}{b_{i-1,0}}$ | — |
| BIBO Stability | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$ | ROC includes $j\omega$-axis |
| Marginal Stability | All poles on $j\omega$-axis (simple, non-repeated) | Bounded but not asymptotically stable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hurwitz Necessary Condition (for all-positive coefficients):** A necessary (but not sufficient) condition for stability is that ALL coefficients of the characteristic polynomial must be real, non-zero, and have the SAME sign (typically positive).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Control Systems:** Routh criterion tells a control engineer whether a feedback amplifier will oscillate or settle.
- **Aerospace:** Stability of autopilot systems — RHP poles cause aircraft divergence.
- **Power Systems:** Stability of grid-connected inverters using Routh-based compensator design.
- **Biomedical:** Designing pacemakers and neural stimulators where instability = patient harm.
- **Audio Engineering:** Loudspeaker feedback elimination by zero placement.

### 2.4 Special Cases in Routh Array

| **Special Case** | **Symptom** | **Resolution Technique** |
|------------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| Zero in first column (isolated) | Element $= 0$, next row $\neq 0$ | Substitute small positive $\epsilon$, then take limit $\epsilon \to 0^+$ |
| Entire row of zeros | Auxiliary polynomial row appears | Form auxiliary polynomial $A(s)$ from row above, differentiate to get new row |
| Row of zeros at top | Indicates symmetry | Apply auxiliary polynomial method |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Routh Array Element Formula

Consider the characteristic polynomial:
$$D(s) = a_n s^n + a_{n-1} s^{n-1} + a_{n-2} s^{n-2} + a_{n-3} s^{n-3} + \cdots$$

The Routh array is built as follows. The first two rows are populated with coefficients in a specific pattern:

$$\begin{aligned}
s^n &: \quad a_n \quad a_{n-2} \quad a_{n-4} \quad \cdots \\
s^{n-1} &: \quad a_{n-1} \quad a_{n-3} \quad a_{n-5} \quad \cdots \\
s^{n-2} &: \quad b_1 \quad b_2 \quad b_3 \quad \cdots \\
s^{n-3} &: \quad c_1 \quad c_2 \quad c_3 \quad \cdots \\
\vdots &: \quad \vdots
\end{aligned}$$

**Computing $b_1$** (first element of $s^{n-2}$ row):

$$b_1 = \frac{(a_{n-1})(a_{n-2}) - (a_n)(a_{n-3})}{a_{n-1}} = -\frac{1}{a_{n-1}} \begin{vmatrix} a_n & a_{n-3} \\ a_{n-1} & a_{n-2} \end{vmatrix}$$

**Computing $b_2$:**

$$b_2 = \frac{(a_{n-1})(a_{n-4}) - (a_n)(a_{n-5})}{a_{n-1}}$$

**Computing $c_1$** (first element of $s^{n-3}$ row):

$$c_1 = \frac{(b_1)(a_{n-3}) - (a_{n-1})(b_2)}{b_1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always express Routh elements as **determinants** divided by the element directly above. This shows the structural pattern and earns full method marks even if arithmetic errors occur.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Complete Stability Analysis

**Problem:** Determine the stability of the system whose characteristic equation is:
$$D(s) = s^4 + 2s^3 + 3s^2 + 4s + 5 = 0$$

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Identify coefficients. Here $a_4 = 1, \; a_3 = 2, \; a_2 = 3, \; a_1 = 4, \; a_0 = 5$. All coefficients are positive — necessary condition satisfied.

**Step 2:** Build the Routh Array.

$$\begin{aligned}
s^4 &: \quad 1 \quad 3 \quad 5 \\
s^3 &: \quad 2 \quad 4 \quad 0 \\
s^2 &: \quad b_1 \quad b_2 \\
s^1 &: \quad c_1 \quad 0 \\
s^0 &: \quad 5
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $b_1$:

$$b_1 = \frac{(2)(3) - (1)(4)}{2} = \frac{6 - 4}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1$$

**Step 4:** Compute $b_2$:

$$b_2 = \frac{(2)(5) - (1)(0)}{2} = \frac{10 - 0}{2} = 5$$

**Step 5:** Compute $c_1$:

$$c_1 = \frac{(1)(4) - (2)(5)}{1} = \frac{4 - 10}{1} = -6$$

**Step 6:** The completed Routh Array:

$$\begin{aligned}
s^4 &: \quad 1 \quad \;\, 3 \quad \;\, 5 \\
s^3 &: \quad 2 \quad \;\, 4 \quad \;\, 0 \\
s^2 &: \quad 1 \quad \;\, 5 \\
s^1 &: \quad -6 \quad \;\, 0 \\
s^0 &: \quad 5
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7:** Examine the first column: $1, \; 2, \; 1, \; -6, \; 5$.

Counting sign changes:
- $1 \to 2$: no change (positive to positive)
- $2 \to 1$: no change
- $1 \to -6$: **1 sign change** (positive to negative)
- $-6 \to 5$: **2nd sign change** (negative to positive)

**Step 8: Conclusion** — There are **2 poles in the Right Half Plane (RHP)**. The system is **UNSTABLE**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Routh Array Building Rules

| **Row Power** | **Column 1 Element** | **Method to Compute** |
|---------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| $s^n$ | $a_n$ | First coefficient (highest power) |
| $s^{n-1}$ | $a_{n-1}$ | Second coefficient |
| $s^{n-2}$ | $b_1$ | Determinant $\dfrac{a_{n-1} \cdot a_{n-2} - a_n \cdot a_{n-3}}{a_{n-1}}$ |
| $s^{n-3}$ | $c_1$ | Determinant $\dfrac{b_1 \cdot a_{n-3} - a_{n-1} \cdot b_2}{b_1}$ |
| $s^{n-k}$ | — | Pattern continues recursively |
| $s^0$ | $a_0$ | Constant term (if no zero rows) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Verdict Cheat Sheet:**
> - **Stable:** All first-column elements have the SAME SIGN (no sign changes).
> - **Marginally Stable:** Sign changes only when zero rows occur (poles on $j\omega$-axis).
> - **Unstable:** Sign changes present in first column → number of RHP poles equals sign changes.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Special Case (Row of Zeros)

**Problem:** Check stability of:
$$D(s) = s^5 + 2s^4 + 24s^3 + 48s^2 - 25s - 50 = 0$$

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
s^5 &: \quad 1 \quad \;\, 24 \quad \;\, -25 \\
s^4 &: \quad 2 \quad \;\, 48 \quad \;\, -50 \\
s^3 &: \quad 0 \quad \;\, 0
\end{aligned}$$

We have a **row of zeros** at $s^3$. Apply the **Auxiliary Polynomial** method:
- Form auxiliary polynomial from row above ($s^4$ row): $A(s) = 2s^4 + 48s^2 - 50$
- Differentiate: $\dfrac{dA}{ds} = 8s^3 + 96s$
- Substitute back into $s^3$ row: $8, \; 96$

Continuing:

$$\begin{aligned}
s^3 &: \quad 8 \quad \;\, 96 \\
s^2 &: \quad \dfrac{(8)(48) - (2)(96)}{8} = \dfrac{384 - 192}{8} = 24 \quad \;\, \dfrac{(8)(-50) - (2)(0)}{8} = -50 \\
s^1 &: \quad \dfrac{(24)(96) - (8)(-50)}{24} = \dfrac{2304 + 400}{24} = \dfrac{2704}{24} \approx 112.67 \\
s^0 &: \quad -50
\end{aligned}$$

First column: $1, \; 2, \; 8, \; 24, \; 112.67, \; -50$. Sign changes: at $-50$ → **1 sign change**. System is **UNSTABLE** with 1 RHP pole.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System Analysis Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LTI System H of s] --> B[Derive Transfer Function]
    B --> C[Extract Characteristic Equation D of s = 0]
    C --> D{Polynomial Degree}
    D -->|Degree 1 or 2| E[Direct Root Computation]
    D -->|Degree 3 or Higher| F[Routh Hurwitz Tabulation]
    E --> G[Identify Pole Locations in s Plane]
    F --> H[Inspect First Column of Routh Array]
    H --> I{Sign Changes Detected?}
    I -->|No Sign Changes| J[STABLE: All Poles in LHP]
    I -->|Sign Changes Present| K[UNSTABLE: Poles in RHP equal to sign changes]
    G --> L[Plot Poles and Zeros on s Plane]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Final Stability Verdict]
```

### 4.2 Routh Array Construction Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Input Polynomial D of s] --> R1[Row s power n: Write Coefficients a_n, a_n-2, a_n-4]
    R1 --> R2[Row s power n-1: Write Coefficients a_n-1, a_n-3, a_n-5]
    R2 --> Check{Row of Zeros Encountered?}
    Check -->|No| Compute[Compute Next Row via Determinant Formula]
    Compute --> NextRow[Row s power n-k: b_i Formula]
    NextRow --> More{More Rows Needed?}
    More -->|Yes| Check
    More -->|No| Final[Extract First Column]
    Check -->|Yes| AuxPoly[Form Auxiliary Polynomial A of s from Previous Row]
    AuxPoly --> Differentiate[Differentiate A of s with respect to s]
    Differentiate --> Replace[Replace Zero Row with Coefficients of dA by ds]
    Replace --> Compute
    Final --> CountSign[Count Sign Changes in First Column]
    CountSign --> Result[Number of RHP Poles equals Sign Changes]
```

### 4.3 Pole-Zero Plot Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP["S PLANE"]
        direction TB
        LHP["LEFT HALF PLANE\nStable Region\nPoles here = Decaying Response"]
        IMG["IMAGINARY AXIS j omega\nMarginally Stable Region\nPoles here = Sustained Oscillation"]
        RHP["RIGHT HALF PLANE\nUnstable Region\nPoles here = Growing Response"]
        Z["ZERO Location\nMarked with Circle O\nForces Output to Zero"]
        P["POLE Location\nMarked with Cross X\nCauses Resonance and Instability"]
    end
```

### 4.4 Stability Decision Flow Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Obtain Characteristic Equation D of s] --> B[Check Necessary Condition:\nAll Coefficients Positive and Non-Zero]
    B --> C{Coefficients OK?}
    C -->|No| D[System is UNSTABLE\nNo Further Analysis Needed]
    C -->|Yes| E[Construct Routh Array]
    E --> F{Zero in First Column?}
    F -->|Isolated Zero| G[Substitute epsilon and Take Limit]
    F -->|Entire Row Zero| H[Use Auxiliary Polynomial Method]
    F -->|No Issue| I[Inspect First Column]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Sign Changes in First Column?}
    J -->|Zero Sign Changes| K[STABLE System]
    J -->|Non-Zero Sign Changes| L[UNSTABLE: Number of RHP Poles equals Sign Changes]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

Define the following terms with respect to the pole-zero representation of an LTI system:
(i) Pole, (ii) Zero, (iii) Characteristic equation.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Pole:** A pole of a transfer function $H(s) = N(s)/D(s)$ is a value of $s$ at which the magnitude of $H(s)$ becomes infinite. Mathematically, poles are the roots of the denominator polynomial $D(s) = 0$. Each pole $p_i$ corresponds to a natural mode $e^{p_i t}$ in the system's time-domain response.

**(ii) Zero:** A zero is a value of $s$ at which the magnitude of $H(s)$ becomes zero. Zeros are the roots of the numerator polynomial $N(s) = 0$. Zeros affect the shape of the frequency response and transient behavior but do not directly impact stability.

**(iii) Characteristic Equation:** The characteristic equation of an LTI system is obtained by setting the denominator of the transfer function to zero: $D(s) = 0$. The roots of the characteristic equation are precisely the poles of the system, which determine the natural response and stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** A simple diagram with the s-plane showing poles as 'X' and zeros as 'O' along with the characteristic equation definition earns full 3 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

State the Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion. What conclusion can be drawn if all elements of the first column of the Routh array have the same sign?

**Model Answer:**

**Routh-Hurwitz Stability Criterion:** The Routh-Hurwitz criterion states that *"the necessary and sufficient condition for a linear system to be stable is that all the elements of the first column of the Routh array must have the same sign (either all positive or all negative), AND there must be no sign changes as we move down the first column."*

**Conclusion:** If all elements of the first column of the Routh array have the same sign with no sign changes, then the system is **STABLE**. This implies that ALL the roots of the characteristic equation lie in the Left Half Plane (LHP), i.e., all poles have negative real parts. The number of sign changes in the first column, if any, equals the number of poles lying in the Right Half Plane (RHP).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** The characteristic equation of a feedback control system is:
$$s^4 + 6s^3 + 11s^2 + 6s + K = 0$$
Determine the range of $K$ for which the system is stable using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a system with transfer function:
$$H(s) = \frac{s^2 + 4s + 3}{s^4 + 2s^3 + 10s^2 + 8s + 8}$$
Plot the pole-zero diagram and comment on stability. **(7 Marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**(a) Solution:**

The characteristic equation is:
$$D(s) = s^4 + 6s^3 + 11s^2 + 6s + K = 0$$

Constructing the Routh array:

$$\begin{aligned}
s^4 &: \quad 1 \quad \;\, 11 \quad \;\, K \\
s^3 &: \quad 6 \quad \;\, 6 \quad \;\, 0 \\
s^2 &: \quad \dfrac{(6)(11) - (1)(6)}{6} = \dfrac{66 - 6}{6} = 10 \quad \;\, \dfrac{(6)(K) - (1)(0)}{6} = K \\
s^1 &: \quad \dfrac{(10)(6) - (6)(K)}{10} = \dfrac{60 - 6K}{10} = \dfrac{6(10 - K)}{10} \\
s^0 &: \quad K
\end{aligned}$$

For stability, all elements in the first column must be positive:
- $1 > 0$ ✓ (always satisfied)
- $6 > 0$ ✓ (always satisfied)
- $10 > 0$ ✓ (always satisfied)
- $\dfrac{6(10 - K)}{10} > 0 \Rightarrow 10 - K > 0 \Rightarrow K < 10$
- $K > 0$

Combining both conditions:
$$\boxed{0 < K < 10}$$

The system is stable for $0 < K < 10$.

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Finding Poles:** The poles are the roots of $D(s) = s^4 + 2s^3 + 10s^2 + 8s + 8 = 0$.

Testing factorization:
$$D(s) = (s^2 + 0.585s + 2.508)(s^2 + 1.415s + 3.189)$$

Solving each quadratic using $s = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$:

For $s^2 + 0.585s + 2.508 = 0$:
$$s = \dfrac{-0.585 \pm \sqrt{0.342 - 10.032}}{2} = \dfrac{-0.585 \pm \sqrt{-9.690}}{2}$$
$$s = -0.293 \pm j1.557$$

For $s^2 + 1.415s + 3.189 = 0$:
$$s = \dfrac{-1.415 \pm \sqrt{2.002 - 12.756}}{2} = \dfrac{-1.415 \pm \sqrt{-10.754}}{2}$$
$$s = -0.708 \pm j1.640$$

**Finding Zeros:** $N(s) = s^2 + 4s + 3 = (s+1)(s+3) = 0$
$$\Rightarrow s = -1, \quad s = -3$$

**Pole-Zero Plot:**

| **Location Type** | **Value of $s$** |
|--------------------|------------------|
| Pole 1 | $-0.293 + j1.557$ |
| Pole 2 | $-0.293 - j1.557$ |
| Pole 3 | $-0.708 + j1.640$ |
| Pole 4 | $-0.708 - j1.640$ |
| Zero 1 | $-1$ |
| Zero 2 | $-3$ |

```
        jω axis
          │
    ──────┼──────  σ axis
          │   -3(O)───── -1(O)
          │
   ×(Pole)│ ×(Pole)    ← Conjugate pairs in LHP
          │
```

**Stability Comment:** All four poles have **negative real parts** ($-0.293$ and $-0.708$). Therefore, all poles lie in the **Left Half Plane (LHP)**, and the system is **STABLE**. Both poles appear as conjugate pairs, indicating damped oscillatory natural modes.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Determine the stability of the system whose characteristic equation is given by:
$$2s^4 + s^3 + 3s^2 + 5s + 10 = 0$$
using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. If unstable, find the number of poles in the right half of the s-plane. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the transfer function $H(s) = \dfrac{(s+2)(s+3)}{(s+1)(s^2+2s+5)}$, determine:
(i) Poles and Zeros
(ii) Pole-zero plot description
(iii) Stability verdict **(7 Marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**(a) Solution:**

The characteristic equation: $2s^4 + s^3 + 3s^2 + 5s + 10 = 0$

**Necessary condition check:** All coefficients ($2, 1, 3, 5, 10$) are positive. Necessary condition is satisfied, so we proceed with Routh array.

**Constructing the Routh Array:**

$$\begin{aligned}
s^4 &: \quad 2 \quad \;\, 3 \quad \;\, 10 \\
s^3 &: \quad 1 \quad \;\, 5 \quad \;\, 0 \\
s^2 &: \quad \dfrac{(1)(3) - (2)(5)}{1} = \dfrac{3 - 10}{1} = -7 \quad \;\, \dfrac{(1)(10) - (2)(0)}{1} = 10 \\
s^1 &: \quad \dfrac{(-7)(5) - (1)(10)}{-7} = \dfrac{-35 - 10}{-7} = \dfrac{-45}{-7} = 6.43 \\
s^0 &: \quad 10
\end{aligned}$$

**First Column Inspection:** $2, \; 1, \; -7, \; 6.43, \; 10$

**Counting Sign Changes:**
- $2 \to 1$: no change (positive to positive)
- $1 \to -7$: **1st sign change** (positive to negative)
- $-7 \to 6.43$: **2nd sign change** (negative to positive)
- $6.43 \to 10$: no change

**Total Sign Changes = 2**

**Conclusion:** The system has **2 poles in the Right Half Plane (RHP)** and is therefore **UNSTABLE**.

---

**(b) Solution:**

**(i) Poles and Zeros:**

*Zeros:* $N(s) = (s+2)(s+3) = 0 \Rightarrow s = -2, \; s = -3$

*Poles:* $D(s) = (s+1)(s^2 + 2s + 5) = 0$
- From $s+1 = 0$: $s = -1$
- From $s^2 + 2s + 5 = 0$: $s = \dfrac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4-20}}{2} = \dfrac{-2 \pm \sqrt{-16}}{2} = -1 \pm j2$

**(ii) Pole-Zero Plot Description:**

| **Feature** | **s-coordinate** | **Location** |
|-------------|------------------|--------------|
| Zero 1 | $-2$ | Real axis, LHP |
| Zero 2 | $-3$ | Real axis, LHP |
| Pole 1 | $-1$ | Real axis, LHP (real pole) |
| Pole 2 | $-1 + j2$ | Upper LHP (complex conjugate) |
| Pole 3 | $-1 - j2$ | Lower LHP (complex conjugate) |

The pole-zero plot has two zeros on the negative real axis, one real pole at $s = -1$, and a complex conjugate pole pair at $-1 \pm j2$.

**(iii) Stability Verdict:**

Since ALL poles ($s = -1$ and $s = -1 \pm j2$) have **negative real parts**, they all reside in the **Left Half Plane (LHP)**. Therefore, the system is **STABLE** (asymptotically stable for causal LTI realization).

The damping factor for the complex pair: $\zeta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 4}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 0.447$, indicating an **underdamped** oscillatory response.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Error (–2 to –3 marks):** Students often forget that a "sign change" in the first column means going from positive to negative OR negative to positive. Two consecutive negatives with a positive in between counts as TWO sign changes.
>
> 2. **Auxiliary Polynomial Misapplication (–3 marks):** When you encounter a row of zeros, you MUST form the auxiliary polynomial from the row ABOVE (not below). Differentiation gives $\dfrac{dA(s)}{ds}$, not $A(s)$ itself.
>
> 3. **Missing Necessary Condition Check (–1 mark):** Always verify all coefficients are positive and non-zero BEFORE constructing the Routh array. If a coefficient is missing or negative, the system is immediately unstable.
>
> 4. **Pole Multiplicity on jω-axis (–2 marks):** A simple pole on the imaginary axis (multiplicity = 1) gives marginal stability. A repeated pole on the imaginary axis (multiplicity ≥ 2) means the system is UNSTABLE — students often confuse these.
>
> 5. **Zero-Row in First Column Without Full Row (–2 marks):** If only the FIRST element is zero (not the entire row), substitute a small positive $\epsilon$, complete the array, then take $\lim_{\epsilon \to 0^+}$.
>
> 6. **Confusing Poles with Eigenvalues (–1 mark):** In state-space systems, eigenvalues of matrix A are the poles. Don't mix these up during cross-topic questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Poles** are roots of $D(s)$; **Zeros** are roots of $N(s)$. Poles dictate stability; zeros shape the response.
- **BIBO Stability** requires all poles to be strictly inside the LHP ($\text{Re}(p_j) < 0$).
- **Characteristic Equation** $D(s) = 0$ defines the natural modes of the system.
- **Routh Array** construction starts with the highest-power coefficients in $s^n$ and $s^{n-1}$ rows.
- **Number of sign changes in first column = Number of poles in RHP.**
- **Auxiliary Polynomial** is used when a row of zeros occurs — formed from the row above, then differentiated.
- **Substitute $\epsilon$** technique handles isolated zeros in the first column.
- **Repeated imaginary poles** ⇒ Unstable (pure oscillator grows as $t \cdot \cos(\omega t)$).
- **Necessary (not sufficient) condition:** All coefficients must be present, real, and same sign.
- **KTU 2024 weightage:** This topic typically carries **12–14 marks** in the End Semester Exam (ESE), with 3-mark conceptual questions in internal assessments.
- **Common system orders tested:** $n = 3, 4, 5$ with $K$ as the parameter to be bounded.
